The only way to start is by starting. RMI’s Electrify cohort is helping 12 teams from across the country — representing 20 local governments and their 30 partner organizations — develop and launch programs to help community members electrify their homes, focusing on heat pumps. 

"We tried to provide the teams with a lot of different electrification program pieces that otherwise all of those municipalities would have had to create on their own," said RMI Manager Michael Gartman, who helped run the cohorts.  

"Giving those folks a clear process to follow with tools, templates, and a community to share ideas with and ask questions is what RMI has provided," he added. 

Partnerships and collaboration are a real game-changer. All 12 teams in the cohort agreed on the importance of the relationships that were built during the two years of the program. The teams were able to connect and see what each other was doing, helping them feel less isolated by sharing what they had learned during their program development. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/3ZOMQLi

#electrification #HomeElectrification

💵 More climate finance is coming to Africa!  

Building on its success in securing millions in climate finance for Pacific and Caribbean nations since 2021, the Climate Finance Access Network (CFAN) has officially announced the launch of its first Africa cohort, supported by the U.S. Department of State.
 
Coordinated by RMI, CFAN enhances the capacity to unlock climate finance in Small Island Developing States (SIDS) and Least Developed Countries by recruiting and training local climate finance advisors to work within governments and local organizations. Currently active in 20 countries across the Pacific and Caribbean, CFAN has made significant progress.  
 
CFAN's current impact by the numbers: 
🌏 20 countries and three cohorts 
💰 $76.6 million in climate finance secured 
💸 $1.4 billion in the global project pipeline 

The three African countries where CFAN will be starting its cohort, the Democratic Republic of Congo, the Republic of Congo, and Cameroon, are just the beginning. 🌱 The goal is to scale the success of CFAN throughout the continent while tailoring its approach to country-specific needs and priorities. Notably, these nations are situated in the Congo Basin, the world’s largest carbon sink, which absorbs approximately 4% of annual global emissions, underscoring the region’s critical role in global climate mitigation. 

This expansion marks a pivotal step in equipping vulnerable nations with the tools and expertise needed to access critical climate finance, empowering them to safeguard their communities and contribute to global climate resilience. 🛠️

Heat pumps can do it all: serving as air conditioners on hot summer days and warming our homes on cold winter nights, all while saving energy and improving air quality. This versatility makes them an increasingly popular green choice. 

In heating mode, air-source heat pumps use refrigerants with high boiling points to absorb heat from the air, even when it’s cold out. The heat pump concentrates this warmth and passes it indoors to warm your home.  

And because a heat pump moves heat instead of generating it – as does a conventional furnace – it can deliver several times more heat energy than it consumes in electricity. This requires much less energy and means lower operating costs than older-style electric resistance heaters. And compared with oil and gas furnaces, heat pumps generate far less harmful air pollution and greenhouse gases.     

Heat pumps have been a hit in milder climates for a long time, and advancements are making them more and more popular in colder climes, too. Learn more about how switching to a heat pump can be a big win for household energy savings, lower health risks, and increased comfort — all while improving the climate: https://bit.ly/3Zsmb5A

The aviation industry is responsible for nearly 3% of global CO2 emissions, roughly on par with Japan, the fifth highest-emitting country. And this past Sunday set a record for busiest day ever for commercial air travel in America, only increasing our carbon output. To get these emissions down, the industry is developing new technologies to help decarbonize aviation and create a more sustainable future. 

Sustainable aviation fuels and other novel propulsion technologies, such as hydrogen, electric, and hybrid aircraft are essential to decarbonizing the industry, but to reach net-zero by 2050, airports need to start planning now. 

Airport infrastructure plays an important role in decarbonization of the aviation industry. Airports must plan for and implement new airport infrastructure that allows for these new technologies to thrive. To do so, they’re going to have to work closely with a variety of stakeholders to make it happen. 

Check out RMI’s new article on airports and net-zero aviation 👉 https://bit.ly/49w2w9K

New approaches to financing can speed up the phase out of coal. In New Jersey, two major coal plants ceased operations 30 months ahead of schedule through an innovative "Renegotiate, Refinance, Redevelop" approach. The project demonstrates the power of collaboration to deliver a big win for investors, customers, and the climate. Through renegotiated power agreements, consumers saved $30 million in energy costs while investors secured more attractive terms.   

This approach has also offered expansive benefits for the climate: As the sites shift to battery storage facilities, millions of tons of carbon emissions will be avoided.  

This case in New Jersey demonstrates an approach that can advance similar collaboration in other energy systems worldwide. The three-pronged strategy — renegotiating power contracts, refinancing with institutional capital, and redeveloping sites for clean energy — offers valuable lessons for utilities, investors, and policymakers working to phase out coal while maintaining grid reliability. https://bit.ly/41pUZHz

Rising demand is driving the growth of “fast fashion” — low-cost clothing often worn only once — made from polyester. Polyester is a synthetic fiber, or plastic, derived from petroleum-based chemicals that constantly shed microplastics. Most polyester fibers aren't biodegradable. This creates big challenges to manage polyester waste as well as to cut the emissions from making, using, and disposing of these products. 

With an annual production volume of approximately 73 million tons, polyester now comprises over half of global textile fiber production. By evaluating the polyester value chain, we can understand pathways to a climate-differentiated chemicals markets, and the technology, policy, and market innovation needed to take a step towards sustainable production of polyester products. 

https://bit.ly/3OEbcRz

Each year, the number of flights grows, and in turn the amount of CO2 emitted increases. However, sustainable aviation fuel, or SAF, could be a fix for the aviation industry’s carbon emissions problem.  

“Sustainable aviation fuel” is an umbrella term for liquid fuels that are made without crude oil. SAF can be produced from corn ethanol, agricultural scraps, or synthetic “e-fuels" derived from green hydrogen and recycled CO2. Although not a perfect solution, SAF is essential to decarbonize flying in the near term and can help create a greener aviation industry. 🌱 ✈️ 

“It’s important for consumers to understand that we are charting a flight path, if you will, toward a zero-carbon aviation system,” says RMI’s Mark Porter. “But that’s not going to happen today, and it won’t be tomorrow. It’s going to take a while to get there.” 

Via Canary Media Inc. https://bit.ly/3CJdYCv

Emissions from existing coal power plants will push global warming beyond 1.5°C. Countries need to immediately end the construction of new coal power plants and phase out existing ones completely by 2040. With 85% of coal demand coming from emerging economies, it’s vital to accelerate the coal-to-clean transition across the developing world.  

The phase-out of coal power is well underway in certain regions, but accelerating the coal-to-clean transition across developing economies is the best way to achievethat goal. This is challenging work, and will depend on domestic policy and planning, plus the support of international partners, local stakeholder support, and raising the ambition of both private and public financial commitments. 

"Accelerating Coal-to-Clean Energy Transitions: First Report and Recommendations of the Coal Transition Commission," authored by the International Energy Agency (IEA), The Powering Past Coal Alliance (PPCA), E3G, Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero (GFANZ), and RMI, and supported by Bloomberg Philanthropies, is a roadmap that shows the opportunities, challenges, and financial and policy solutions to speed up the transition away from coal to clean energy: https://bit.ly/48N9wOV

We’ll need to remove 10 gigatons of CO2 each year to avoid reaching climate tipping points, according to recent projections based on IPCC estimates. A gigaton is a massive amount, so it can be hard to grasp what a “gigaton of carbon dioxide removal” means.  

One gigaton of emissions is equivalent to:  
      🏭 Running 220 coal-fired plants for one year
      🚗 Driving 215 million gasoline-powered vehicles for one year  

Our new article breaks down the massive scale of carbon dioxide removal (CDR) needed by 2050 to stabilize the climate 👉  https://bit.ly/3CPDYMI

Cleantech and renewable energy are vital for slashing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. These technologies rely on minerals and metals that must be extracted, used, and disposed of responsibly. To limit harmful pollution, warming emissions, excessive water use, and other damage to communities and ecosystems, we must manage these resources sustainably 

The minerals and metals used in cleantech provide an opportunity  to do so, and move away from an entirely extractive energy economy: 

"Unlike fossil fuels, the materials used for batteries, solar cells and wind turbines offer enormous economic potential for reuse. Remarkably, the cumulative mass of materials needed to support the clean energy transition through 2050 is less than the amount of coal currently extracted each year," RMI CEO Jon Creyts](urn:li:person:znZm2PJ_Fx) told @[World Economic Forum. "By re-engineering material flows, we can keep scarce high-value resources in circulation and reduce our dependence on ongoing extraction."

Learn more from 14 experts who spoke with WEF about how to responsibly use resources as the energy transition accelerates: https://bit.ly/4fOn1Ro

When organic waste – like food scraps, yard clippings, and cardboard – breaks down in landfills and dumpsites around the world, it creates methane, a climate super pollutant with over 80 times the near-term warming power of carbon dioxide. 

Thankfully, there are proven, cost-effective strategies to reduce and recycle organic waste and strengthen emission controls at disposal sites that could curb nearly 90 percent of methane emissions from solid waste.  

More than 30 countries at #COP29 signed on to set specific targets to reduce waste sector methane and develop new policies and programs to achieve those targets. Focusing on the waste sector is essential to achieve the Global Methane Pledge’s goal to cut methane by 30% below 2020 levels, by 2030, limit near-term temperature rise, and avoid dangerous climate tipping points. Quick action means benefits today: since methane is released alongside other harmful pollutants, addressing methane from organic waste will also protect public health, improve air quality, and advance environmental justice. https://bit.ly/4eIDuF9

Food waste is the single most landfilled material and causes nearly 60% of landfill methane emissions, the EPA reports. Methane, a powerful greenhouse gas, is generated when organic materials — such as food waste, yard waste, and paper — decompose in landfills without oxygen. As a result, landfills are the third largest source of methane in the United States with annual emissions equivalent to driving 66 million cars or operating 79 coal-fired power plants for an entire year. 

Stakeholders at every level can help cut methane emissions by preventing organic materials from ending up in landfills. Such "upstream strategies" include minimizing waste, donating excess food, and recycling residual organics into animal feed, compost, or biogas. 

"Downstream strategies" focus on improving landfill design, operations, and monitoring practices to increase methane capture and reduce methane leaks. Combining upstream and downstream efforts could slash waste methane emissions by 15% by 2030, according to the Center for Global Sustainability at the University of Maryland. 

Beyond the climate benefits, cutting methane emissions from waste can help address food insecurity, reduce health risks, improve the quality of life for nearby communities, create jobs, and produce valuable products, such as compost. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/3Zb9tIv

The five key steps include: 

1️⃣ Developing a program team and goals 

2️⃣ Supporting local contractor engagement 

3️⃣ Designing incentive offerings 

4️⃣ Developing community education and outreach 

5️⃣ Launching and managing the program 

Learn more and take action in your community: https://bit.ly/3Zhorhl

As traditional fossil fuel power plants approach retirement at the same time electricity demand is expected to grow, US regulators who are tasked with ensuring the affordability and reliability of the electric grid are focusing on resource adequacy — the ability of the grid to supply enough electricity to meet demand under a range of possible future conditions.

Risks to resource adequacy in the Western United States are primarily driven by demand growth uncertainties, extreme weather and climate change, delays in the construction of planned power generating resources, and slow transmission expansion.  

By improving planning practices to reflect current drivers of risk, considering more types of resource adequacy solutions, and seeking reforms in utility business models, regulators can reduce the risk of future resource adequacy shortfalls while continuing to transition to a low-carbon grid that benefits customers and enables local economic development. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/4fY0Kjr

This work is made possible with support from the Heising-Simons Foundation.

As demand for electricity grows and the current grid system ages, the US needs to expand the capacity of its transmission infrastructure to effectively deliver electricity to end users.  

Recent spending on transmission lines in the United States has focused on lower-voltage local projects, which typically serve a single utility, instead of regional projects, which can meet multiple utilities’ needs at the same time. Relying on local projects and not coordinating with regional planning is more costly and inefficient for addressing overall grid upgrades. 

There is little oversight for local transmission projects because of a regulatory gap as a result of several factors at the state, regional, and federal levels. Our report lays out 11 recommendations to close the regulatory gap and help build the electricity grid of the future with better bang for the customer’s buck: https://bit.ly/3AYb7Fd

#Transmission #Electricity #ElectricityGrid

As #COP29 draws to a close, the need for actionable solutions across high-emission sectors is clearer than ever. The steel industry, a major driver of economic and social development, is also one of the most energy-intensive sectors, emitting roughly 7% of global pollution. In China, steel’s share of emissions is even higher, accounting for around 16% of the annual total. Accelerating the deployment of near-zero-carbon steel projects is essential to achieve global climate goals and presents an opportunity for high-quality, sustainable growth within the industry.  

RMI’s latest report, Unlocking the First Wave of Breakthrough Steel Investments in China, provides an in-depth analysis of the economic pathways for the steel industry's shift to near-zero emissions in China. The report examines how stakeholders — including policymakers, demand-side buyers, and financial institutions — can facilitate this transition. It also offers six recommendations aimed at expediting the transition process.

During Gender Day at #COP29, RMI’s Energy Transition Academy (ETA) hosted a virtual roundtable — featuring women leaders from around the world — on the critical role of women’s leadership in advancing renewable energy projects across the Caribbean, India, the Pacific, and Sub-Saharan Africa. 

The webinar underscored the importance of inclusive leadership in creating clean energy solutions, emphasizing that diverse perspectives are essential for sound decision-making and effective climate action. Despite the different geographies represented in the panel, “there are so many common threads in our paths”, noted @[Nitisha Agrawal](urn:li:person:w-A_vNnf_V), founder of the Smokeless Cookstove Foundation in India. “Forums like these establish community and a common shared hope and vision. Our problems, our solutions, are not so different from women in other regions.”  

The discussions highlighted the experiences and stories of women energy leaders advancing renewable energy projects in their countries, and the importance of capacity building and mentorship programs to support career growth. “Great opportunities are not out of reach, as long as they are visible to us,” panelist TeAnn Thomas added, “as the climate space is still evolving, there’s always new opportunities to explore and each of us has a role to play in making these visible for the next generation of energy leaders.” TeAnn spoke from experience, as she is an alumnus of both the ETA Global Caribbean Fellowship Program and the WIRE Network.  

Joining RMI’s Amber Zirnhelt, RPP, MCIP and Valentina G. on the roundtable were Nitisha Agrawal](urn:li:person:w-A_vNnf_V), @[Smokeless Cookstove Foundation](urn:li:person:uP_y-T8_5l) and Clean Cooking Mentorship Program hosted by @[Sustainable Energy for All (SEforALL), Clean Cooking Alliance (CCA) and GWNET: Global Women's Network for the Energy Transition; Saudatu B., Rural Electrification Agency of Nigeria and Africa Minigrids Program; Nnyeka R. Prescod, Climate Finance Access Network (CFAN); and TeAnn Thomas, Turks and Caicos Islands Government and Women in Renewable Energy (WIRE) Network.

The webinar built on the vision of centering gender and women empowerment in the climate agenda at COP and beyond, and rode the momentum of the WIRE Network’s recent convening in Barbados, where women leaders came together to strategize on advancing sustainable energy solutions across the Caribbean. "Energy efficiency is the most important lever not being pulled right now," RMI CEO Jon Creyts](urn:li:person:znZm2PJ_Fx) told @[Bloomberg News. "It will make the entire energy transition cheaper, faster, fairer, and more secure."

A pioneering financial deal in Chile demonstrates how innovative lending mechanisms can speed the retirement of coal assets and support affected communities. The $125-million partnership between IDB Invest and Engie Energia Chile has established a model financial mechanism that links interest rate reductions to early coal plant retirement while also funding renewable development. 

The structure's efficacy has been validated through real results. Multiple generating units at the Tocopilla thermal facility have ceased operations ahead of their scheduled retirement date, while a 151-megawatt wind generation facility in Calama progresses toward commissioning.  

This successful implementation can be attributed to several critical enabling conditions: Chile's comprehensive policy framework supporting coal phase-out initiatives; definitive demand signals from industrial electricity consumers; strategic deployment of blended finance by development institutions; and the increasingly favorable economics of renewable energy generation. 

Chile’s progress extends beyond this deal. In 2019, they decided to phase out coal. At the time, the South American country had 28 coal-fired power plants. At #COP29 on Friday, Chilean Minister of Environment Maisa Rojas provided a status update on a panel moderated by RMI’s Brian  O'Hanlon and hosted by The Powering Past Coal Alliance (PPCA): Chile has closed 11 coal-fired power plants and there are 9 more available for closing or retrofitting by 2026.

To learn more about how Chile made this happen and the lessons it learned, check out this case study: https://bit.ly/3YRKVUx

To decarbonize the steel sector, reducing emissions from making iron is essential. And green hydrogen provides a viable pathway to achieve near-zero emissions in the ironmaking process.  

Globally, steel supply chains are dependent on key regions that supply iron ore. That’s why iron and steel are usually produced in the same integrated facilities, near access to cheap coal.  

When iron ore reserves are co-located with renewable energy resources, a higher value green hydrogen-based iron product can be produced. This presents the opportunity to physically separate the making of steel from the production of iron to establish Green Iron Corridors. The production of green iron can instead take place in areas with ample renewables, to make clean hydrogen. Transporting that green iron to existing steel centers can help steelmaking remain where demand is strong. 

At #COP29 in Baku, Azerbaijan, RMI brought together representatives from various geographies across the supply chain — iron ore miners, renewable energy, and hydrogen developers, financiers, and government officials — to explore the emerging production and trade dynamics for Green Iron Corridors and identify action items to make them a reality.  

Learn more about green iron corridors: https://bit.ly/3CvptgP 

[Pictured: Sadesh Sookraj of IFC - International Finance Corporation, Bronwyn Grieve of Fortescue, Alex Hewitt of CWP Global, Khroumbaly Mohamed Lehbib of the Ministry of Petroleum, Mines and Energy, Mauritania, Thomas Koch Blank of RMI]

Energy efficiency is the most important lever that is not being pulled right now to advance climate goals. Improving energy efficiency in industry, buildings, transport, and power sectors could generate 40% of the emissions reductions required by the Paris Agreement.  To do so, we would need to double the pace of energy efficiency upgrades by 2030, but there has been a lack of leadership and credible government plans for energy efficiency.

RMI is proud to be part of a coalition of countries, businesses, and organizations anchored by Mission Efficiency that is urging world leaders at #COP29 to commit to fund energy efficiency because it will make the energy transition cheaper, faster, fairer, and more secure. 

Learn more and join the movement: https://bit.ly/4fNPFkY

Katalist, a 'book and claim' registry launched by the Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller Center for Zero Carbon Shipping and RMI, aims to accelerate decarbonization in the shipping industry.  

The maritime sector sits at the center of the global economy – more than 80% of world trade travels by sea – and is responsible for around 3% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Many shipping companies are taking action to reduce their emissions, but it can be challenging to find customers willing to pay for the higher cost of clean shipping fuels.  

Katalist helps bridge this gap by separating the low-emissions product from its sustainability benefits and transferring them to another party through a registry, enabling customers to claim the environmental benefits of low-emissions transport without physically receiving it, while stimulating overall use of clean fuels.  

Freight customers willing to pay a green premium for low-emission transport services can reliably use Katalist to achieve their decarbonization goals. And ship operators using low-emission fuels can use it to access a broader market of freight customers willing to pay an environmental supplement. The registry will help drive investments in lower-emission fuels, boosting their cost-competitiveness and commercial availability. 

Learn more about how #Katalist is #FuelingChange and facilitating the decarbonization of the maritime industry: www.katalist.eco 

An extended thank you to Energy Web for supporting the development of this registry.

Carbon is a necessary raw material for chemical industry products, which means the sector can’t fully decarbonize. But changing where CO2 is sourced from and how it is used has the potential to reduce emissions from chemical manufacturing processes. Carbon is currently sourced mainly from fossil fuels, so the industry must focus on "defossilization" and reducing emissions to mitigate its climate impact. 

One defossilization pathway is carbon utilization, a process in which carbon dioxide is captured from industrial or atmospheric sources to be reused as a feedstock. Although this process is energy intensive, it can help produce high value chemicals and fuels with a much lower carbon intensity compared to conventional fossil feedstock routes. By 2050, CO2 utilization along with other solutions could result in an 89% reduction in the amount of carbon derived from fossil fuels for use in chemicals. 

Learn more about CO2 utilization and how it can be paired with other emissions reduction solutions in the chemical manufacturing industry: https://bit.ly/3AudbV7

Hydrogen is the smallest element, but it plays a big role in the chemical industry. In the United States, 10 million metric tons of hydrogen are produced each year, with over 90 percent of it used as a feedstock for chemical products. 

Because most hydrogen today is derived from natural gas, hydrogen production accounts for almost half of the chemical sector emissions in the United States. Thankfully, there are cleaner, lower emissions pathways to producing hydrogen, such as using renewable energy to convert water into hydrogen. To further reduce emissions in the chemical sector, cleaner hydrogen can also be used for heating as a replacement for natural gas. 

Due to its wide role in the chemicals industry, reducing emissions from hydrogen production would have a huge impact on emissions from the industry overall. However, making the change to cleaner hydrogen for heating should be weighed against other decarbonization opportunities, such as direct electrification and enhanced energy efficiency.

Learn more about how hydrogen produced through lower emissions routes can reduce greenhouse gas emissions from the chemicals sector: https://bit.ly/3AfULHM

Manufacturing the chemicals that form the building blocks of many of our everyday items requires high temperatures. Thermal batteries can not only meet those heating needs, they can also help plants achieve significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, improve energy efficiency, and potentially lower energy costs. 

Thermal batteries are made with materials that have a high capacity to absorb and store heat. They are heated to temperatures up to 1800 degrees Celsius with electricity, and then can release the heat as needed to industrial plants for uses such as heating for reactions, distillation, and drying. Thermal batteries are made of a proven technology that can be integrated into existing plants to maximize both economic and environmental benefits. 

Learn more about how thermal batteries can serve as an alternative for industrial heating: https://bit.ly/3Agz7TC

Who says playing SimCity doesn’t prepare you for real life? • Pair solar installation with electrification 

• Align with community priorities

Virtual power plants (VPPs) aggregate smart thermostats and appliances, electric vehicles, and distributed energy resources of households and businesses to provide many of the same services as a traditional power plant by coordinating the charging, discharging, and management of all the connected devices. 

VPPs reduce power sector emissions by shifting demand to be met by lower-emissions energy resources. In 2024, VPPs across the country could shift demand to avoid up to 7.3 million tons of carbon dioxide emissions. By 2035, VPPs could avoid 12 to 28 million tons, or two to four percent of projected US power sector carbon emissions. 

However, few regions have fully integrated VPPs into grid planning and operations. Grid decision makers need to evaluate, plan for, and realize the full value of VPPs, including avoided transmission and distribution costs.  

Learn more about the key conditions for fully realizing VPPs’ grid benefits: https://bit.ly/3YaNVeu

With a growing demand for energy, many regions in the Global South are investing in their rich stock of renewable resources instead of relying on imported fossil fuels. Solar and wind generation in the Global South has been growing at 23 percent a year for the past five years and already supplies nine9 percent of electricity— only five years behind the Global North. 
  
Despite starting this transition later, the Global South is moving faster than the Global North. One- fifth of the Global South has already overtaken the Global North in terms of the share of solar and wind or electrification. By 2030, the Global South could be adding as much renewable capacity as the Global North. 
  
Even with these positive advancements, there remains the necessity to go faster, and to do more to help low-income countries. Strong domestic policy, global capital directed especially to low-income countries, and technology transfer are critical to accelerating the energy transition and boosting economic growth across the Global South. https://bit.ly/3zUuwGF

Green shipping corridors are maritime routes between one or more ports to enable ships to use zero-emission fuels and technologies, helping to accelerate the adoption of low-emission shipping fuels and reduce greenhouse gas emissions across the maritime sector. 

Our latest joint feasibility study explores a green shipping corridor for dry bulk trade between the US Gulf Coast and Japan, which is consistently one of the top 10 US trading partners. 

This analysis, supported by the Green Hydrogen Catapult and led by RMI in collaboration with the Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller Center for Zero Carbon Shipping, Cargill, and Hy Stor Energy, focuses on the: 

• Technical, economic, and operational feasibility of a green corridor 

• Adoption of green methanol in dual-fuel bulk carriers by 2030 

• Identifying cost drivers and opportunities to reduce premiums 

• Evaluating the impact of policies and regulations on zero-emission technology 

The insights uncovered can be applicable to other trading routes. Check out the full study here to explore the results in more detail: https://bit.ly/3YkuYHp

As the planet gets hotter and more humid, demand for residential air-conditioning is set to triple by 2030, with billions of room AC units being installed across the globe. To meet this demand while simultaneously tackling pollution and energy efficiency, RMI and its partners launched the Global Cooling Prize in 2018 to spur innovative cooling technologies that have 5X lower climate impact and announced two winners in 2021. 
 
In June, The Washington Post joined RMI in India to see how Global Cooling Prize-winning prototypes are being tested and primed to tackle some of the most hot and humid temperatures on Earth — and to gauge how much they could shrink air conditioning’s rapidly growing emissions. 
 
"We must switch and try to accelerate the market toward these super-efficient ACs now," RMI cooling expert Ankit Kalanki told The Post. "Otherwise, we will be adding a large number of inefficient units that will only contribute to this vicious loop of warming."

If nearly all AC units sold globally meet the Global Cooling Prize contest parameters by 2040, the improved cooling technology could prevent up to half a degree Celsius of global warming by the end of the century.   
 
Read on to learn how RMI, the Clean Cooling Collaborative, CEPT Research and Development Foundation, and many other partners across industries and governments are working on a global effort to commercialize next generation ACs and get them onto store shelves and into homes and apartments: https://lnkd.in/eN3ag4Sv

Slashing methane emissions is among the fastest – and cheapest – ways to slow the pace of global warming in this decisive decade. But first, we have to find where methane is being released. 
 
New satellites and monitoring tools and technologies from RMI Environmental Defense Fund, Carbon Mapper, and more are making it clearer than ever where methane is being released worldwide.  With this improved transparency , decision-makers can target their methane reduction strategies to the places where they are needed most.  
 
"We've finally gotten to the space where we understand fundamentally that things that are invisible can harm us -- and it held us back for a really long time. That's where I think the satellite data is going to be absolutely transformational, because methane isn’t invisible anymore. [...] That's really going to open up minds and doors and decisions all over -- financial, actors, governments, industry, all over the place," said RMI’s Deborah Gordon during our #ClimateWeekNYC event today on efforts to reduce methane from the oil and gas sector. 
 
RMI’s Oil Climate Index plus Gas (OCI+) tool, supported by Bloomberg Philanthropies, estimates methane leakage based on the amount of oil and gas produced for oil and gas basins globally. https://bit.ly/3U4oJFt

Critical sectors of our economy like cement, steel, shipping, and aviation urgently require decarbonization. Technologies exist that can significantly reduce emissions in these sectors. However, scaling up these alternatives is expensive and requires high upfront investments, tightening supply, and driving up prices for buyers. Enter demand aggregation. 

Demand aggregation can help overcome these barriers by consolidating demand from multiple individual buyers into a single, larger signal. This helps de-risk new markets for buyers, producers and their financiers, kickstarting new, low-emissions supply chains. Together, climate-ambitious consumers can send strong demand signals for low-emissions products and aid suppliers in attracting investment to scale rapidly.

The steel sector currently generates 11% of global carbon dioxide emissions. To reduce emissions while meeting global demand, the approach to making iron must shift.

Historically, iron and steel have been produced in large integrated facilities which run on coal. Because of this, access to iron ore and cheap coal resources were crucial elements in deciding where ironmaking and steelmaking would take place. With low-cost renewable energy, the direct reduction of iron (DRI) – an alternative to the traditional blast furnace – will play an increasingly important role in the production of steel. Green hydrogen can serve as the feedstock for the DRI process, offering a viable pathway to near-zero-emissions iron and steel production.

Moving forward, steel production can enter a new era where a portion of iron production is decoupled from where steel is made, creating green iron corridors. This would mean locating green iron production in regions with optimal renewable energy and access to iron ore and exporting it to steelmaking regions that have strong manufacturing capabilities and are close to demand.

Restructuring half of primary steelmaking to use green iron could save > $25 billion annually across the 10 highest priority importers at today's prices, which translates to a savings of $80-125 per ton of steel. Further, fast-tracking corridor opportunities between existing and emerging iron exporter regions to the EU and Asia could abate an annual ~30 million tons of CO2 by 2030.

This report, developed by RMI and the Green Hydrogen Catapult, outlines how green iron corridors can offer significant benefits along the supply chain including cost savings, increased efficiency, and economic opportunity for both importing and exporting countries. Of the fifty importer/exporter options evaluated, more than 10 key corridors emerged as high contenders.  

Paving the way for global green iron trade will require bilateral political action to recognize the net benefits. We look forward to continuing the conversation around green iron corridors at #ClimateWeekNYC and #CEM15.

There are going to be a lot of acronyms flying around #COP29, but the most important one is the #NCQG: The New Collective Quantified Goal on Climate Finance. To learn what it is, how we reach it, what ‘collective and quantified’ really mean, and why it’s our best hope for meeting climate ambition, read on!

A streamlined, accessible approach to climate finance could make all the difference in climate-vulnerable regions. The NCQG will seek to build a practical pathway for timely, effective distribution of funds to communities on the front lines of a changing climate.

Wildfires threaten our forests, communities, and environment. But what if we could prevent them AND create sustainable building materials?

Our report, “Fighting Fire with Low Carbon Buildings,” explores *why* waste trees from wildfire mitigation harvests should be used to create mass timber products: it improves forest health 🌲 and it can help build low embodied carbon buildings 🏗️.

1️⃣  Right Size Now: Start small with community-based manufacturers to test and refine business models and use today's existing supply. 
2️⃣  Plan for Scale: Prepare for larger investments to meet the growing demand for sustainable wood products and forest restoration.

How can electricity rates accelerate or hinder heat pump adoption? In a new policy brief, RMI’s Joe Dammel, Mike Henchen, and Amar Shah reveal that many heat pump users pay disproportionately high bills due to outdated rate structures, particularly during winter months (see chart below). 
 
For utilities and their regulators to improve rate design and make energy costs lower and fairer for homeowners that electrify, RMI proposes several near- and long-term solutions, such as: 

1️⃣  Seasonal heating rates – aligning rates with seasonal demand peaks can lower winter bills while ensuring utilities recover costs fairly  

2️⃣  Time-varying pricing – implementing rates that reflect the real-time cost of electricity throughout the day would encourage off-peak usage, reduce grid strain, and lower costs to customers 

3️⃣  Targeted rebates – offering direct financial support (e.g. upfront or monthly) to heat pump users currently overcharged under existing rate structures could make electrification more accessible and equitable

When Hurricane Francine cut off power to almost half a million customers in New Orleans last September, First Grace United Methodist Church still had lights, water, and air conditioning thanks to its solar-plus-battery-storage microgrid. 

First Grace is part of a network of Community Lighthouses born out of the experience New Orleans residents faced after Hurricanes Katrina and Ian. The Community Lighthouses are 10 community buildings, with more planned for the future, powered by solar and batteries and designed to provide essentials like power, air conditioning, water, meals, a community gathering space, and more when storms hit.  

Solar-plus-battery-storage microgrids are typically the first step to building a resilience hub as they provide reliable power when the grid goes out. They also benefit communities when skies are clear, while simultaneously supporting local businesses.

Building a microgrid is more appealing and doable than ever before, with solar and battery costs declining and other support available. To help communities overcome microgrid implementation challenges, RMI led a "Microgrids for Resilient Communities" cohort where members and RMI learned from each other’s direct experiences.

The Carbon Mapper Coalition — of which RMI is a member — launched the Tanager-1 satellite in August 2024 to detect and monitor methane and carbon dioxide super-emitters around the world. Utility regulation must balance the needs of all stakeholders. One component of utility regulation that is not in balance is the allowed return on equity (ROE), or rate of profit offered by the regulatory process. 

Right now, ROEs are higher than objective benchmarks would suggest is necessary to attract capital for critical investments. We recommend ways that regulators and power sector stakeholders can more confidently estimate a utility’s cost of equity, to better inform allowed ROEs. These include observing utilities that trade close to book value, a sense check based on relatable benchmarks, and ensuring inputs to financial models reflect best practices. 

RMI’s latest brief busts myths about lower ROEs and provides a roadmap for utility regulators and stakeholders to right-size utility ROE, while ensuring ample access to funding and aligning incentives to make electricity more affordable: https://bit.ly/3Ddsgfj

From financing to policy to local community buy-in, ecosystem support is key to a successful clean industrial hub. Securing the social license for development is particularly critical to set up a project for success. Effective stakeholder management is critical for keeping projects on track to reach final investment decisions and begin construction. Without community support and buy-in, first-of-a-kind or later generation decarbonization projects often face delays or cancellations. In short, missing any of the key pillars of holistic support— policy, finance, research and development, and community engagement — can derail the entire timeline for achieving decarbonization goals. But there’s a fix: proactively identifying, analyzing, and engaging with key stakeholders, and RMI’s stakeholder analysis and mapping tool, S.A.M., simplifies this process. 

S.A.M. is a digital tool for analyzing and mapping relationships between all types of stakeholders. This visualization can help developers understand the complexities of local power dynamics on the ground, and learn about concerns and objections from key stakeholders before starting a long, expensive permitting process. Identifying and understanding the local stakeholder landscape enables better outreach and engagement strategies to derisk project deployment.   

Beyond mapping project stakeholders, S.A.M. is incredibly versatile, with applications for grid planning and electricity markets, supply chain visualization, partnership management and research in fields like finance, energy, public health, sustainability, urban planning, and more. This makes it a valuable tool not only for clean industrial hubs, but for holistic project management.

Access to clean, safe, affordable energy is life changing yet remains a challenge around the world. To mark Black History Month, RMI explores how helping communities shift to lower-cost, clean energy can save money, build business, and improve quality of life for Black communities in the United States.

In 27 cities stretching from Honolulu to Boston, RMI organized cohorts to make rooftop solar affordable and accessible for lower- and moderate-income (LMI) communities. In total, these Solarize campaigns helped nearly 1,200 households install more than 10 megawatts of residential solar, thanks to close collaboration between governments, community-based organizations, and others. 

The solar panels are already delivering real saving. And over their 25-year lifespan, the panels will avoid $8 million in electricity bills and reduce CO2 emissions by 106,000 tons, equivalent to planting over 1.76 million trees. Overall, 40 percent of the households that purchased solar systems through RMI’s Solarize cohort programs were in low- to middle-income communities. 

RMI also developed an Electrify cohort to help 12 teams — representing 20 local governments and their 30 partner organizations — develop and launch programs to help their community members electrify their homes, focusing on heat pumps. Across the country, these Electrify programs are projected to result in over 3,000 heat pump installations, which will help lower heating and cooling costs and improve air quality for households. A major part of these programs’ success was ensuring that community-based organizations were involved to ensure that resident voices were part of program design. 

RMI’s Solarize and Electrify cohorts have made cleantech available to more Americans, saving them money on their energy bills while also creating business opportunities for local contractors.

This year marks the 10th anniversary of the Paris Agreement. As part of this milestone, countries are preparing to submit updated nationally determined contributions (NDCs), their blueprints to cut greenhouse gas emissions and increase their resilience to heatwaves, drought, floods, and other warming-induced extremes.  

#NDCs are more than just commitments. They define how each country will contribute to the Paris Agreement’s goal by reducing their emissions. Each nation sets their own targets based on their unique circumstances, capabilities, and development priorities. While major emitters in the G20 will face the most scrutiny, Small Island Developing States and other vulnerable nations rely on NDCs to access critical climate finance and adaptation support. 

RMI is working with governments to strengthen and implement their NDCs. Through technical support, policy guidance, and investment strategies, RMI helps countries accelerate renewable energy transitions, advance clean transportation systems, and unlock climate finance, particularly in small island nations, Africa, and other emerging economies. 

With six months until the revised NDC deadline, in September 2025, nations must turn ambition into action. Strong, well-defined NDCs will not only drive national policies, but also attract investment and speed the global clean energy transition.  

Learn more about NDCs, why they are so important, and what’s next for these countries: https://bit.ly/4b7rrRI

To build a successful sports team, you need more than just individuals who can score. Defense, tactics, roster depth, and teamwork all play a role as well. 

Similarly, a reliable electricity grid requires different components — including dispatchable and intermittent energy resources — which work together to maintain the availability of electricity for day-to-day and unusual situations, such as during extreme weather events. 

🏭 "Dispatchable" energy resources can be ramped up or down to increase or decrease power output on-demand during normal operating conditions, although sometimes they fail and cause outages. 

☀️ "Intermittent" energy resources like standalone solar and wind generation bolster the grid, reduce costs during their operation, and have weather-dependent, forecastable outputs. They can be “firmed” or made increasingly dispatchable with the addition of battery energy storage systems. 

Dispatchability is often misrepresented as the primary driver of electricity grid reliability, but system reliability as a whole is more important than dispatchability alone. 

To create affordable, whole-system reliability for customers, grid planners should assess and value how various system components such as intermittent resources, battery storage, virtual power plants, and electric transmission all complement each other and contribute to the grid. Learn more: https://bit.ly/41kMnjR

The US electricity grid needs new regional transmission lines to reduce energy waste, add new clean energy resources, and make the grid more resilient against extreme weather. 

But utilities are choosing to build smaller-scale transmission projects to take advantage of a regulatory gap that enables transmission projects within a utility’s territory to avoid robust review by federal and state regulators. Building out local transmission earns utilities guaranteed profits, but longer-range, higher voltage power lines can deliver greater benefits for customers. 

Regional transmission projects require greater coordination and collaboration between utilities, state regulators, and regional grid operators. With a regional-first approach, "you could be addressing local and regional needs simultaneously and meeting both needs in a more efficient manner," RMI’s Claire W. told Canary Media Inc.

RMI cofounder Amory Lovins honors the life and legacy of President Jimmy Carter: 

"I had the pleasure of meeting President Jimmy Carter in the Oval Office in 1977. He was such a fine engineer that, in that first meeting, the Energy Secretary (Jim Schlesinger) said three things — all technical, all wrong — and President Carter corrected him on all three. He’d invited me in to discuss my 1976 Foreign Affairs article reframing energy solutions around efficiency and renewables to achieve many social, economic, environmental, health, and security benefits. He’d mastered that article, and with impressive strategic foresight, launched decades of US progress on those lines. A decade ago he told me how helpful he’d found it. I feel blessed by the honor of having known this inspiring and remarkable man."

Components of modern buildings – concrete, steel, insulation, and more – all create climate pollution during their production, transportation, installation, and demolition. The total lifecycle climate pollution of these construction materials is what’s known as a building’s embodied carbon emissions. 

Modern buildings generally have a lot of embodied carbon emissions, but there are cost-effective materials and techniques that can reduce their carbon footprint — or even store carbon pollution. Microgrids function as a smaller version of the electricity grid — even smaller than a minigrid. They typically serve a specific facility — such as a hospital, water treatment plant, or campus — and can provide backup power during outages. Renewable microgrids could shift our grid to cleaner energy, while also making it more resilient.  

With the falling prices of solar panels, batteries, and other cleantech, plus federal support, microgrids are becoming even more appealing, especially to cities. "Since I started working on this in 2020, a lot more cities are becoming excited," RMI’s Michael Liebman told Fast Company. "The fact that there are all of these different grants and loans available within the Inflation Reduction Act definitely helps the business case for these projects." https://bit.ly/4iWkeHM

As knowledge grows, technologies improve. On the other hand, mature commodities stay roughly the same. Continuing to develop clean technologies will cut costs and pollution while delivering other benefits. 

For every doubling of deployment, solar and battery prices fall by about 20% — and prices have plummeted, down 99.9% since the 1950s. Batteries prices have fallen by 97% since the 1990s. 

The energy transition can be seen as a shift from dumb commodities to smart technologies. Learn more: https://bit.ly/3ZLlp44

Among utilities, integrated resource planning (IRP) is used to ensure that there will be enough electricity supply to meet tomorrow’s demand. IRPs are becoming more complex as the U.S. electricity grid evolves with the addition of renewable energy, the retirement of fossil fuel plants, the growth of distributed energy resources, and more extreme weather. 

A fundamental part of IRPs is grid modeling. However, traditional grid modeling assumptions for a centralized fossil fuel-based grid miss some critical factors. To better evaluate the roles of renewables, energy storage, and demand flexibility, grid planners need to: 

⏰ Use more detailed temporal data – “Time slices” and other aggregate data miss important production and demand dynamics over the course of the day and undervalue the benefits of energy storage.  

🌩️ Expand weather data to integrate information from multiple years – Relying on a single or average year of data can miss extreme weather events. 

🔋 Consider the resources of neighboring systems – Factoring in the availability of nearby energy resources helps avoid overbuilding and can help plan for efficient transmission lines that can serve more than one system, improving affordability and reliability. 

☀️ Model multiple renewable energy profiles – In diverse or large geographies, using more detailed geospatial data captures the benefits of renewable resources across multiple locations. 

Updating U.S. electricity grid modeling assumptions can help ensure that the future of the grid is affordable, reliable, and keeps the most vulnerable electricity customers top of mind.  https://bit.ly/401w5wJ

With over 2,000 stalls, the Wuse Market is the largest market in Abuja, Nigeria. Until recently, shop owners have relied on gasoline and diesel backup generators to power the market. These generators are expensive to run, extremely noisy, and fill the air with harmful emissions for both people and the planet.

To cut costs and emissions and increase power supply and longevity, GVE Projects Ltd, in partnership with Abuja Electricity Distribution Plc and the Wuse Market Association, have commissioned a 1 MW solar minigrid with battery storage to remove the need for these diesel generators.

This minigrid project will provide reliable and silent power to the shop owners and boost the local economy by allowing the market to stay open later. The Wuse Market can act as a catalyst for similar projects across Nigeria, increasing access to safe and consistent energy for those who need it the most.

2025 marks a decade since the Paris Agreement. Countries are now preparing to submit updated nationally determined contributions (NDCs) — their plans for reducing emissions and building resilience. 

For many nations, particularly Small Island Developing States and other climate-vulnerable economies, NDCs are more than just commitments. They serve as a crucial link to accessing climate finance, helping countries implement real, on-the-ground solutions. But securing funding for these priorities remains a challenge. 

This is where strengthening climate finance access becomes essential. At CFAN, advisors work alongside governments to navigate this complex process — supporting the design of finance-ready projects, identifying funding opportunities, and helping ensure that ambitious climate plans translate into tangible action. 

With just six months until the revised NDC deadline, there is a real opportunity to align national policies with available funding and investment. Strong, well-defined NDCs can unlock critical resources and drive meaningful progress toward climate resilience and a just energy transition. 

Learn more about why NDCs matter and what’s next in a recent piece written by CFAN Director Laetitia De Marez: https://bit.ly/4k1DsMy

C-PACE (Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy) loan programs have provided $9 billion for clean energy in commercial real estate across 25 U.S. states. But with a few key updates, C-PACE could unlock billions more for low-carbon materials and construction practices that reduce pollution while supporting American manufacturing. 

🔑 How do we get there?
✅ Recognize and fund low-carbon materials and whole-building embodied carbon reductions—updating C-PACE guidelines to support both material-specific improvements and overall reductions through Life Cycle Assessments (LCAs).
✅ Increase financing limits for projects that cut embodied carbon, making sustainable choices more cost-effective.
✅ Align C-PACE with state Buy Clean policies, new CALGreen codes, and voluntary certifications like LEED to ensure consistency nationwide.

RMI is facilitating a C-PACE for Embodied Carbon Reduction Working Group to make these updates a reality. Please email abukauskas@rmi.org if you would like to participate!

Read more from Auri Bukauskas, Anish Tilak, and Satyam Maharaj 👇   https://lnkd.in/gcgNGefh

What if public agencies could use their purchasing power to clean up one of the world’s dirtiest materials?

Concrete is everywhere — roads, bridges, buildings, sidewalks — but making it generates a lot of pollution. In the U.S., government-funded projects drive nearly half of all domestic concrete use, meaning public agencies can play a major role in cutting its emissions. 

One solution? Advance market commitments (AMCs). Used successfully to support public health goals, like accelerating vaccine development, AMCs could play a similar role in driving climate progress. By committing to purchase cleaner concrete from domestic suppliers, governments and other large buyers can invest in the next generation of clean, American-made construction products today. 

A new policy brief from RMI and Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC) explores how AMCs can accelerate the shift to cleaner concrete, outlining key challenges, opportunities, and a five-year plan for making it happen.

Can't think of a better way to kick off 2025 than celebrating this major win for building decarbonization: As of January 1, all of Chicago’s municipal buildings — from fire stations to international airports — are powered by 100% renewable energy 🌞💡!

“Cities and other local governments just don’t appreciate their ability to not just support their residents but also shape markets,” said Popkin. “Chicago is demonstrating directly how cities can lead by example, implement ambitious goals amidst evolving state and federal policy changes, and leverage their purchasing power to support a more equitable renewable energy future.”

A circular battery economy that repurposes, reuses, or recycles end-of-life batteries can help strengthen the electric vehicle (EV) supply chain. Marginal wells—low-producing oil and gas wells that extract less than eight bathtubs worth of oil or gas per day—are spread across the United States and are usually overlooked by elected officials and regulators. Nearly one in ten Americans lives in a county with over 1,000 marginal wells. These wells can be poorly maintained, leading to negative health effects on surrounding communities that outweigh the economic benefits of extracting such small amounts of oil or gas. 

And, like all leaky oil and gas systems, marginal wells can be a significant source of methane emissions that can cause fires and explosions, contribute to smog, and heat the planet over 80 times more powerfully than carbon dioxide

Utilities hold the key to the Global South’s clean energy future – a majority of which are state-owned power companies that control how energy is generated, distributed, and financed. As organic waste such as food scraps and yard clippings decomposes in landfills, it generates methane — a greenhouse gas with over 80 times the heat-trapping power of carbon dioxide in the short term. In California, landfills are the second-largest source of human-caused methane emissions, representing 22 percent of the state’s 2022 methane inventory. 

One way to stop these methane emissions is to keep organic waste from reaching landfills in the first place. In January 2022, California implemented state law SB 1383 with the goal of cutting landfill-bound organic waste 75 percent by 2025. The landmark law has already diverted 295 million pounds of food waste from landfills, created over 440 jobs in food recovery, and provided millions of meals to those in need.

With hurricane season on the horizon, the case for resilient energy infrastructure is stronger than ever.

RMI’s Solar Under Storm 2018 report was a turning point for the solar industry, offering the first comprehensive look at how solar energy systems perform in high-wind, hurricane-prone regions. Based on forensic analyses of hurricane damage and expert input from across the Caribbean, the report laid out clear, actionable design principles that have since influenced how resilient solar is deployed around the world.

Key insights from the original report include: 
🔅Prioritizing connection integrity and mountain hardware.
🔅Using dual post piers for ground mount systems to reduce vibrations. 
🔅Utilizing fixed mount systems, not tracking systems. 
🔅Selecting solar modules with independent testing for wind effects. 
🔅Standardizing resilient design — turning Caribbean best practices into widespread norms across the global industry. 

Drawing on years of field experience and system performance in some of the world’s most storm-exposed regions, the upcoming Solar Under Storm III report takes the next step. Last Friday, RMI’s Deborah Gordon spoke at the annual Society of Environmental Journalists conference on the hidden toll of America’s abandoned, orphaned, and marginal oil and gas wells.

Data centers and new manufacturing facilities are being built across the United States, leading to growing electricity demand that challenges the existing grid.  

Building new facilities that consume large amounts of electricity alongside new solar, wind, and battery resources — an approach RMI calls "Power Couples" — near existing natural gas generators with approved grid interconnections can help rapidly supply clean electricity and maintain grid reliability.

In the power couple structure, data centers wouldn’t even draw from the grid, RMI’s Uday Varadarajan explained to Canary Media Inc.. Instead, they’d be connected to clean power behind the "point of interconnection" between existing fossil fuel power plants and the wider grid. This approach could also make electricity more affordable for average customers while reducing air pollution. https://bit.ly/3QNPHiu

Methane leaks are a common occurrence that wastes energy, and costs consumers—and oil and gas companies—a lot of money 💰. But why does methane leak, when are companies not aware, how can we spot it, and how can we fix the problem? 

Methane leaks from many different places, including faulty equipment, pressure changes underground, and from old, barely-producing marginal and abandoned wells. While some of these leaks are small, others can be huge causing a whopping 66 percent of the gas produced to be thrown away. The good news is that solutions, like equipment replacement, routine monitoring, open certification, and buyers' prioritizing the procurement of low-leakage gas, can stop leakage and cut methane waste. 

At #CERAWeek, energy solutions across the board were discussed, including how oil and gas can aid in the energy transition. RMI’s latest article takes a dive deep into why methane leaks, and what companies can do to fix leaks and save money: https://bit.ly/3R8OUIS

🌍 Unlocking Climate Finance Across the Caribbean: Transformative Workshops in Barbados, The Bahamas, and Jamaica

At CFAN, we believe that unlocking climate finance isn't just about numbers. It's about empowering people, building partnerships, and driving transformative change. Over the past few months, our CFAN Advisors have led in-country workshops in Barbados, the Bahamas, and Jamaica, equipping participants with the knowledge and tools to design and deliver bankable climate projects. 

📍 In Barbados, we explored the development of a sustainable climate finance architecture through hands-on exercises, stakeholder mapping, and a comprehensive examination of accessing climate finance mechanisms. 

📍 In the Bahamas, we explored innovative financing instruments like blended finance and green bonds, using real-world case studies such as the South Coast Water Reclamation Project and the Race for Resilience Program for the Family Islands. 

📍 In Jamaica, we focused on stakeholder engagement and practical skills for developing climate finance proposals, sparking collaboration and a shared vision for resilience. Participants valued the interactive sessions, from problem tree analysis to stakeholder mapping. 

Each workshop reflected CFAN’s commitment to country-driven capacity building, creating safe spaces for dialogue, collaboration, and learning. Collectively, over 100 participants, including representatives from government, civil society, and the private sector, engaged deeply with climate finance concepts tailored to their national contexts.

What if there was a way we could effectively store millions of tons of carbon while helping to solve America's housing crisis, create jobs, and boost manufacturing — all at once?  

Turns out, there is. And the solution is rooted in something we’re already producing in abundance: biomass. 

Right now, the U.S. is facing a housing shortage of 4-7 million homes. But building new homes comes at a steep climate cost, adding around 55 million tons of embodied CO₂ emissions every year. At the same time, we generate over a billion tons of agricultural, forestry, and landfill waste each year — material that often has no market value and can even increase wildfire and pollution risks. So why not use that waste to help build the homes we need?  

By converting biomass into building materials — like insulation, flooring, panels, and low-carbon concrete alternatives — we could *store* carbon instead of emitting it, build homes more sustainably, and create jobs in domestic manufacturing, particularly in rural areas where the biomass is sourced. 

A new RMI report by Chris Magwood, Auri Bukauskas, Tracy Huynh, and Victor Olgyay shows that by 2050, even with conservative adoption rates, the U.S. could:  

🔹 Store over 100 million metric tons of CO₂e in new homes 
🔹 Unlock $79 billion in domestic manufacturing opportunities 
🔹 And create tens of thousands of direct jobs, with hundreds of thousands more through the broader supply chain 

And the best part? Unlike many carbon removal solutions still in development, biomass building products are already being produced and used across the U.S. and globally. They’re cost-competitive, commercially available, and ready to scale.  

Curious to learn more about how we can build housing, create jobs, and store carbon at the same time? Read Building With Biomass: A New American Harvest 👇

Indian cities are grappling with multiple environmental pressures — facing extreme summer heat, water scarcity, and monsoon flooding.  

New analysis from RMI and Water, Environment, Land and Livelihoods (WELL) Labs explores how nature-based solutions (NbS), in tandem with traditional gray infrastructure solutions such as culverts and storm drains, can help cities build resilience, improve water security, and cool urban environments. 

The report synthesizes insights from a multi-stakeholder workshop of practitioners, policymakers, and researchers. Together, they identified the barriers to NbS adoption and co-developed actionable solutions across building, neighborhood, and city scales. 

Key takeaways from the workshop show that the effectiveness of NbS depends on local context, yet systemic gaps in governance, finance, and policy often limit progress. Scaling beyond pilots will require stronger national and/or state-level frameworks, along with valuing ecological functions to mobilize financing and ensure long-term adoption. 

The findings lay the groundwork for a policy architecture to integrate NbS into India’s urban future — offering pathways for climate resilience, healthier communities, and sustainable growth.

Climate pollution created during the production of plastics is often overshadowed by concerns about plastic waste and recycling. Despite existing emissions accounting standards and guidance, it’s still difficult to get a complete assessment of a plastic product’s climate impact, which makes it harder to make informed, low-emissions purchasing and design decisions.

🚚 India’s road to cleaner transport is in view, and zero-emission trucks (ZETs) are ready to lead the way. While subsidies are an important start, reaching India’s goal of putting 10,000 zero-emission trucks on the road in the next three years will require substantial funding for charging infrastructure. What’s needed now is collaboration between governments, financiers, utilities, and operators pulling in the same direction. 

The rewards are substantial: cleaner air, enhanced energy security, and lower logistics costs that will benefit businesses and communities across India.

Amusement parks, surf spots, innovative landfills… Orange County, California has it all.  

OC Waste & Recycling, County of Orange (OCWR) diverts organic waste (like food and yard scraps) and monitors its landfills to keep methane, a powerful greenhouse gas, from escaping into the atmosphere. OCWR’s Smart Landfill Program can identify methane leaks and fine-tune methane capture from its landfills. 🗓️ July 24, 2025, is this year’s Earth Overshoot Day — the point at which humanity has consumed all the resources that Earth can regenerate in one year. Everything we use from July 25 through December is “on ecological credit,” drawing down forests, soils, and biodiversity. 

Over the past decade, the date has hovered around late July, offering a striking reminder: we’re consistently overshooting by nearly five months every year. The biggest driver? Carbon pollution. The good news: we know what it takes to change course. Decarbonizing energy systems, cutting food waste, investing in regenerative land use and biodiversity, and building efficient, low-carbon infrastructure are all methods we can take to reduce our burden on the planet.  

At RMI, we’re advancing practical solutions that address overshoot at its core — by driving deep decarbonization, energy equity, and systems-level change. 

🌱 Every action matters — and collective momentum can move this date in the right direction. 🔗 Learn more about our work to accelerate the clean energy transition: https://bit.ly/4lJMXR6

Indonesia’s nickel and aluminum industries are growing fast, and so is their reliance on coal-fired captive power. More and more of these projects are using off-grid coal power, known as “captive power,” which runs outside the main electricity system and is hard to regulate. 

Our new report shows that captive power in Indonesia has more than doubled in just five years, and over 80% of it is coal-fired. Most of this growth is tied to nickel smelters, with aluminum projects also on the rise. If things keep going this way, these two industries could become the biggest source of new coal power in the country by 2030. 

This has serious climate impacts. In 2023, emissions from Indonesia's nickel industry were estimated to account for one-quarter of the country’s total energy and industrial emissions reported from the prior year. Even though Indonesia has policies aimed at reducing reliance on coal-fired power plants, many industrial projects are exempt, allowing them to continue using captive coal power. Weak market signals, limited grid access, and missing emissions data are making it easier for coal to keep growing — just as global trade rules are demanding cleaner and more transparent supply chains. 

📘 This report provides a detailed look at trends, drivers, and enabling conditions to decarbonize power systems for Indonesia’s mineral industries, while offering recommendations for aligning industrial growth with national climate and sustainability goals. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/4nV2EGD

For years, environmentalists and other activists focused primarily on the climate impacts of carbon dioxide, choosing to ignore the warming power of methane. At the time, it was believed methane was a small fraction of emissions. But as methane detection technologies have rapidly advanced, the scale and severity of methane’s threats to our climate, energy security, and economy have become clear. Methane has over 80 times more heat-trapping potency than carbon dioxide and could cause 45 percent of warming in the next 10 years. 

As Carl Pope shares in his op-ed, published in this morning’s The New York Times, "With each passing year extreme weather events do more and more damage to human communities...We need to start reducing atmospheric overheating in time to prevent such catastrophes, and cutting methane emissions is the only quick way to do so."

Methane leaks occur at every step in the oil and gas supply chain. As RMI’s research into marginal wells has highlighted, half of oil field leaks come from oil wells that no longer produce significant oil or gas for sale. Properly sealing these and other oil and gas operations will immediately benefit the climate, help reduce air, land, and water pollution, and avoid detrimental impacts to human health. https://nyti.ms/4lmGcUI

Embodied carbon, or the emissions released during the lifecycle of building materials, is projected to increase 35 percent by 2050 — more than half driven by concrete alone.  

That’s why RMI, the Carbon Leadership Forum, and the University of Washington Life Cycle Lab released a new report, Embodied Carbon Pathways to 2050 for the United States. The report sets 1.5°C-aligned embodied carbon targets for US buildings for the first time, analyzes the gap between business-as-usual and our targets, and models several scenarios showing how policies and strategies could close that gap. 

Key takeaways:  

💡 A portfolio of solutions is essential: no single fix will deliver the reductions we need. 

🏗️ Concrete is central: cement and clinker substitution, kiln efficiency, and carbon capture are critical levers. 

✨ Design matters: optimizing steel and concrete structures and substituting lower-carbon materials can deliver big near-term gains. 

⚡ Scale rapidly, while investing in emerging tech: scaling today’s best practices delivers most reductions before 2040; emerging tech will play a bigger role after. 

📜 Policy drives progress: standards, incentives, product-level limits, and carbon pricing are among the most impactful tools. 

Explore the report ➡️ https://lnkd.in/ezdXj248

As this summer’s heat dome drives triple-digit temperatures and dangerous humidity across much of the United States, access to reliable cooling has become essential, not just for comfort, but to protect health, preserve food and medicine, and keep critical infrastructure operating. 

But the surge in AC demand is pushing our outdated grid to the brink — and exposing the urgent need for cleaner cooling and electricity solutions.  
A new piece by RMI’s Ankit Kalanki, Lauren Shwisberg, and Ian McGavisk, explores how to meet rising cooling needs without overloading the system or accelerating emissions:   

🔹 Super-efficient ACs: Most ACs in our homes today are energy inefficient and poor at handling humidity. Next-gen ACs tested in India cut energy use by 60%, reduce peak demand by up to 50%, and manage humidity better — making them ideal for hot, humid U.S. climates. 

🔹 Virtual Power Plants (VPPs): By coordinating smart thermostats, EVs, and batteries, VPPs shift energy use during peak demand, avoiding outages and reducing the need for costly peaker plants. Last year, utilities in 34 states and Puerto Rico took action to scale VPPs. RMI analysis indicates that they could grow to meet 60 GW of demand by 2030.  

🔹 Cooling innovation: Third Derivative, RMI’s climate tech accelerator, portfolio companies, Blue Frontier, Inc., Transaera, Inc., and mimic are developing innovative solutions to how we stay cool in a hotter, more humid world.  

Everyone deserves access to safe, secure cooling without disproportionately impacting our power grid, straining consumer’s pockets, and increasing warming. The technology exists — now it’s time to scale it.

What do grain straw, biochar concrete, and corn husks have in common? 

They’re all part of a solution to build homes while storing carbon and reducing waste.  

Every year, the United States builds millions of homes, generating up to 80 million tons of embodied CO2, roughly the annual emissions of entire countries like Norway or Austria. Meanwhile, we have over 1.1 billion tons of underutilized biomass from farms, forests, and landfills — material that could be used to build instead of being left to decompose or burned. 

RMI’s report by Chris Magwood, Auri Bukauskas, PhD, Tracy Huynh, PE, and Victor Olgyay, Building with Biomass, shows how we can: 

✅ Support housing affordability, job development, and US manufacturing 
✅ Cut embodied carbon in construction 
✅ Turn biomass into durable, carbon-storing building materials 

Just swapping standard materials for bio-based ones in 25 percent of new homes by 2050 could store 100 million tons of carbon — equal to taking 21 million cars off the road. 

These materials aren’t hypothetical. They’re available, cost-comparable, and ready to scale.

Over the past two decades, governments have supported particular industries to accelerate the clean energy transition: 

📍 Early 2000s: Renewable energy and energy efficiency solutions were seen as helpful but niche technologies to address energy security and environmental concerns. 

📍 After 2008: The rapidly declining costs of renewables, a surge in US oil and gas production that changed the energy security dynamics, and the diplomatic success of the 2015 Paris Agreement elevated climate change to a primary driver of energy policy. 

📍 2020: The global pandemic exposed supply chain vulnerabilities, triggering a new wave of industrial policy designed to reshore manufacturing, diversify sourcing, and safeguard economic competitiveness. 

📍 Now: Electricity demand from data centers, manufacturing, and electrification has created an urgent need for stable, reliable, and increasingly low-carbon power. Solar, wind, and battery storage are the fastest and cheapest to deploy. 

“Green industrial policy” might have started as a way to tackle rising energy prices, air pollution, and climate concerns, but now it is now being reshaped by trade relationships, supply chains, and geopolitics. The next evolution is an industrial strategy that harnesses the potential of advanced energy systems to support the industries needed for growing, security-conscious nations.

The New Energy Industrial Strategy (NEIS) Center is building a global network of researchers, policymakers, and private sector stakeholders who are advancing new energy industrial strategies — policies and interventions that leverage advanced energy systems to support the industries needed for growing, security-conscious nations.

Clean and improved cookstove carbon credits aim to change that by helping households transition to cleaner fuels or more efficient stoves. Since 2004, more than 181 million credits have been issued, but designing and monitoring these projects is complicated. Cooking practices vary widely by region, culture, and season, making it hard to accurately measure impact.
