text:
#COP30 host Brazil is a clean energy powerhouse, powered largely by clean hydro, solar, and wind, and is a leading producer of biofuels.  

But there’s a paradox at play in this ecologically sensitive country.  

Oil and gas production has also surged over the past decade. Overseen by state-owned Petrobras and increasingly regulated by the National Agency for Petroleum (ANP), technological developments have unlocked new reserves. And in the months before COP, Brazil voted to further expand production. 

And rising oil and gas output highlights the importance of reducing related emissions. Brazil’s exceptional data transparency across its oil and gas industry — including details about production practices, field locations, and market activity all open to the public — offers a way to do so. It also provides a template that other countries can implement, using verifiable, open inputs.  

Using ANP’s publicly reported data, RMI’s OCI+ tool finds that although Brazil has one of the lowest average production emissions intensities, these numbers vary greatly among Brazil’s oil and gas assets.  

For example, onshore oil and gas assets have much higher average and wide-ranging production emissions intensities compared to offshore oil developments. With asset-level data from ANP, climate-warming emissions can be better identified and addressed to help build a more transparent, lower-methane oil and gas sector that builds towards a cleaner, more resilient future.  

Learn more about how data transparency, strict regulation, prudent design, and careful operation of oil and gas assets can minimize energy waste, cut emissions, and increase security: https://ow.ly/zrUZ50XroQK

Carbon removal, together with a transition to clean energy, is essential to stabilizing the climate and reducing excess greenhouse gases. It also offers opportunities to clean up waste, restore ecosystems, and create jobs in emerging industries. RMI’s new discussion paper explores how governments can accelerate carbon removal through smart, coordinated market action. 

To stabilize our climate, the world will need to remove around 100 million tons of carbon dioxide each year by 2035 — roughly equal to the annual emissions of Japan’s entire power sector. Achieving that scale will require about $20 billion in annual investment to drive innovation and early deployment. While progress is being made, the industry is not yet on track. 

A government-led advance market commitment (AMC) offers a powerful way to close this gap. By guaranteeing future demand, governments can attract private investment, reduce costs through competition, and set clear standards for a credible global carbon removal market. Similar mechanisms have helped other critical public goods, and the same approach can now accelerate carbon removal. 

The next decade will determine whether carbon removal becomes a cornerstone of climate action or remains a missed opportunity. This paper outlines design options and invites governments, industry, and civil society to work together to build a mechanism that delivers durable climate impact. 

📘 Read more: https://ow.ly/vS0Z50XkuIG

California is leading the transition to electric trucking, and the Bay Area is positioned to be a proving ground for the transition’s success. Meeting the power demand in this region can reduce electricity costs and enhance the competitiveness of electric fleets — but seizing this opportunity will require support from multiple actors.  

Coordination between fleets, utilities, regulators, and charging infrastructure providers can help successfully transition to an electrified freight future. Additionally, state and local governments can accelerate timelines with targeted funding, standardized permitting, and long-range plans that integrate freight electrification into regional land use and transportation strategies. 

RMI’s new regional roadmap highlights where and when infrastructure will be needed and how stakeholders can take action to support different types of electric trucks and electrification scenarios over time: https://ow.ly/R5CM50XktKz

Electricity is getting more and more expensive. Roughly one third of American households report having to forgo food, medicine, or other basic needs to pay their energy bills. 

These increasing prices are driven by aging grid infrastructure, extreme weather and wildfires, fuel price volatility, load growth, and misaligned utility incentives. 

The good news: policy solutions are available to address high energy burdens. These policies have potential benefits beyond cost savings, like increased resilience and security, better air quality and health, and job creation and economic development. 

RMI’s toolkit provides guidance on over two dozen policies that state legislators, their staff, advocates, and others can use to advance electricity affordability at the state level: https://ow.ly/5oCr50XjHRO

RMI is proud to welcome Pat Wood, III, and Paula Glover to our Board of Trustees — two leaders whose careers have shaped the landscape of U.S. energy policy, markets, and innovation. Their combined expertise will help guide RMI’s work to advance a clean, affordable, and reliable energy future in the United States and around the world. 

Pat Wood, III, served as chairman of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) under President George W. Bush and now leads Hunt Energy Network. Throughout his career, he has been a driving force behind competitive power markets, grid modernization, and energy system reliability. Paula Glover, president of the Alliance to Save Energy, has championed energy efficiency and equitable policy solutions that deliver real benefits for communities and the economy. Her leadership has helped secure landmark federal investments in efficiency and climate resilience. 

Together, Wood and Glover bring a deep understanding of how markets, policy, and technology can work in tandem to accelerate the energy transition. Their insight and experience will strengthen RMI’s ability to deliver practical, scalable solutions that meet the moment — supporting a prosperous, secure, and sustainable future for all. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/48QOfWJ

Methane intensity — how much methane is wasted from leaks along the oil and gas supply chain — is a critical consideration for multiple applications. Insurance companies use methane intensity as an underwriting guideline to gauge system safety, and banks and investors are following suit. 

Two main metrics to consider are the gas loss rate — how much methane ends up in the atmosphere and not in the market — and the volumetric methane intensity, which considers the methane emitted based on combined oil and gas throughout the system. Both metrics can be assessed and compared over desired system boundaries.  

A comprehensive look at oil and gas methane intensities requires the use of both metrics to fairly assess complex global petroleum systems and find places to cut down on methane waste sector-wide. https://ow.ly/PUhA50XirPW

State laws promoting advanced transmission technologies (ATTs) could cut customer electricity costs, relieve congestion, and improve grid reliability — but only if they are implemented with care. 

ATTs like grid-enhancing technologies (GETs) and advanced conductors route power efficiently through existing power lines to deliver more electricity through current infrastructure. Utilities and regulators can realize the value of ATTs if they include them throughout generation and transmission planning. 

Passing state ATT laws is a first step. States that lead on implementation can unlock investments that will deliver benefits for consumers and utilities alike: https://ow.ly/26VG50Xinf3

We’re entering one of the fastest-growing periods of electricity demand in over a decade. While data centers get most of the attention, the real story is bigger — electric cars, growing cities, and millions of people getting air conditioners and other appliances for the first time. 

That’s good news for progress, but it also means higher energy bills and more pressure on power grids. RMI’s analysis shows that we need smarter, faster solutions — adding clean energy like solar and batteries, strengthening the grid with alternative transmission technologies, and accelerating efficiency to waste less power in buildings and technology.  

Electricity use will keep rising in the years ahead, and meeting that demand with clean, affordable, and reliable power is one of the biggest challenges — and opportunities — of our time. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/4oxb7z5

Manufacturing processes need a lot of heat to operate. Luckily, existing energy efficient technologies like industrial heat pumps, electric boilers, and thermal energy storage can modernize low- and medium-temperature range operations. At the same time, energy efficiency upgrades, electrification, and advanced geothermal systems can help industrial facilities improve air quality while maintaining or even improving their regional or global competitiveness.  

RMI analyzed how policymakers in Michigan and Illinois can help manufacturers transition to this next generation of equipment as it continues to become more affordable and improve in performance. 

⬇️ Links to each MI and IL's advanced manufacturing roadmap are in the comments. ⬇️

Teamwork makes the dream work. When electric utilities and clean energy project developers engage with communities effectively, they can reduce project risk and ensure targeted benefits get delivered and are tracked over time.  

Thoughtful engagement can help stakeholders understand the community landscape, adjust projects to address concerns, communicate benefits and opportunities, and establish a shared vision of the project outcomes and goals. By orienting to community needs and building partnerships, project developers can increase community trust and support for the project. 

RMI’s resources offer guidance, strategies, and insights for how project developers can work effectively with rural communities: https://bit.ly/475hXWT

Virtual power plants (VPPs) can affordably and reliably integrate distributed energy resources (DERs) like solar panels, smart thermostats, and electric vehicle chargers into the grid to help meet growing electricity demand. 

But to effectively assist the grid, VPPs need appropriate technical requirements and policies. Onerous requirements associated with metering — the measurement of the amount of energy delivered — and telemetry — the ability to collect and automatically transmit real-time data to a grid operator — are major barriers to scaling up VPPs in the US. 

RMI’s latest report evaluates five options for VPPs to communicate performance and cost-effectively support the grid: https://bit.ly/4hp2nJa

Data centers, manufacturing facilities, and electrification are driving up electricity demand. This creates an opportunity: If utilities invest proactively in the grid and strategically allocate costs, growing demand for electricity could deliver cleaner air, new economic development, and lower average electricity costs. 

Electrification can lower costs under the right conditions and with enough preparation. Having cost allocation discussions early in the regulatory cycle can promote fairness and affordability as the grid is modernized. Aligning costs with benefits in cost allocation frameworks can help all families benefit from residential electrification, avoiding uneven results that subsidize customers with outsized electricity demand at the expense of average customers. 

RMI’s Becky Xilu Li and Joseph Daniel share more in Utility Dive: https://bit.ly/42Q9KmW

Batteries are an incredible tool for maintaining grid stability and reliability. Their benefits extend beyond their most well-known use — storing abundant, intermittent electricity so that it is available when demand spikes — to include helping the grid keep voltage, frequency, and power load in check and supporting critical systems during outages. 

Grid operators and utilities value the flexibility of batteries, RMI’s Mark Dyson told Financial Times. "They can be a ‘swiss army knife’ and do whatever is needed on the grid, when and where that is required." 

Learn more about how smart planning and interconnection can help harness the benefits of batteries in the United States: https://bit.ly/4hgxBlE

Across the United States, the transition to affordable, clean energy is taking shape in ways we can all see and feel. Solar panels line the rooftops of schools and libraries. EV charging stations stand ready in grocery store parking lots. Electric buses and service vehicles are rolling out to replace diesel fleets. 

Behind every project is a story, and every story has to start somewhere: a conversation, a calculation, a leap of faith. These projects come to life through the persistence and creativity of individuals and teams determined to build a cleaner future. 

RMI's new ‘Voices of Clean Energy’ shares these stories. Each profile highlights the people turning ideas into action: grant writers, project managers, engineers, community leaders, and more. Each story captures the technical expertise, community ties, and determination that helps our nation’s energy systems progress. 

By elevating these voices, we honor the individuals building a healthier energy landscape — and invite others to join in. 

Hear their stories: https://bit.ly/47iK1WF

Over one million e-bikes have been sold per year in the United States since 2022 — a shift that signals how quickly people are embracing cleaner, more affordable, and more practical ways to get around. 

In a recent CNN feature, Ellen Kennedy, RMI expert on carbon-free transportation, explained why this is a win for Americans’ pocketbooks and air quality. 

“It is 99% more efficient to ride an e-bike than to drive a gas car. They require so little energy. An entire month of e-bike charging will cost you less than one day of running your air conditioner at home.” 

For many households, that means thousands of dollars saved each year on fuel and maintenance — on top of the health and climate benefits. E-bikes are proving that the clean energy transition can deliver immediate benefits for families and communities while cutting carbon and creating a healthier future. 

Watch the full piece: https://cnn.it/4mTLiIb

How can US regions lead the shift to clean, affordable energy? Creativity and efficiency. 

Providing dependable energy from a reliable grid is vital to maintaining resilient, competitive economies. If investment in our future energy needs lags, US businesses and households will face higher costs and reliability risks. 

New strategies are needed to overcome uncertainty driven by policy changes. Economic developers can help communities balance near-term competitiveness with long-term resilience by aligning demand with regional capabilities to compete in the global marketplace. 

Creativity and efficiency are becoming the new operating models for economic and community development in the US, defining which regions will lead in the next chapter of the energy transition: https://bit.ly/46JsV46

We hope you’ll join RMI and Third Derivative at Trellis Impact this year in San Jose, CA, from October 28–30 to exchange insights on carbon accounting, clean energy, industrial decarbonization, next-gen transport, and the innovation pipelines making it all possible.

Net-zero aligned targets are now embedded across the financial system, but progress is hard to see. As climate goals have gained board-level oversight and attention from shareholders and clients, their challenges are mounting — technical, economic, legal, and reputational. Meanwhile, emissions keep rising. 

If the point of commitments and targets was always to see finance flow to the transition, then shouldn’t we ask: What is stopping the transition-enabling transactions from happening? 

In our latest report we offer a hopeful and pragmatic path forward: one where banks are empowered to do what they do best — make deals that move capital toward the transition. 

We believe the following four shifts are essential: 

1️⃣ Recognize banks as commercial actors: Banks have a business model to adhere to, but that doesn’t mean they don’t have a role to play. Understanding that climate-smart investing is just smart investing is only the start. 

2️⃣ Let them lead through what they know: Structuring, advising, deploying capital. That’s how banks can accelerate the transition, not just measure it. 

3️⃣ Build smarter transition intelligence: Metrics that rely on high-level or aggregated data on companies and sectors have not always led to helpful insights, incentives, or market signals. Focusing on the insights that drive financial decisions are key. 

4️⃣ Design systems that work: Climate action is collaborative. Policy, finance, and real-economy actors must work together to remove roadblocks and reward progress. 

By working with, not against, the realities of the financial system, we can unlock the next wave of climate progress. 

📘 Explore the report to learn more: https://bit.ly/3KAzr4D

Latin America is at the center of a global shift to clean energy and climate action. From Uruguay’s switch to nearly 100% renewable electricity to Brazil’s hosting of #COP30 this November, as well as region-wide progress in energy efficiency and sustainable finance, Latin America is a powerful player in clean energy and climate initiatives. 

Across a region home to nearly 700 million people, RMI is committed to supporting regionally driven and equitable action on clean energy and climate. Our work includes: 

🔅 Advancing Brazil’s green iron opportunity, showing how the country can lead the world in low-carbon industrial innovation. 
🔅 Partnering with investors and regulators in the region to align financial portfolios with climate goals and unlock new opportunities. 
🔅 Publishing climate and finance research in Spanish and Portuguese, to expand access and ensure that regional voices are at the center of the transition. 

This Hispanic/Latine Heritage Month, RMI celebrates the strength of collaboration across cultures and languages. We reaffirm our commitment to collaborating closely with Latin American leaders on innovative solutions. 

Explore our Latin America research and insights: 
🔗 Brazil's Green Iron Opportunity: https://bit.ly/3SCeVRK
🔗 Powering Up the Global South: https://bit.ly/3J2YaxT
🔗 Institutional Investors Can Make the Climate Transition Bloom in Latin America: https://bit.ly/3J1BqhI

Each step of the oil and gas production process — from extraction, processing, and transport — needs energy. This means that emissions intensity – the level of emissions per barrel of oil or gas — can vary wildly depending on those processes. We also can’t forget super-heating methane, which can leak at any one (or all) of these steps. 
 
This difference in intensity can have profound impacts on energy security and public safety, but has largely been hidden from markets, regulators, and citizens for decades. 

RMI’s free interactive Oil Climate Index plus Gas (OCI+) tool reveals life-cycle emissions, highlights areas to prevent energy waste, and shares strategies so that industry, policymakers, investors, and researchers can take action to tackle methane leaks. The latest update of OCI+ also includes satellite observation data from Carbon Mapper’s Tanager-1 and other point source satellites to combine detected methane measurements with modeled estimates.  

OCI+ underscores the critical role of targeted, resource-specific strategies to reduce energy waste, cut oil and gas emissions, and make the benefits of reducing emissions more transparent for decision-makers. https://bit.ly/4hhewzT

Do you know what type of refrigerant your HVAC system uses? Do you know how much is in it?  

Managing refrigerant charge is an important aspect of keeping ACs and heat pumps running efficiently. Even small leaks or undercharging can lower performance, driving up bills, straining the grid, and worsening climate pollution. 

The good news is that it's fixable. Our new article explains how 👉 https://bit.ly/3VKjk6R

The western electricity grid is powerful because no state stands alone — utilities continuously adjust supply and demand to route power to where it can serve customers most reliably and affordably.  

As power needs grow due to increased manufacturing, new data centers, and more, regional transmission can help balance dynamic demand on a daily basis. During severe weather, unexpected plant outages, or sudden demand surges, regional transmission can be the difference between the lights staying on and a widespread blackout. 

Investing in regional transmission planning and expansion can help every Western state secure lasting economic growth, affordable electricity, and long-term reliability: https://bit.ly/3VOi0Qk

Shoonya, a national electric mobility campaign in India by NITI Aayog and RMI just turned 4! For four years, Shoonya has been building momentum for zero-pollution transport in our cities to be visible, aspirational, and achievable. Since its start in 2021, Shoonya’s partners have grown from 20 to more than 230, enabling over 1.1 billion clean rides and deliveries across India. 

At a recent Shoonya event where leaders reaffirmed the role of EVs in advancing the state’s net-zero goals, new milestone briefs and studies were released. Panel discussions and working sessions explored strategies to expand consumer awareness, strengthen charging infrastructure, and accelerate zero-emission trucking — laying the groundwork for the next wave of state-led EV action. 

As Shoonya enters its fifth year, the conclave showed how collaboration between government, industry, and communities can make clean mobility accessible and affordable for all.  

Learn more about Shoonya’s journey here:  https://bit.ly/4pUSQxb

At the top of the corporate docket: effectively meeting rising costs for power, dealing with the rise in power demand from data centers, and how to lift US competitiveness in clean industry.  

RMIers led conversations on removing market barriers, such as establishing better carbon accounting standards, as well as technical steps to lower emissions from shipping, aviation, and other industries. 

Here’s what they took away from the whirlwind week👇 

https://bit.ly/3KwsOQI

“Renewables get all the headlines because they’re visible,” says RMI cofounder Amory Lovins. “But energy is invisible, and the energy you don’t use is almost unimaginable.”   

By rethinking how our buildings, vehicles, factories, and data centers are put together, Amory explains, whole-system “integrative design” can boost energy efficiency fivefold while cutting costs and equipment needs. 

From passive homes to lightweight electric cars and retrofitted skyscrapers, proven solutions already show how small design shifts can yield massive savings. The challenge now is making integrative design literacy mainstream.  

Celebrate #EEDay2025 by reading the key takeaways from Amory's recent Stanford talk on integrative design 👉 https://bit.ly/4oiep9x

The clean vehicle tax credit for purchasing or leasing an electric vehicle (EV) ends today, but there are still ways for consumers to cut costs and convert to clean energy. Homeowners can pursue other tax credits to save money on residential clean energy projects and home efficiency upgrades. 

RMI’s Olivia Alves recommends starting with a home energy assessment. “That is really the bread and butter for a lot of these types of retrofits,” she told The Associated Press. From there, families can prioritize other home projects whose tax credits are going to phase out, such as heat pumps, EV chargers, and improvements to windows, doors, and insulation. 

RMI’s Clean Energy Incentive Hub has the latest information on how to use federal clean energy incentives before they expire to maximize cost savings: https://bit.ly/42MU43C

Solving climate challenges at scale requires more than breakthrough tech. It takes cross-sector collaboration and strategy alignment.  

That’s why RMI is proud to be a convening partner with Trellis Group at #TrellisImpact 2025 an annual 3-day convergence of climate tech innovators, corporate leaders, NGOs, service providers, and finance professionals working to accelerate real-world deployment.

🔹 500+ speakers, including 7 RMIers

✈️ Air travel is one of the fastest-growing sources of climate pollution — and making it cleaner is one of the toughest challenges we face. 

New fuels and propulsion technologies like sustainable aviation fuel, hydrogen, and electric aircraft are promising — but without serious planning and infrastructure investment at airports, they won’t scale fast enough. 

RMI, in partnership with POWDR and the #PlayForever Foundation, hosted workshops in Utah and Colorado to help unlock this path forward — identifying what airports need to do now to prepare for this transition, and where coordinated action across the aviation ecosystem can accelerate momentum. 

If you work in aviation, energy, or infrastructure: this is the time to be the early-mover! Cleaner air travel is coming, and the planning starts now. 

More info 👇 
https://bit.ly/3IDdNf9

From choosing raw materials to selecting suppliers, buying teams can shape entire markets. Every purchasing decision can help reduce emissions, support resource efficiency, and encourage responsible practices across supply chains. 

To make this easier, RMI has released a new resource: Accelerating Green Commodity Transformation — Corporate Low-Carbon Procurement How-to Guide, a step-by-step toolkit with strategies, templates, and case studies to help you align internally, engage suppliers, and turn commitments into action. 

This guide breaks down practical steps for low-carbon purchasing and shows how small shifts in procurement can add up to big change across sectors. 

To complement the guide, RMI and Scope 3 Peer Group will host a series of webinars this fall, spotlighting real-world strategies companies can use today to accelerate low-carbon procurement. 

👉 Explore the guide + register for the webinars: https://bit.ly/4nJX3lw

Robert Fortunato's “Green Idea House” — a remodeled 1950s home in California that generates more energy than it uses — exemplifies how thoughtful design and energy-efficient technology can cut costs and pollution. RMI’s Chris Magwood weighs in on what it takes to build more efficient and sustainable homes and why anyone tackling a renovation should have a firm plan with clear goals to accomplish. 

👉 https://ow.ly/g7Jk50X3gWa

Clean energy can deliver economic growth, make companies more cost-competitive, and cut consumers’ risks of surprise spikes in energy prices. Motivated by these and other benefits of advanced energy systems, communities and states across America, as well as most countries globally, are speeding the shift.  

And as homeowners, cities, and countries reckon with rising costs due to damage amplified by climate change — such as homes lost to wildfire and crops lost to drought  — cutting emissions remains a top priority, along with improving energy accessibility, reliability, and affordability.  

RMI analysis highlights the market dynamics, politics, and policy driving the shift to clean energy: https://bit.ly/46z1cSk

Many worthwhile energy projects around the world are slowed by financial obstacles. Barriers can include high up-front capital costs to scale new technology, uncertain revenue streams for first-of-a-kind projects, political and currency risks in emerging markets, or simply returns that are too low or long-term for purely commercial investors.  

The United States — in partnership with allies and multilateral banks — can support projects to unlock private capital in a way that prioritizes strategic geographies and technologies, diversifies supply chains, and expands markets for US companies. By doing so, the US can not only advance national security, but it can also strengthen strategic supply chains for essential raw materials and help mobilize private investment into critical clean energy projects — especially in emerging markets and frontier technologies. 

https://bit.ly/4mvBlk4

The improved affordability and accessibility of advanced energy solutions are already transforming lives around the world.  

"Eighty percent of the world is still very much moving on the fast track toward clean energy, because it makes economic sense, because it makes sense from a resiliency perspective, because it makes sense from an employment and a job perspective. And ultimately it makes sense from an equity and inclusion perspective," RMI CEO Jon Creyts shared during the TIME100 Climate Leadership Forum at #ClimateWeekNYC.  

At RMI, we’re working with other organizations, business leaders, and decision makers to showcase the market-based solutions that are driving the world’s shift to cleaner, more resilient, and more affordable energy options: https://bit.ly/46zQE5h

"[Companies] want to have a share in producing clean energy technologies, selling them to the world," and to profit from "the single largest capital formation in the history of the world."   — Sarah Ladislaw, Managing Director, U.S. Program, RMI 

In conversation at #ClimateWeekNYC with Axel Threlfall of Reuters and Drew Bond of C3 Solutions, Sarah spotlighted the geopolitical and economic urgency of the clean energy transition — and the private sector's pivotal role in shaping it. 

🟢 Clean energy isn’t just a climate solution — it’s a global economic transformation. 
🟢 Now is the moment for companies to invest, lead, and build the future they want to operate in. 

🔗 For a deeper dive into the U.S. role in the global clean energy race, explore RMI’s new energy statecraft playbook: https://bit.ly/4mvBlk4

🎥 Watch the full session via #Reuters: https://bit.ly/3WaGvak

Nations continue to miss their commitments to cut global warming pollution. But, as countries slow down, big companies are speeding up — they’re cutting emissions, saving energy, and lowering costs. RMI is helping the private sector move faster by strengthening markets for sustainable products through aggregated demand, collective action, advocacy, and clean energy projects.  

RMI partners directly with companies to co-develop strategies and solutions, including how to decarbonize supply chains, design infrastructure, and optimize the clean energy grid. Through these collaborations, we help businesses accelerate innovation and tap into opportunities for clean solutions. 

We serve as implementers, conveners, advisors, and collaborators to help companies deliver and scale market-driven solutions across the energy system. Learn more about how we work with companies to move ambition to action: https://bit.ly/42LvYpP

#UNGA80 #UN80 #UnitedNations

Businesses trying to reduce their scope 3 emissions need to buy cleaner fuels and products such as steel, cement, aluminum, plastics, and fertilizers. But production of these clean inputs is only just getting started, meaning supply is limited and buying options are scarce. 

Corporate buyers and procurement teams hold the key to changing that. By showing a willingness to pay for cleaner materials and signing longer-term contracts, companies can give suppliers the confidence and financing they need to scale up production.  

Our new report, launched today at #GreenMarketsDay25 at #ClimateWeekNYC, breaks down the tools companies can use to accelerate this transition and illustrates how voluntary buyer action has helped scale other solutions such as CDR technology and sustainable aviation fuel. 

Here’s what RMI’s Josh Henretig had to say:  

"Acting early isn’t just about cutting emissions, it’s a strategic investment. First movers strengthen their brands, secure scarce supply, and help shape the future markets." 

For more, read our report, Catalytic Procurement: How Corporations Can Lead the Green Commodity Transition 👉 https://bit.ly/4nJp4JR

Center for Green Market Activation (GMA), Lowercarbon Capital, Smart Freight Centre

Improving efficiency reduces waste, costs, and pollution while still allowing us to meet our needs.  

Delivering fully on the promise of energy efficiency means creating not just better products, buildings, and factories, but upgrading broader systems such as cities, industrial hubs, and global supply chains. Part of that shift will include upgrading from fossil fuel technologies to advanced energy and electrification solutions, which are often two to four times more efficient.  

Coupled with a rapid transition to advanced energy, this holistic approach — evaluating not just a single system but how multiple systems can work better in harmony  — can make the transition faster, more affordable, and more widely beneficial. https://bit.ly/4gBaZvT

Construction on the Empire State Building, one of the world’s first supertall buildings, began in 1930. Nearly a century later, the architectural triumph is pushing the frontiers of energy efficiency.  

Empire State Realty Trust and RMI were able to reduce the Empire State Building’s energy consumption by 50% thanks to deep retrofits in the late 2000s. As part of the major upgrade, some 6,500 windows were remanufactured on site, to let in light but reflect heat. By cutting the building’s heating and cooling needs, the improvements helped net $17 million in savings. 

The Empire State Buildings savings offer other cities and states a model to cut waste and pollution in their own buildings: https://bit.ly/3Iel8BY

RMI’s Energy Transition Academy (ETA) has launched its Global Women in Clean Energy (GWiCE) Fellowship, a program designed to strengthen women practitioners' technical and leadership capacity, and to catalyze a more inclusive and equitable clean energy workforce in their regions. 

With cohorts in Africa, and soon Indonesia, the GWiCE Fellowship combines technical and leadership training, mentorship, and networking to prepare women to lead the deployment of clean energy solutions including minigrids and hydropower. Alongside technical training, GWiCE fellows learn from peers and women leaders and conclude the five-month program with an in-person workshop focused on leadership skills development, including strategic thinking and stakeholder engagement. 

On the eve of New York Climate Week, this work highlights the critical role of women’s leadership in shaping a resilient, sustainable future. It also reminds us that energy access is not merely a cornerstone of a sustainable energy future, it is a catalyst for social progress and inclusive economic development. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/41ZXDTO

Carbon 👏 credits 👏 are👏 about👏 more👏 than👏 just👏 carbon! 

Yes, you heard us right. At their core, carbon credit projects operate within dynamic social and ecological systems. So, why stop at measuring only emissions? While carbon data shows how much greenhouse gases were avoided, reduced, or removed, the full story lies in the socio-environmental (SE) data — covering human rights, community benefits, biodiversity, and more.  

Back in July, we launched the Carbon Crediting Data Framework (CCDF) to help the voluntary carbon market organize, assess, and compare data on carbon credits. During its development, we talked with developers, standards bodies, ratings agencies, and other experts. They all agree: SE data is just as critical in decision making as carbon. 

That’s why we created a specialized SE toolkit within the CCDF. It gives voluntary carbon market actors the structure and best practices they need to credibly measure and report these broader impacts to demonstrate real benefits and even capture market premiums.  

Read our recent article to learn how pairing emissions data (how much carbon was avoided, reduced, or removed) with SE data (how it impacts people and ecosystems), can help projects achieve greater impact and long-term success. 

🔗 https://lnkd.in/g6rnrt6x

Cement and concrete are responsible for ~8 percent of global carbon emissions, yet low-carbon alternatives remain scarce and not available at scale. Why? It’s a classic chicken-and-egg problem: buyers can’t access clean cement, and producers won’t invest in it without clear demand signals.  

Today, RMI and the Center for Green Market Activation (GMA) are launching the Sustainable Concrete Buyers Alliance (or SCoBA) to help break this cycle.  

In the words of our CEO Jon Creyts, “By aggregating demand from major buyers and establishing rigorous standards, we're creating the market conditions for breakthrough low-carbon cement innovations to scale.”  

This approach builds on the success of RMI and GMA’s buyer alliances in aviation, steel, and trucking — proving that collective demand can unlock clean solutions in even the hardest-to-abate sectors. 

👉 Read the press release to learn more: https://bit.ly/3IxcApI

In July 2025, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) was signed into law. While the megabill relieves some tax uncertainty, it also raises new questions for businesses looking to build in the United States. 

Here, we break down the bill’s key provisions that impact the advanced energy manufacturing sector and highlight challenges and opportunities ahead for policymakers and the private sector. https://bit.ly/46bk2js

Global electric vehicle (EV) adoption is accelerating, with sales up nearly 500% worldwide since 2020. US sales are expected to add another 2.5 million EVs between 2025 and 2030. The success of the transition will depend on whether drivers and fleets can access reliable charging infrastructure. 

For small customers like multifamily housing residents, local businesses, and delivery fleets, connecting new charging stations to the grid can be costly and complicated. Delays, surprise upgrade costs, and unclear processes too often cause projects to stall — leaving many potential EV adopters behind. 

That’s why RMI partnered with EPRI, Interstate Renewable Energy Council (IREC), the Alliance for Transportation Electrification, and Clean Cities and Communities to create a roadmap to make grid connections faster and more accessible for these small EV customers. The roadmap lays out practical solutions; clarifies the roles of utilities, cities, and regulators; and shows how collaboration can reduce costs and expand access to electric transportation. 

The EV future is already here. Let’s make sure it’s one that everyone can plug into. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/46kGpBL

🔋 Batteries are the workhorse of an affordable, reliable electricity grid. They can pump power into the grid when it needs it most, balance it when there are huge swings in demand, and help jumpstart the system if it falters. 

Texas has helped monetize the services batteries provide to systems, which has grown the market and made it competitive, lowering costs for everyone. These huge battery additions have saved customers millions and helped avoid serious reliability issues in extreme weather.  

Batteries can also store energy when costs are low and make it available when demand is high. This is happening in Texas and in California, where CAISO has shifted to hourly assessments to determine if there is enough generation and resources to meet the electricity demand. This has increased competitiveness and incentivized a surge of flexible and longer-duration battery storage deployment.

Learn how US grid planners can take action to capitalize on the reliability and affordability benefits of batteries: https://bit.ly/3I5SIdf

Kotzebue, Alaska, sits above the Arctic Circle, surrounded by a frozen sea for much of the year. No roads lead there, with only planes providing year-round access (or dog sleds and snowmachines for the more intrepid). Residents have long relied on the town’s diesel power plant for electricity, with fuel shipped in annually.  

Making electricity this way comes at a cost: Kotzebue’s leaders have to accept whatever fuel prices the market dictates at the time, a variable over which they have no control. They also must wait until the sea ice thaws for any hope of resupply.  

A hard look at cost and reliability made them seek an alternative path: embracing technologies such as wind, solar, and batteries to create a more resilient and affordable energy system.  

In the final article of our Energy Leadership Accelerator series, we explore how communities like Kotzebue are achieving energy independence and economic resilience through bold, place-based innovation.  

At the intersection of climate adaptation and energy sovereignty, modern energy infrastructure and new technologies are reducing costs, stabilizing local economies, and giving communities control over their future. 

Read the full article: https://bit.ly/4nv1eBz

🌐 From cooperative-led utilities in Alaska to solar installations throughout the Caribbean, remote communities are solving complex energy challenges with resourcefulness and innovation. Across these distant geographies runs a common thread — shared constraints, shared opportunities, and now, shared connection. 

The next article from our Energy Leadership Accelerator (ELA) explores the commonalities between isolated and islanded regions, ranging from fuel insecurity and infrastructure gaps to leadership strategies and climate impacts. It draws on conversations with fellows and energy professionals who met in Alaska earlier this year to exchange insights and solutions for building resilient and innovative energy systems. 

The ELA program was designed around a simple hypothesis: that isolated and islanded communities, even at opposite ends of the map, can accelerate their energy transitions by learning from one another. That sense of connection is now one of the most powerful outcomes of the program. 

📝 Learn more: https://bit.ly/4mfuqv7

CFAN was created to help countries accelerate access to the finance needed for climate solutions and resilience. Recent project successes announced at #COP30 show what that looks like in practice 👇

This initiative supports high-risk, high-impact climate projects through an incubation ➡️ acceleration ➡️ amplification model, driving innovation where it’s needed most.

The USD5 million project, announced at #COP30 during the Adaptation Fund's “Strengthening Adaptation through Indigenous Peoples and Traditional Knowledge” event, will provide support across 14 Pacific countries. The project moved from project proposal to approval in just five months earlier this year – a remarkable example of regional collaboration and effective mobilization that will help scale solutions across the Pacific.

“Unlocking finance requires planning," Kunal notes, adding that, “Being based at SPC, I get the unique opportunity to unlock and access fit-for-purpose financing for vulnerable communities. There’s no one-size-fits-all path, but with the right partnerships and planning, Pacific SIDS can fully tap appropriate sources of finance.”

✨ Last week, members of CFAN’s Pacific Regional Team, the Pacific Community-SPC, and GGGI Pacific brought together officials from eight government ministries and representatives from Fiji’s private sector. Over the following two days, they participated in a Climate Finance Capacity-Building Training, purpose-built to deepen trust and collaboration while accelerating #climatefinance flows. 

The CFAN Advisor to SPC who spearheaded the training, Kunal Singh, shares that, “[This] training created a shared space for government and private sector actors to explore how they can jointly drive transformational climate action, from policy to investment. Participants gained practical skills on developing investment-ready and bankable projects, integrating fit-for-purpose data into decision-making, and aligning national efforts with regional and global climate finance priorities.” 

Dr. Sivendra Michael opened the workshop saying, "Climate finance cannot remain a theoretical dialogue confined to ministries or donor corridors; it must become the architecture upon which our entire development model is built.”

As Kunal notes, the workshop “underscores the growing recognition that effective climate action requires partnership, across institutions, sectors, and systems - to mobilise finance where it matters most. Together, we’re building the enabling conditions for a more resilient, climate-ready Fiji.” 

We’re honored that our CFAN Pacific Team, Jale Samuwai, Ph.D., Aminisitai Delaisainiai and Botho M. were able to provide support for this meaningful convening, and deeply appreciative of our regional partners who made it possible. 

Vinaka vakalevu to all the participants and coordinators, and here’s to a resilient, thriving Pacific!  💚 🙏

Today we’re highlighting work from our CFAN Advisor to Vanuatu, Andres Toro Runruil! 🇻🇺

Andres has been working to accelerate climate finance flows in Vanuatu for the past three years, and this story, shared by our long-time Pacific implementing partner GGGI Pacific, offers insight into what this work looks like:

In early October, Andres travelled with a team across Motolava and Vanualava Islands to engage with local communities and understand their emergency alert systems as part of the Adaptation Fund Concept Note aimed at enhancing Vanuatu’s Early Warning Systems.

Community members shared that use of radio is phasing out due to limitations in equipment and signal access, with many shifting their primary communication channels to SMS and Facebook. This is not without challenges, however, with people reporting walking 20 minutes just to find a signal in some areas.

The insights gleaned from these consultations indicate that communities are eager for stronger connectivity, including satellite solutions – and that a dependable power supply is critical to ensure network reliability. These insights and others will inform how the concept note is refined and adapted, ensuring that proposed interventions are grounded in local needs and priorities and that the technology is both sound and resilient.

We extend our appreciation to the communities of Motolava and Vanualava, the GGGI Pacific team, the Secretariat of the Pacific Regional Environment Programme (SPREP), and to Andres and all our Pacific advisor cohort for the meaningful work they do 🙏

Let’s build a climate for education 💪 

CFAN has partnered with Save the Children Australia under the Climate-Smart Education Systems Initiative. This work helps Ministries of Education in Caribbean Small Island Developing States (#SIDs) tap into finance and build resilient education systems. 

Education remains one of the most underfunded sectors in climate finance — with only 0.17% of projects from major funds between 2006 and 2023 prioritizing it. By building capacity to navigate complex funding processes, this initiative gives ministries the tools to secure resources, improve infrastructure, and ensure continuity of learning for all children. 

These efforts are part of CFAN’s expanded training model — flexible, tailored, and designed to match partners’ priorities. Whether it’s for government, civil society, or youth groups, CFAN helps build the technical and institutional skills needed to develop strong proposals and access financing.

For decades, utility-led energy efficiency programs have helped Americans upgrade their homes and lower their utility bills. But states are starting to realize that focusing on weatherization and electrification can provide more bang for their buck than incentivizing gas furnaces and water heaters. 

New York, Massachusetts, Maine, California, Connecticut, and Washington DC have already begun evolving their energy efficiency programs to reflect this. But in many states — even those that have committed to reducing emissions — these programs are still missing opportunities to help customers adopt newer electric technologies that deliver greater energy and pollution savings.  

In a new article, RMI's Olivia Prieto, Joe Dammel, and Mike Henchen map this trend and outline six reforms to help states realign their energy efficiency programs with today’s priorities. 

🔗 https://lnkd.in/gjrgyJ4n

💵 More climate finance is coming to Africa! 💵

Building on its success in securing millions in climate finance for Pacific and Caribbean nations since 2021, the Climate Finance Access Network (CFAN) has officially announced the launch of its first Africa cohort, supported by the United States Department of State.
 
Coordinated by RMI, CFAN enhances the capacity to unlock climate finance in Small Island Developing States (SIDS) and Least Developed Countries by recruiting and training local climate finance advisors to work within governments and local organizations. Currently active in 20 countries across the Pacific and Caribbean, CFAN has made significant progress.  
 
CFAN's current impact by the numbers: 
🌏 20 countries and three cohorts 
💰 $76.6 million in climate finance secured 
💸 $1.4 billion in the global project pipeline 

The three African countries where CFAN will be starting its cohort, the Democratic Republic of Congo, the Republic of Congo, and Cameroon, are just the beginning. 🌱 The goal is to scale the success of CFAN throughout the continent while tailoring its approach to country-specific needs and priorities. Notably, these nations are situated in the Congo Basin, the world’s largest carbon sink, which absorbs approximately 4% of annual global emissions, underscoring the region’s critical role in global climate mitigation. 

This expansion marks a pivotal step in equipping vulnerable nations with the tools and expertise needed to access critical climate finance, empowering them to safeguard their communities and contribute to global climate resilience. 🛠️

By 2030, Delhi is set to become the world’s largest metropolitan area, driving a 50% rise in peak electricity demand. With renewable energy poised to meet half of the city’s power needs, the focus must shift to grid flexibility — ensuring a stable, reliable power supply amid rapid demand surges. 

RMI’s latest report outlines key solutions to tackle these challenges, including: 
⚡ Demand Response: Shift electricity usage during peak hours to reduce stress on the grid. 
💡 Managed EV Charging: Optimize electric vehicle and bus charging to flatten demand curves. 
🔌 Battery Energy Storage Systems: Enable peak shaving, load shifting, and better integration of renewables. 
🏭 Virtual Power Plants: Aggregate and optimize distributed energy resources for efficient grid management. 

The road ahead includes regulatory pilots, scaling up programs, and cost-effective frameworks to unlock these benefits. Together, policymakers, businesses, and communities can set a global benchmark for urban energy solutions. 

Download the report and learn more: https://bit.ly/4igaOqd

Heat pumps can do it all: serving as air conditioners on hot summer days and warming our homes on cold winter nights, all while saving energy and improving air quality. This versatility makes them an increasingly popular green choice. 

In heating mode, air-source heat pumps use refrigerants with high boiling points to absorb heat from the air, even when it’s cold out. The heat pump concentrates this warmth and passes it indoors to warm your home.

And because a heat pump moves heat instead of generating it – as does a conventional furnace – it can deliver several times more heat energy than it consumes in electricity. This requires much less energy and means lower operating costs than older-style electric resistance heaters. And compared with oil and gas furnaces, heat pumps generate far less harmful air pollution and greenhouse gases.     

Heat pumps have been a hit in milder climates for a long time, and advancements are making them more and more popular in colder climes, too. Learn more about how switching to a heat pump can be a big win for household energy savings, lower health risks, and increased comfort — all while improving the climate: https://bit.ly/3Zsmb5A

The aviation industry is responsible for nearly 3% of global CO2 emissions, roughly on par with Japan, the fifth highest-emitting country. And this past Sunday set a record for the busiest day ever for commercial air travel in America, only increasing our carbon output. To get these emissions down, the industry is developing new technologies to help decarbonize aviation and create a more sustainable future. 

Sustainable aviation fuels and other novel propulsion technologies, such as hydrogen, electric, and hybrid aircraft are essential to decarbonizing the industry, but to reach net-zero by 2050, airports need to start planning now. 

Airport infrastructure plays an important role in decarbonization of the aviation industry. Airports must plan for and implement new airport infrastructure that allows for these new technologies to thrive. To do so, they’re going to have to work closely with a variety of stakeholders to make it happen.

Rising demand is driving the growth of “fast fashion” — low-cost clothing often worn only once — made from polyester. Polyester is a synthetic fiber, or plastic, derived from petroleum-based chemicals that constantly shed microplastics. Most polyester fibers aren't biodegradable. Each year, the number of flights grows, and in turn the amount of CO2 emitted increases. However, sustainable aviation fuel, or SAF, could be a fix for the aviation industry’s carbon emissions problem.

“Sustainable aviation fuel” is an umbrella term for liquid fuels that are made without crude oil. SAF can be produced from corn ethanol, agricultural scraps, or synthetic “e-fuels" derived from green hydrogen and recycled CO2. Although not a perfect solution, SAF is essential to decarbonize flying in the near term and can help create a greener aviation industry. 🌱 ✈️ 

“It’s important for consumers to understand that we are charting a flight path, if you will, toward a zero-carbon aviation system,” says RMI’s Mark Porter. “But that’s not going to happen today, and it won’t be tomorrow. It’s going to take a while to get there.”

When organic waste – like food scraps, yard clippings, and cardboard – breaks down in landfills and dumpsites around the world, it creates methane, a climate super pollutant with over 80 times the near-term warming power of carbon dioxide. 

Thankfully, there are proven, cost-effective strategies to reduce and recycle organic waste and strengthen emission controls at disposal sites that could curb nearly 90 percent of methane emissions from solid waste.  

More than 30 countries at #COP29 signed on to set specific targets to reduce waste sector methane and develop new policies and programs to achieve those targets. Focusing on the waste sector is essential to achieve the Global Methane Pledge’s goal to cut methane by 30% below 2020 levels, by 2030, limit near-term temperature rise, and avoid dangerous climate tipping points. Quick action means benefits today: since methane is released alongside other harmful pollutants, addressing methane from organic waste will also protect public health, improve air quality, and advance environmental justice. https://bit.ly/3V4HvNm

Food waste is the single most landfilled material and causes nearly 60% of landfill methane emissions, the EPA reports. Methane, a powerful greenhouse gas, is generated when organic materials — such as food waste, yard waste, and paper — decompose in landfills without oxygen. As a result, landfills are the third largest source of methane in the United States with annual emissions equivalent to driving 66 million cars or operating 79 coal-fired power plants for an entire year. 

Stakeholders at every level can help cut methane emissions by preventing organic materials from ending up in landfills. Such "upstream strategies" include minimizing waste, donating excess food, and recycling residual organics into animal feed, compost, or biogas. 

"Downstream strategies" focus on improving landfill design, operations, and monitoring practices to increase methane capture and reduce methane leaks. Combining upstream and downstream efforts could slash waste methane emissions by 15% by 2030, according to the Center for Global Sustainability at the University of Maryland. 

Beyond the climate benefits, cutting methane emissions from waste can help address food insecurity, reduce health risks, improve the quality of life for nearby communities, create jobs, and produce valuable products, such as compost. 

Learn more: https://bit.ly/3Zb9tIv

As traditional fossil fuel power plants approach retirement at the same time electricity demand is expected to grow, US regulators who are tasked with ensuring the affordability and reliability of the electric grid are focusing on resource adequacy — the ability of the grid to supply enough electricity to meet demand under a range of possible future conditions. 

Risks to resource adequacy in the Western United States are primarily driven by demand growth uncertainties, extreme weather and climate change, delays in the construction of planned power generating resources, and slow transmission expansion.  

By improving planning practices to reflect current drivers of risk, considering more types of resource adequacy solutions, and seeking reforms in utility business models, regulators can reduce the risk of future resource adequacy shortfalls while continuing to transition to a low-carbon grid that benefits customers and enables local economic development.

A pioneering financial deal in Chile demonstrates how innovative lending mechanisms can speed the retirement of coal assets and support affected communities. The $125-million partnership between IDB Invest and Engie Energia Chile has established a model financial mechanism that links interest rate reductions to early coal plant retirement while also funding renewable development. 

The structure's efficacy has been validated through real results. Multiple generating units at the Tocopilla thermal facility have ceased operations ahead of their scheduled retirement date, while a 151-megawatt wind generation facility in Calama progresses toward commissioning.  

This successful implementation can be attributed to several critical enabling conditions: Chile's comprehensive policy framework supporting coal phase-out initiatives; definitive demand signals from industrial electricity consumers; strategic deployment of blended finance by development institutions; and the increasingly favorable economics of renewable energy generation. 

Chile’s progress extends beyond this deal. In 2019, they decided to phase out coal. At the time, the South American country had 28 coal-fired power plants. At #COP29 on Friday, Chilean Minister of Environment Maisa Rojas provided a status update on a panel moderated by RMI’s Brian O’Hanlon and hosted by Powering Past Coal Alliance (PPCA): Chile has closed 11 coal-fired power plants and there are 9 more available for closing or retrofitting by 2026.

To decarbonize the steel sector, reducing emissions from making iron is essential. And green hydrogen provides a viable pathway to achieve near-zero emissions in the ironmaking process.  

Globally, steel supply chains are dependent on key regions that supply iron ore. That’s why iron and steel are usually produced in the same integrated facilities, near access to cheap coal.  

When iron ore reserves are co-located with renewable energy resources, a higher value green hydrogen-based iron product can be produced. This presents the opportunity to physically separate the making of steel from the production of iron to establish Green Iron Corridors. The production of green iron can instead take place in areas with ample renewables, to make clean hydrogen. Transporting that green iron to existing steel centers can help steelmaking remain where demand is strong. 

At #COP29 in Baku, Azerbaijan, RMI brought together representatives from various geographies across the supply chain — iron ore miners, renewable energy, and hydrogen developers, financiers, and government officials — to explore the emerging production and trade dynamics for Green Iron Corridors and identify action items to make them a reality.  

Learn more about green iron corridors: https://bit.ly/3CvptgP

Katalist, a 'book and claim' registry launched by the Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller Center for Zero Carbon Shipping and RMI, aims to accelerate decarbonization in the shipping industry.  

The maritime sector sits at the center of the global economy – more than 80% of world trade travels by sea – and is responsible for around 3% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Many shipping companies are taking action to reduce their emissions, but it can be challenging to find customers willing to pay for the higher cost of clean shipping fuels.  

Katalist helps bridge this gap by separating the low-emissions product from its sustainability benefits and transferring them to another party through a registry, enabling customers to claim the environmental benefits of low-emissions transport without physically receiving it, while stimulating overall use of clean fuels.  

Freight customers willing to pay a green premium for low-emission transport services can reliably use Katalist to achieve their decarbonization goals. And ship operators using low-emission fuels can use it to access a broader market of freight customers willing to pay an environmental supplement. The registry will help drive investments in lower-emission fuels, boosting their cost-competitiveness and commercial availability. 

Learn more about how #Katalist is #FuelingChange and facilitating the decarbonization of the maritime industry: www.katalist.eco 

An extended thank you to Energy Web for supporting the development of this registry.

Carbon is a necessary raw material for chemical industry products, which means the sector can’t fully decarbonize. But changing where CO2 is sourced from and how it is used has the potential to reduce emissions from chemical manufacturing processes. Carbon is currently sourced mainly from fossil fuels, so the industry must focus on "defossilization" and reducing emissions to mitigate its climate impact. 

One defossilization pathway is carbon utilization, a process in which carbon dioxide is captured from industrial or atmospheric sources to be reused as a feedstock. Although this process is energy intensive, it can help produce high value chemicals and fuels with a much lower carbon intensity compared to conventional fossil feedstock routes. By 2050, CO2 utilization along with other solutions could result in an 89% reduction in the amount of carbon derived from fossil fuels for use in chemicals. 

Learn more about CO2 utilization and how it can be paired with other emissions reduction solutions in the chemical manufacturing industry:

Hydrogen is the smallest element, but it plays a big role in the chemical industry. In the United States, 10 million metric tons of hydrogen are produced each year, with over 90 percent of it used as a feedstock for chemical products. 

Because most hydrogen today is derived from natural gas, hydrogen production accounts for almost half of the chemical sector emissions in the United States. Thankfully, there are cleaner, lower emissions pathways to producing hydrogen, such as using renewable energy to convert water into hydrogen. To further reduce emissions in the chemical sector, cleaner hydrogen can also be used for heating as a replacement for natural gas. 

Due to its wide role in the chemicals industry, reducing emissions from hydrogen production would have a huge impact on emissions from the industry overall. However, making the change to cleaner hydrogen for heating should be weighed against other decarbonization opportunities, such as direct electrification and enhanced energy efficiency.  

Learn more about how hydrogen produced through lower emissions routes can reduce greenhouse gas emissions from the chemicals sector:

Manufacturing the chemicals that form the building blocks of many of our everyday items requires high temperatures. Thermal batteries can not only meet those heating needs, but they can also help plants achieve significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, improve energy efficiency, and potentially lower energy costs.

Thermal batteries are made with materials that have a high capacity to absorb and store heat. They are heated to temperatures up to 1800 degrees Celsius with electricity, and then can release the heat as needed to industrial plants for uses such as heating for reactions, distillation, and drying. Thermal batteries are made of a proven technology that can be integrated into existing plants to maximize both economic and environmental benefits.

Learn more about how thermal batteries can serve as an alternative for industrial heating:

• Pair solar installation with electrification 

• Align with community priorities

Virtual power plants (VPPs) aggregate smart thermostats and appliances, electric vehicles, and distributed energy resources of households and businesses to provide many of the same services as a traditional power plant by coordinating the charging, discharging, and management of all the connected devices. 

VPPs reduce power sector emissions by shifting demand to be met by lower-emissions energy resources. In 2024, VPPs across the country could shift demand to avoid up to 7.3 million tons of carbon dioxide emissions. By 2035, VPPs could avoid 12 to 28 million tons, or two to four percent of projected US power sector carbon emissions. 

However, few regions have fully integrated VPPs into grid planning and operations. Grid decision makers need to evaluate, plan for, and realize the full value of VPPs, including avoided transmission and distribution costs.  

Learn more about the key conditions for fully realizing VPPs’ grid benefits: https://bit.ly/4hcYk21

Slashing methane emissions is among the fastest – and cheapest – ways to slow the pace of global warming in this decisive decade. But first, we have to find where methane is being released.

New satellites and monitoring tools and technologies from RMI Environmental Defense Fund, Carbon Mapper, and more are making it clearer than ever where methane is being released worldwide. With this improved transparency, decision-makers can target their methane reduction strategies to the places where they are needed most.

“We've finally gotten to the space where we understand fundamentally that things that are invisible can harm us -- and it held us back for a really long time. That's where I think the satellite data is going to be absolutely transformational, because methane isn’t invisible anymore. [...] That's really going to open up minds and doors and decisions all over -- financial, actors, governments, industry, all over the place,” said RMI’s Deborah Gordon during our #ClimateWeekNYC event today on efforts to reduce methane from the oil and gas sector. 
 

RMI’s Oil Climate Index plus Gas (OCI+) tool, supported by Bloomberg Philanthropies, estimates methane leakage based on the amount of oil and gas produced for oil and gas basins globally. https://bit.ly/3MYW9RW

When Hurricane Francine cut off power to almost half a million customers in New Orleans last September, First Grace United Methodist Church still had lights, water, and air conditioning thanks to its solar-plus-battery-storage microgrid. 

First Grace is part of a network of Community Lighthouses born out of the experience New Orleans residents faced after Hurricanes Katrina and Ian. The Community Lighthouses are 10 community buildings, with more planned for the future, powered by solar and batteries and designed to provide essentials like power, air conditioning, water, meals, a community gathering space, and more when storms hit.  

Solar-plus-battery-storage microgrids are typically the first step to building a resilience hub as they provide reliable power when the grid goes out. They also benefit communities when skies are clear, while simultaneously supporting local businesses.

Building a microgrid is more appealing and doable than ever before, with solar and battery costs declining and other support available. To help communities overcome microgrid implementation challenges, RMI led a "Microgrids for Resilient Communities" cohort where members and RMI learned from each other’s direct experiences.

From financing to policy to local community buy-in, ecosystem support is key to a successful clean industrial hub. Securing the social license for development is particularly critical to set up a project for success. Effective stakeholder management is critical for keeping projects on track to reach final investment decisions and begin construction. Without community support and buy-in, first-of-a-kind or later generation decarbonization projects often face delays or cancellations during the planning and permitting. In short, missing holistic support— covering policy, finance, research and development, and community engagement — the whole timeline for achieving decarbonization goals can fall apart. But there’s a fix: proactively identifying, analyzing, and engaging with key stakeholders, and RMI’s stakeholder analysis and mapping tool, S.A.M., makes that possible. 

S.A.M. is a digital tool that creates clear visuals of how different stakeholders interact, and can help developers understand the realities and local power dynamics on the ground. This way, they can learn about concerns and objections before starting a long, expensive permitting process. Identifying and understanding the connections enables better outreach and engagement strategies to derisk project deployment.   

Beyond mapping project stakeholders, S.A.M. is incredibly versatile, with use applications for in grid planning and electricity markets, supply chain visualization, partnership management and research in fields like finance, energy, public health, sustainability, urban planning, and more. This makes it a valuable tool not only for clean industrial hubs, but for holistic ecosystem support across industries.

Access to clean, safe, affordable energy is life changing yet remains a challenge around the world. To mark Black History Month, RMI explores how helping communities shift to lower-cost, clean energy can save money, build business, and improve quality of life for Black communities in the United States.    

In 27 cities stretching from Honolulu to Boston, RMI organized cohorts to make rooftop solar affordable and accessible for lower- and moderate-income (LMI) communities. In total, these Solarize campaigns helped nearly 1,200 households install more than 10 megawatts of residential solar, thanks to close collaboration between governments, community-based organizations, and others. 

The solar panels are already delivering real saving. And over their 25-year lifespan, the panels will avoid $8 million in electricity bills and reduce CO2 emissions by 106,000 tons, equivalent to planting over 1.76 million trees. Overall, 40 percent of the households that purchased solar systems through RMI’s Solarize cohort programs were in low- to middle-income communities. 

RMI also developed an Electrify cohort to help 12 teams — representing 20 local governments and their 30 partner organizations — develop and launch programs to help their community members electrify their homes, focusing on heat pumps. Across the country, these Electrify programs are projected to result in over 3,000 heat pump installations, which will help lower heating and cooling costs and improve air quality for households. A major part of these programs’ success was ensuring that community-based organizations were involved to ensure that resident voices were part of program design. 

RMI’s Solarize and Electrify cohorts have made cleantech available to more Americans, saving them money on their energy bills while also creating business opportunities for local contractors.

Clean Industrial Hubs can play a critical role in decarbonizing the heavy industry and transport sectors and can spur economic growth, create employment opportunities, and unlock new technologies. But what exactly are they? 

Clean Industrial Hubs bring together project developers, policymakers, financial institutions, and community-based organizations to support regional clusters of clean energy and industrial decarbonization projects. These stakeholders work together to minimize their environmental impact while benefiting the local economy by sharing infrastructure, creating demand for low-emissions fuels and materials, and implementing innovative technology.

For a hub to be successful there are three main components that must be considered: 

🌱 A supportive ecosystem 

🏗️ A variety of decarbonization projects  

🌐 Reliable connective infrastructure

This year marks the 10th anniversary of the Paris Agreement. As part of this milestone, countries are preparing to submit updated nationally determined contributions (NDCs), their blueprints to cut greenhouse gas emissions and increase their resilience to heatwaves, drought, floods, and other warming-induced extremes.  

NDCs are more than just commitments. They define how each country will contribute to the Paris Agreement’s goal by reducing their emissions. Each nation sets their own targets based on their unique circumstances, capabilities, and development priorities. While major emitters in the G20 will face the most scrutiny, Small Island Developing States and other vulnerable nations rely on NDCs to access critical climate finance and adaptation support. 

RMI is working with governments to strengthen and implement their NDCs. Through technical support, policy guidance, and investment strategies, RMI helps countries accelerate renewable energy transitions, advance clean transportation systems, and unlock climate finance, particularly in small island nations, Africa, and other emerging economies. 

With six months until the revised NDC deadline of September 2025, nations must turn ambition into action. Strong, well-defined NDCs will not only drive national policies, but also attract investment and speed the global clean energy transition.  

Learn more about NDCs, why they are so important, and what’s next for these countries: https://bit.ly/41lrzda

To build a successful sports team, you need more than just individuals who can score. Defense, tactics, roster depth, and teamwork all play a role as well. 

Similarly, a reliable electricity grid requires different components — including dispatchable and intermittent energy resources — which work together to maintain the availability of electricity for day-to-day and unusual situations, such as during extreme weather events. 

🏭 "Dispatchable" energy resources can be ramped up or down to increase or decrease power output on-demand during normal operating conditions, although sometimes they fail and cause outages. 

☀️ "Intermittent" energy resources like standalone solar and wind generation bolster the grid, reduce costs during their operation, and have weather-dependent, forecastable outputs. They can be “firmed” or made increasingly dispatchable with the addition of battery energy storage systems. 

Dispatchability is often misrepresented as the primary driver of electricity grid reliability, but system reliability as a whole is more important than dispatchability alone. 

To create affordable, whole-system reliability for customers, grid planners should assess and value how various system components such as intermittent resources, battery storage, virtual power plants, and electric transmission all complement each other and contribute to the grid. Learn more: https://bit.ly/4gY5qWZ

The US electricity grid needs new regional transmission lines to reduce energy waste, add new clean energy resources, and make the grid more resilient against extreme weather. 

But utilities are choosing to build smaller-scale transmission projects to take advantage of a regulatory gap that enables transmission projects within a utility’s territory to avoid robust review by federal and state regulators. Building out local transmission earns utilities guaranteed profits, but longer-range, higher voltage power lines can deliver greater benefits for customers. 

Regional transmission projects require greater coordination and collaboration between utilities, state regulators, and regional grid operators. With a regional-first approach, "you could be addressing local and regional needs simultaneously and meeting both needs in a more efficient manner," RMI’s Claire Wayner told Canary Media. 

Learn more about the local transmission regulatory gap: https://bit.ly/3QeWEIZ

As food scraps, yard clippings, and other organic waste decompose in landfills, they create methane, a powerful greenhouse gas. New initiatives at OC Waste & Recycling (OCWR) landfills tackle methane leaks by turning organic waste into compost and making technology upgrades to boost methane capture.  

Processing organic waste separately to create compost gives it a second life, transforming it into a resource that sequesters carbon, enriches soil, boosts crop yields, and cuts reliance on chemical fertilizers. To date, OCWR’s composting program has diverted over 64,000 tons of organic waste to make free compost and mulch for residential and community use. It’s a simple process with profound impacts, linking waste reduction to stronger agricultural systems and healthier communities 

OCWR’s Smart Landfill Program (SLP) uses real-time data assessment, robotic dogs, drone technology, and infrared imaging to monitor and manage the landfill system, improving efficiency and cutting methane and co-pollutants. The SLP can proactively identify leaks and fine-tune methane capture from its landfills, reducing greenhouse gas emissions by up to 15 percent. 

By diverting organic waste and implementing smart landfilling technologies, OCWR is demonstrating how to comprehensively cut methane emissions while delivering local benefits. https://bit.ly/3CyCZ3G

Heat pumps outsold gas furnaces in the US for the first time in 2022, and the trend has only continued. 

“Over 2024, there’s definitely a substantial increase in the sales of heat pumps relative to furnaces,” RMI’s Russell Unger told The Wall Street Journal. According to shipment data from the Air-Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute, Americans bought 37% more air-source heat pumps than gas furnaces during the first 11 months of the year. Although this appears like a new development, RMI’s national heat pump sales tracker shows that this is part of a decades-long consistent trend toward building electrification. 

Because heat pumps move heat, rather than create it, they can produce over four times as much heat energy (in kWh) as they consume in electricity, resulting in lower energy waste, better performance, and in many cases lower operating costs.
