RMI cofounder Amory Lovins honors the life and legacy of President Jimmy Carter: 

"I had the pleasure of meeting President Jimmy Carter in the Oval Office in 1977. He was such a fine engineer that, in that first meeting, the Energy Secretary (Jim Schlesinger) said three things — all technical, all wrong — and President Carter corrected him on all three. He’d invited me in to discuss my 1976 Foreign Affairs article reframing energy solutions around efficiency and renewables to achieve many social, economic, environmental, health, and security benefits. He’d mastered that article, and with impressive strategic foresight, launched decades of US progress on those lines. A decade ago he told me how helpful he’d found it. I feel blessed by the honor of having known this inspiring and remarkable man."

A new roadmap, launched by The Bezos Earth Fund charts the course to remove 10 gigatons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere annually by 2050. The report, prepared in collaboration between RMI, and the Earth Fund, will help researchers, governments, and communities align on how to safely and efficiently scale technological approaches to remove greenhouse gases (GHGs) from the atmosphere. 

Communities around the world are working hard to cut GHG emissions, by increasing energy efficiency, switching to renewable energy sources, managing landfills, and other strategies. These efforts need to be supported and ramped up.  

We also need to support complementary efforts to remove GHGs already in the atmosphere. As the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) states, carbon dioxide removal is now necessary to reach our climate goals. If deployed sustainably and responsibly, these technologies can also deliver other benefits to the environment, economy, and local communities.

Among utilities, integrated resource planning (IRP) is used to ensure that there will be enough electricity supply to meet tomorrow’s demand. IRPs are becoming more complex as the U.S. electricity grid evolves with the addition of renewable energy, the retirement of fossil fuel plants, the growth of distributed energy resources, and more extreme weather.

A fundamental part of IRPs is grid modeling. However, traditional grid modeling assumptions for a centralized fossil fuel-based grid miss some critical factors. To better evaluate the roles of renewables, energy storage, and demand flexibility, grid planners need to: 

⏰ Use more detailed temporal data – “Time slices” and other aggregate data miss important production and demand dynamics over the course of the day and undervalue the benefits of energy storage.  

🌩️ Expand weather data to integrate information from multiple years – Relying on a single or average year of data can miss extreme weather events. 

🔋 Consider the resources of neighboring systems – Factoring in the availability of nearby energy resources helps avoid overbuilding and can help plan for efficient transmission lines that can serve more than one system, improving affordability and reliability. 

☀️ Model multiple renewable energy profiles – In diverse or large geographies, using more detailed geospatial data captures the benefits of renewable resources across multiple locations. 

Updating U.S. electricity grid modeling assumptions can help ensure that the future of the grid is affordable, reliable, and keeps the most vulnerable electricity customers top of mind.  https://bit.ly/401w5wJ

To cut costs and emissions and increase power supply and longevity, GVE, in partnership with Abuja Electric Company (AEDC) and the Wuse Market Association, have commissioned a 1 MW solar minigrid with battery storage to remove the need for these diesel generators.

The Wuse Market can act as a catalyst for similar projects across Nigeria, increasing access to safe and consistent energy for those who need it the most.

With hurricane season on the horizon, the case for resilient energy infrastructure is stronger than ever.

RMI’s Solar Under Storm 2018 report was a turning point for the solar industry, offering the first comprehensive look at how solar energy systems perform in high-wind, hurricane-prone regions. Based on forensic analyses of hurricane damage and expert input from across the Caribbean, the report laid out clear, actionable design principles that have since influenced how resilient solar is deployed around the world. 

Key insights from the original report include: 
🔅Prioritizing connection integrity and mountain hardware.
🔅Using dual post piers for ground mount systems to reduce vibrations. 
🔅Utilizing fixed mount systems, not tracking systems. 
🔅Selecting solar modules with independent testing for wind effects. 
🔅Standardizing resilient design — turning Caribbean best practices into widespread norms across the global industry. 

Drawing on years of field experience and system performance in some of the world’s most storm-exposed regions, the upcoming Solar Under Storm III report takes the next step. It captures what’s worked and what needs to evolve — bringing sharper recommendations and new real-world case studies to the forefront.

Data centers and new manufacturing facilities are being built across the United States, leading to growing electricity demand that challenges the existing grid.  

Building new facilities that consume large amounts of electricity alongside new solar, wind, and battery resources — an approach RMI calls "Power Couples" — near existing natural gas generators with approved grid interconnections can help rapidly supply clean electricity and maintain grid reliability.

In the power couple structure, data centers wouldn’t even draw from the grid, RMI’s Uday Varadarajan explained to Canary Media. Instead, they’d be connected to clean power behind the "point of interconnection" between existing fossil fuel power plants and the wider grid. This approach could also make electricity more affordable for average customers while reducing air pollution. https://bit.ly/3QNPHiu

Indonesia’s nickel and aluminum industries are growing fast, and so is their reliance on coal-fired captive power. More and more of these projects are using off-grid coal power, known as “captive power,” which runs outside the main electricity system and is hard to regulate.

Most of this growth is tied to nickel smelters, with aluminum projects also on the rise. These early adopters have proven that with the right tools, nonprofit solar ownership can lower costs, generate community benefits, and expand affordable energy access. 

RMI’s new RISE Guide highlights real-world best practices and successes, and offers insights on scaling solar through nonprofit partnerships — including ways programs might adapt due to potential policy shifts: https://bit.ly/3TIk23k

As temperatures rise and heat waves grip much of the United States and the globe, air conditioning has become a daily essential for more people. But this comes at a cost: air conditioning generates 7% of global annual emissions.  

RMI’s global cooling team, led by Ankit Kalanki, is working to change that.  

“Air conditioning is becoming a lifeline in this overheated world,” says Kalanki. “It’s no longer a luxury. We rely on air conditioning for comfort, to feel productive, to feel safe and healthy, and this is an invisible driver of electricity demand and emissions.” As we push to commercialize “super-efficient” AC alternatives and make them accessible and affordable, there’s plenty builders, designers, and consumers can do right now to cool indoor spaces and reduce their AC-related energy costs. 

Read more in TIME: https://bit.ly/44muZN6

This Caribbean Heritage Month, we celebrate the leadership, resilience, and innovation driving energy transformation across the Caribbean. 

Island nations are taking bold steps to shift away from costly imported fossil fuels to clean, resilient power systems — and RMI is proud to work alongside governments, utilities, and communities to support this journey. 

Across the region, RMI has supported: 

🌴 29 completed and 22 renewable energy projects under development, representing a total of over 430 MW of solar generation capacity across 20 island nations 
🌴 Microgrid installations, including a 100% solar-powered system on Ragged Island in the Bahamas and Anegada 
🌴 National energy transition planning to unlock sustainable investments 
🌴 Regional workforce development in partnership with RMI's Energy Transition Academy 
🌴 The development of hurricane-resistant solar minigrids, to ensure resilient energy infrastructure when it’s needed most 

Through RMI's Climate Finance Access Network (CFAN), Caribbean nations are unlocking critical climate finance. CFAN has established a $769 million project pipeline across countries and regional organizations such as the CARICOM Development Fund, Jamaica, Grenada and the Grenadines. Advisors are embedded within national governments and regional institutions, including the CARICOM Development Fund, to accelerate access to funding for high-impact climate projects. 

These efforts reflect the strength of partnership and the momentum building toward a resilient energy future — powered by collaboration, local expertise, and the unwavering spirit of Caribbean communities. 

Learn more about our work in the region:

-Climate Finance Access Network: https://bit.ly/3g4GNhi

The Global South is rich in renewable energy resources. RMI is working in Africa, Southeast Asia, and island nations to help accelerate clean energy adoption by demonstrating energy planning and policy processes, piloting projects to address a wide range of energy needs, and supporting access to climate finance, all while facilitating capacity building and workforce development. 

With your support, RMI can continue to work alongside communities to co-create resilient energy solutions that ensure homes, schools, businesses, and essential services have access to reliable, affordable, clean power when and where they need it. 

From now through June 30, your contribution is tripled, thanks to a generous match. Give today: https://bit.ly/3GAaAeV

Just Released: Solar Under Storm III ⚡ 

Building on the findings of Solar Under Storm (2018) and Solar Under Storm II (2020), this third edition delivers a timely update to best practices for designing and installing ground-mount solar PV systems in hurricane-prone regions.

The new report incorporates lessons from the past few years, including from Hurricane Beryl, the earliest Category 5 hurricane ever recorded in the Atlantic, which devastated parts of the Grenadines in July 2024. RMI engineers conducted in-depth field investigations at three solar installations in the storm’s path, revealing a clear contrast between systems designed and built with specific resilience measures and those that were not.

Solar Under Storm III updates technical recommendations based on real-world damage assessments, highlights key failure points, and reinforces proven design features like dual-post piers, through-bolting, and vibration-resistant connections. 

As solar power becomes critical infrastructure in coastal and island regions across the globe, resilience can no longer be an afterthought. This report offers the tools to get them built right. 

🔗 Read the full report: https://bit.ly/3ZJTRgh

Sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) is available now and is critical to reducing aviation emissions, but cost barriers and unpredictable demand have hindered SAF’s growth. 

To show the market that demand for SAF exists and bridge the gap between SAF suppliers and customers, RMI and partners, with philanthropic support, brought together nine founding companies to form the Sustainable Aviation Buyers Alliance (SABA). Since 2021, the initiative has expanded to include 36 member companies and has helped channel more than $200 million into SAF investment. Demand aggregating organizations like SABA create certainty in the market and encourage producers to scale up the supply of lower-carbon products, resulting in lower costs as they reach economies of scale.  

Aviation’s success has helped illuminate pathways to reducing emissions for other heavy industries like shipping, trucking, and steel: https://bit.ly/4n7I6u5

🎡 RMI is at London Climate Action Week representing solutions for climate finance, methane, and energy innovation. 

Experts worldwide are traveling to London to discuss best practices to increase security, lower consumer costs, and meet the growing challenge of increasing energy demand. RMI is excited to be among the experts attending and hosting events to prepare our global colleagues to meet the challenges and opportunities of the energy transition with levers such as: 

💸 Climate finance: An estimated $4.5 trillion of global investment in clean energy needed by 2030 to avoid the worst consequences of climate change. This necessary investment will spur economic growth for corporations and consumers alike. 

🏭 Curbing methane: A powerful pollutant, reducing methane emissions means reducing waste and cleaning up our communities. RMI is working with partners to detect and reduce methane leakage from the waste and oil and gas sectors. 

🔆 Industrial innovation: Deploying climate-positive innovative technologies at scale in industrial sectors will encourage investment and accelerate the energy transition. 

To learn more about what RMI is bringing to London and to connect with us, visit our page: https://bit.ly/4li3o6x

Indonesia is at a crossroads in its transportation journey. Its need to move both passengers and goods is growing as the economy expands, population rises, and urban development continues.  

In Jakarta, Indonesia’s capital and largest city, two-wheelers such as motorcycles and scooters dominate mobility, accounting for 64% of all trips, and four-wheelers — cars, minivans, taxis, and light-duty freight vehicles — are a close second. Switching to electric vehicles (EVs) offers an opportunity to lower the total cost of vehicle ownership for consumers, create jobs and economic development, and improve air quality for the community.  

Indonesia’s government is committed to having 13 million electric two-wheelers and 2 million electric four-wheelers on the road by 2030. Indonesia can support the continued development of a thriving EV ecosystem through enabling policies, accessible and affordable EV financing, technology adoption, and strong consumer awareness: https://bit.ly/43Th5TF

We have work to do.

❄️ Help bring super-efficient air conditioners, which can use 60 percent less energy than typical ACs, to market

➕ And so much more!

As average energy costs continue to rise, millions of Americans are struggling to afford the energy necessary for basic needs such as heating, cooling, and cooking. In 2024, one in three US households reported cutting back on food, delaying medical care, or keeping their homes at unsafe temperatures in order to pay their household energy bills. 

Despite the magnitude of this problem, there are proven policy solutions. State public utility commissions (PUCs) have the power to implement safeguard policies that help energy-insecure customers afford their bills.  

RMI created the Energy Poverty Policy Simulator (EPPS) to evaluate different policies, their effects on average bills and energy burdens, and their estimated total cost of implementation with clear, data-driven evidence. The EPPS is the first tool that allows decision makers and stakeholders to leverage a suite of energy poverty policies in every state to chart a path to ending energy poverty: https://bit.ly/44bx1AB

tag_name: [1] 14. Drive Market For Climate Products And Services
text:
Around 2.3 billion people globally still cook with wood, charcoal, coal, or kerosene. These fuels are costly, time-consuming to gather, and harmful to people and the planet by contributing to indoor air pollution and about 2% of global greenhouse gas emissions. 

Clean and improved cookstove carbon credits aim to change that by helping households transition to cleaner fuels or more efficient stoves. Since 2004, more than 181 million credits have been issued, but designing and monitoring these projects is complicated. Cooking practices vary widely by region, culture, and season, making it hard to accurately measure impact. Early methodologies didn’t always capture this complexity, leading to concerns about credit quality. 

Our latest Technical Explainer from the Carbon Markets Initiative gives a clear and neutral overview of how the market is evolving, from improved monitoring practices to new, more rigorous methodologies. With stronger data, better design, and growing alignment under the Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market’s Core Carbon Principles, cookstove credits are entering a new phase where they can deliver credible, lasting benefits for both people and the climate. 

Read the full explainer: https://ow.ly/Sum050XxQs0

Brazil is taking steps toward becoming a major producer of green e-fuels, backed by strong renewable resources and new national policies. 

At the Oceans of Opportunity Summit in Rio de Janeiro, held just before #COP30 in Brazil, leaders from across the maritime and energy sectors reviewed early findings for a proposed green e-fuel export shipping corridor between the Port of Açu in Brazil and the Port of Antwerp-Bruges in Belgium. The analysis shows that Brazil is well-positioned to supply competitively priced e-fuels to Europe as new maritime and climate regulations drive demand. 

The Port of Açu is emerging as a key site for this effort, with several e-fuel projects announced and the infrastructure needed to support large-scale production and export. RMI and Global Maritime Forum's pre-feasibility study indicates that the corridor is technically viable and could begin operating in the early 2030s.

text:
Representatives from around the world are gathering today in Belém, Brazil for #COP30 Agriculture Day.  

As one of the world’s leading agricultural producers, #Brazil relies heavily on nitrogen fertilizer. But with such a high demand, the country has to import most of its supply — mainly from China, Russia, Nigeria, and Oman — leaving it vulnerable to global market swings and geopolitical uncertainty. 

Despite its strong agricultural sector, Brazil has struggled to sustain or expand domestic fertilizer production, largely due to high natural gas prices, a key input for producing ammonia and its fertilizer derivatives. 

As demand continues to rise, these challenges could open the door to a new opportunity: by leveraging its abundant renewable energy resources, Brazil could produce low-emissions fertilizers at home, improving energy security, reducing reliance on imports and ensuring price stability.  

In a new joint publication, RMI and the E+ Institute explore how Brazil can take steps to produce low-carbon fertilizers at home more affordably while advancing its climate goals.  

Learn more 👇  https://ow.ly/zEmf50XtQJN

As Jamaica recovers from the devastating impacts of Hurricane Melissa, solar energy systems are offering hope amid widespread power outages. The New York Times highlights how rooftop solar and batteries, once viewed mainly as a path toward energy independence, is now proving vital to recovery and resilience. 

RMI’s David Gumbs, Director of the Islands Energy Program, is featured in the piece stating that, “With solar, you maintain some ability to continue generating electricity without relying on hundreds of miles of damaged power lines. In the Caribbean context, when the hurricane passes, if I have rooftop solar and batteries and if I can keep my refrigerator running, my entire neighborhood benefits.” 

Rooftop solar has grown rapidly in Jamaica, rising from less than 1.4 MW in 2015 to nearly 65 MW in 2023. As the country works toward generating 50% of its electricity from renewables by 2030, the importance of distributed, resilient power is front and center.  

Our thoughts are with all those in Jamaica and across the region who are rebuilding in the wake of Hurricane Melissa.

India is starting to build the foundation of its domestic battery industry to support its clean energy, mobility, and manufacturing ambitions. The direction it takes now will shape the strength and sustainability of India’s energy future. 

RMI’s new report explores how circular practices like reuse, repurposing, and recycling can help India meet rising battery demand while reducing import dependence and securing critical mineral supply. With electric vehicle sales climbing past two million and battery needs projected to increase 40-fold by 2050, designing circularity into the system from the start is both a practical and strategic move. 

The report finds that battery circularity could create over ₹75,500 crore (~US8.5 billion) in value, meet more than 40 percent of India’s lithium, nickel, and cobalt demand by 2050, and generate thousands of green jobs. Early policies like the Battery Waste Management Rules and a growing base of startups in reuse and recycling are already setting the stage, but stronger coordination across industry, finance, and government will be essential to scale these efforts nationwide.

text:
Carbon removal, together with a transition to clean energy, is essential to stabilizing the climate and reducing excess greenhouse gases. It also offers opportunities to clean up waste, restore ecosystems, and create jobs in emerging industries. RMI’s new discussion paper explores how governments can accelerate carbon removal through smart, coordinated market action. 

To stabilize our climate, the world will need to remove around 100 million tons of carbon dioxide each year by 2035 — roughly equal to the annual emissions of Japan’s entire power sector. Achieving that scale will require about $20 billion in annual investment to drive innovation and early deployment. While progress is being made, the industry is not yet on track. 

A government-led advance market commitment (AMC) offers a powerful way to close this gap. By guaranteeing future demand, governments can attract private investment, reduce costs through competition, and set clear standards for a credible global carbon removal market. Similar mechanisms have helped other critical public goods, and the same approach can now accelerate carbon removal. 

The next decade will determine whether carbon removal becomes a cornerstone of climate action or remains a missed opportunity. This paper outlines design options and invites governments, industry, and civil society to work together to build a mechanism that delivers durable climate impact. 

📘 Read more: https://ow.ly/GNm050XkuJt

Across the United States, the transition to affordable, clean energy is taking shape in ways we can all see and feel. Solar panels line the rooftops of schools and libraries. EV charging stations stand ready in grocery store parking lots. Electric buses and service vehicles are rolling out to replace diesel fleets. 

Behind every project is a story, and every story has to start somewhere: a conversation, a calculation, a leap of faith. These projects come to life through the persistence and creativity of individuals and teams determined to build a cleaner future. 

RMI's new ‘Voices of Clean Energy’ shares these stories. Each profile highlights the people turning ideas into action: grant writers, project managers, engineers, community leaders, and more. In a recent CNN feature, Ellen Kennedy, RMI expert on carbon-free transportation, explained why this is a win for Americans’ pocketbooks and air quality. 

“It is 99% more efficient to ride an e-bike than to drive a gas car. They require so little energy. An entire month of e-bike charging will cost you less than one day of running your air conditioner at home.” 

For many households, that means thousands of dollars saved each year on fuel and maintenance — on top of the health and climate benefits. E-bikes are proving that the clean energy transition can deliver immediate benefits for families and communities while cutting carbon and creating a healthier future. 

Watch the full piece: https://cnn.it/4mTLiIb

Net-zero aligned targets are now embedded across the financial system, but progress is hard to see. As climate goals have gained board-level oversight and attention from shareholders and clients, their challenges are mounting — technical, economic, legal, and reputational. Meanwhile, emissions keep rising. 

If the point of commitments and targets was always to see finance flow to the transition, then shouldn’t we ask: What is stopping the transition-enabling transactions from happening? 

In our latest report we offer a hopeful and pragmatic path forward: one where banks are empowered to do what they do best — make deals that move capital toward the transition. 

We believe the following four shifts are essential: 

1️⃣ Recognize banks as commercial actors: Banks have a business model to adhere to, but that doesn’t mean they don’t have a role to play. Understanding that climate-smart investing is just smart investing is only the start. 

2️⃣ Let them lead through what they know: Structuring, advising, deploying capital. That’s how banks can accelerate the transition, not just measure it. 

3️⃣ Build smarter transition intelligence: Metrics that rely on high-level or aggregated data on companies and sectors have not always led to helpful insights, incentives, or market signals. Focusing on the insights that drive financial decisions are key. 

4️⃣ Design systems that work: Climate action is collaborative. Policy, finance, and real-economy actors must work together to remove roadblocks and reward progress. 

By working with, not against, the realities of the financial system, we can unlock the next wave of climate progress. 

📘 Explore the report to learn more: https://rmi.org/insight/recalibrating-the-role-of-banks-in-the-energy-transition/?utm_campaign=organic&utm_source=Facebook&utm_medium=social&utm_content=RMIBrand

Do you know what type of refrigerant your HVAC system uses? Do you know how much is in it?  

Managing refrigerant charge is an important aspect of keeping ACs and heat pumps running efficiently. Even small leaks or undercharging can lower performance, driving up bills, straining the grid, and worsening climate pollution.

Construction on the Empire State Building, one of the world’s first supertall buildings, began in 1930. Nearly a century later, the architectural triumph is pushing the frontiers of energy efficiency.  

Empire State Realty Trust and RMI were able to reduce the Empire State Building’s energy consumption by 50% thanks to deep retrofits in the late 2000s. As part of the major upgrade, some 6,500 windows were remanufactured on site, to let in light but reflect heat. By cutting the building’s heating and cooling needs, the improvements helped net $17 million in savings. 

The Empire State Buildings savings offer other cities and states a model to cut waste and pollution in their own buildings: https://bit.ly/3IAhDWs

Carbon 👏 credits 👏 are👏 about👏 more👏 than👏 just👏 carbon! 

Yes, you heard us right. At their core, carbon credit projects operate within dynamic social and ecological systems. So, why stop at measuring only emissions? While carbon data shows how much greenhouse gases were avoided, reduced, or removed, the full story lies in the socio-environmental (SE) data — covering human rights, community benefits, biodiversity, and more.  

Back in July, we launched the Carbon Crediting Data Framework (CCDF) to help the voluntary carbon market organize, assess, and compare data on carbon credits. During its development, we talked with developers, standards bodies, ratings agencies, and other experts. They all agree: SE data is just as critical in decision making as carbon. 

That’s why we created a specialized SE toolkit within the CCDF. It gives voluntary carbon market actors the structure and best practices they need to credibly measure and report these broader impacts to demonstrate real benefits and even capture market premiums.  

Read our recent article to learn how pairing emissions data (how much carbon was avoided, reduced, or removed) with SE data (how it impacts people and ecosystems), can help projects achieve greater impact and long-term success. 

🔗 https://rmi.org/beyond-the-ton-documenting-the-benefits-to-communities-and-ecosystems-at-the-heart-of-carbon-projects/?utm_campaign=organic&utm_content=RMIBrand&utm_medium=social&utm_source=Facebook

Cement and concrete are responsible for ~8 percent of global carbon emissions, yet low-carbon alternatives remain scarce and not available at scale. Why? It’s a classic chicken-and-egg problem: buyers can’t access clean cement, and producers won’t invest in it without clear demand signals.  

Today, RMI and the Center for Green Market Activation (GMA) are launching the Sustainable Concrete Buyers Alliance (or SCoBA) to help break this cycle.  

In the words of our CEO Jon Creyts, “By aggregating demand from major buyers and establishing rigorous standards, we're creating the market conditions for breakthrough low-carbon cement innovations to scale.”  

This approach builds on the success of RMI and GMA’s buyer alliances in aviation, steel, and trucking — proving that collective demand can unlock clean solutions in even the hardest-to-abate sectors. 

👉 Read the press release to learn more: https://bit.ly/4gsjXLC

Kotzebue, Alaska, sits above the Arctic Circle, surrounded by a frozen sea for much of the year. No roads lead there, with only planes providing year-round access (or dog sleds and snowmobiles for the more intrepid). Residents have long relied on the town’s diesel power plant for electricity, with fuel shipped in annually.  

Making electricity this way comes at a cost: Kotzebue’s leaders have to accept whatever fuel prices the market dictates at the time, a variable over which they have no control. They also must wait until the sea ice thaws for any hope of resupply.  

A hard look at cost and reliability made them seek an alternative path: embracing technologies such as wind, solar, and batteries to create a more resilient and affordable energy system.  

In the final article of our Energy Leadership Accelerator series, we explore how communities like Kotzebue are achieving energy independence and economic resilience through bold, place-based innovation.  

At the intersection of climate adaptation and energy sovereignty, modern energy infrastructure and new technologies are reducing costs, stabilizing local economies, and giving communities control over their future. 

Read the full article: https://bit.ly/46AiFec

Rising demand is driving the growth of “fast fashion” — low-cost clothing often worn only once — made from polyester. Polyester is a synthetic fiber, or plastic, derived from petroleum-based chemicals that constantly shed microplastics. Most polyester fibers aren't biodegradable. This creates big challenges to manage polyester waste as well as to cut the emissions from making, using, and disposing of these products. 

With an annual production volume of approximately 73 million tons, polyester now comprises over half of global textile fiber production. By evaluating the polyester value chain, we can understand pathways to a climate-differentiated chemicals markets, and the technology, policy, and market innovation needed to take a step towards sustainable production of polyester products. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Food waste is the single most landfilled material and causes nearly 60% of landfill methane emissions, the EPA reports. Methane, a powerful greenhouse gas, is generated when organic materials — such as food waste, yard waste, and paper — decompose in landfills without oxygen. As a result, landfills are the third largest source of methane in the United States with annual emissions equivalent to driving 66 million cars or operating 79 coal-fired power plants for an entire year. 

Stakeholders at every level can help cut methane emissions by preventing organic materials from ending up in landfills. Such "upstream strategies" include minimizing waste, donating excess food, and recycling residual organics into animal feed, compost, or biogas. 

"Downstream strategies" focus on improving landfill design, operations, and monitoring practices to increase methane capture and reduce methane leaks. Combining upstream and downstream efforts could slash waste methane emissions by 15% by 2030, according to the Center for Global Sustainability at the University of Maryland. 

Beyond the climate benefits, cutting methane emissions from waste can help address food insecurity, reduce health risks, improve the quality of life for nearby communities, create jobs, and produce valuable products, such as compost. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Virtual power plants (VPPs) aggregate smart thermostats and appliances, electric vehicles, and distributed energy resources of households and businesses to provide many of the same services as a traditional power plant by coordinating the charging, discharging, and management of all the connected devices. 

VPPs reduce power sector emissions by shifting demand to be met by lower-emissions energy resources. In 2024, VPPs across the country could shift demand to avoid up to 7.3 million tons of carbon dioxide emissions. By 2035, VPPs could avoid 12 to 28 million tons, or two to four percent of projected US power sector carbon emissions. 

However, few regions have fully integrated VPPs into grid planning and operations. Grid decision makers need to evaluate, plan for, and realize the full value of VPPs, including avoided transmission and distribution costs. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about the key conditions for fully realizing VPPs’ grid benefits.

With a growing demand for energy, many regions in the Global South are investing in their rich stock of renewable resources instead of relying on imported fossil fuels. Solar and wind generation in the Global South has been growing at 23 percent a year for the past five years and already supplies nine percent of electricity— only five years behind the Global North.

Despite starting this transition later, the Global South is moving faster than the Global North. One-fifth of the Global South has already overtaken the Global North in terms of the share of solar and wind or electrification. By 2030, the Global South could be adding as much renewable capacity as the Global North. 

Even with these positive advancements, there remains the necessity to go faster, and to do more to help low-income countries. Strong domestic policy, global capital directed especially to low-income countries, and technology transfer are critical to accelerating the energy transition and boosting economic growth across the Global South.

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

As the planet gets hotter and more humid, demand for residential air-conditioning is set to triple by 2030, with billions of room AC units being installed across the globe. To meet this demand while simultaneously tackling pollution and energy efficiency, RMI and its partners launched the Global Cooling Prize in 2018 to spur innovative cooling technologies that have 5x lower climate impact and announced two winners in 2021. 

In June, @washingtonpost/ @postclimate joined RMI in India to see how Global Cooling Prize-winning prototypes are being tested and primed to tackle some of the most hot and humid temperatures on Earth — and to gauge how much they could shrink air conditioning’s rapidly growing emissions. 

"We must switch and try to accelerate the market toward these super-efficient ACs now," RMI cooling expert Ankit Kalanki told The Post. "Otherwise, we will be adding a large number of inefficient units that will only contribute to this vicious loop of warming."

If nearly all AC units sold globally meet the Global Cooling Prize contest parameters by 2040, the improved cooling technology could prevent up to half a degree Celsius of global warming by the end of the century. 

Click the link in our bio to learn how RMI, the Clean Cooling Collaborative, @ceptresearch, and many other partners across industries and governments are working on a global effort to commercialize next generation ACs and get them onto store shelves and into homes and apartments.

Oil and gas will be a big point of discussion this #CERAWeek and RMI is there discussing all things methane. 

For years, methane mitigation was all but ignored because industry players had little motivation to prioritize cutting methane waste. Methane leakage led to increased energy prices for consumers, and profit loss for the industry. But there are many market-based incentives that can help with methane mitigation and benefit everyone. 

Increasingly reliable data, smarter policies, and more robust standards are strengthening incentives for action. Mitigating methane waste is a win-win-win opportunity for energy security, energy efficiency, and the climate.

#CERAWeek is here, and energy security is top of mind for many conference goers. The deployment of renewable energy has gained historic momentum over the past few years, helping to increase our energy efficiency and affordability, but the problem of methane — its waste and ensuing pollution — looms large. 

When it enters the atmosphere, methane leaking from oil and gas pipelines, wells, tanks, and other infrastructure harms local air quality and superheats our planet. Leaks also decrease energy security as it wastes valuable resources, all while increasing prices for consumers and eroding industry revenues. That’s why it’s key to address the methane waste issue, and in turn create jobs, protect public safety, and bolster national security. 

Energy security conversations are becoming more and more commonplace. And with possible regulatory changes ahead, fixing the leaks can ensure that America’s energy is safe, reliable, and affordable for everyone. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

When Hurricane Francine cut off power to almost half a million customers in New Orleans last September, First Grace United Methodist Church still had lights, water, and air conditioning thanks to its solar-plus-battery-storage microgrid. 

First Grace is part of a network of Community Lighthouses born out of the experience New Orleans residents faced after Hurricanes Katrina and Ian. The Community Lighthouses are 10 community buildings, with more planned for the future, powered by solar and batteries and designed to provide essentials like power, air conditioning, water, meals, a community gathering space, and more when storms hit. 

Solar-plus-battery-storage microgrids are typically the first step to building a resilience hub as they provide reliable power when the grid goes out. They also benefit communities when skies are clear, while simultaneously supporting local businesses.

Building a microgrid is more appealing and doable than ever before, with solar and battery costs declining and other support available. To help communities overcome microgrid implementation challenges, RMI led a "Microgrids for Resilient Communities" cohort where members and RMI learned from each other’s direct experiences. 

Visit the link in our bio to learn what factors to consider and how to create a microgrid to support your community.

Methane pollution from leaking oil and gas wells is a big problem, but because methane is invisible and odorless, tracking these leaks has been challenging. Now, new methane satellites and advanced sensing technology are changing the game, making it easier to find, track, and stop leaks, and the massive energy waste they represent. 

MethaneSAT and @carbonmapper both launched their own satellites last year to fill key gaps in detection. 

By identifying leaks, these satellites can not only prevent pollution, they can also help oil and gas operators keep products in the pipeline, saving them money in the long run and consumers money on their heating and electricity bills. 

As we approach #CeraWeek where methane will be center stage, we're taking a look at how satellite tech is helping tackle methane pollution. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

As we wrap up #HubsWeek, let’s chat about the final—and most important—piece for a successful clean industrial hub: decarbonization projects. 

These projects are often first-of-a-kind in their industry, cutting or even eliminating greenhouse gas emissions from traditionally heavy polluting industrial, heavy transport, and energy sectors. They lean on new technologies, particularly industrial electrification, green hydrogen, and sustainable fuels. 

Not only can these projects help create hundreds of thousands of new jobs, but they also have the potential to reduce carbon pollution in industrial centers. Of course, to really see a positive change in communities worldwide, we need to expand these efforts far beyond a few states. 

To meet net-zero goals, we’ll need to build more than 700 net-zero industrial projects by 2030. But following California and Texas’ success across the globe will require supportive policies, strategic investments, and tailored regional strategies. 

With more than 190 industrial decarbonization projects announced in the US alone, tracking the progress of these efforts is crucial to reaching our goals. To address this, Mission Possible Partnership launched their Global Project Tracker, which allows industry groups and other stakeholders to identify potential partners or clean energy hubs, financial institutions to see potential investment options, and policymakers to track progress in their regions and inform decision making. Tracking progress and expanding these efforts worldwide will enable the US and other countries to drive rapid, transformative change and secure a sustainable, low-carbon future. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about the outlook for Clean Industrial Hubs, and Mission Possible Partnerships Global Project Tracker.

From financing to policy to local community buy-in, ecosystem support is key to a successful clean industrial hub. Securing the social license for development is particularly critical to set up a project for success. Effective stakeholder management is critical for keeping projects on track to reach final investment decisions and begin construction. Without community support and buy-in, first-of-a-kind or later generation decarbonization projects often face delays or cancellations during the planning and permitting. In short, missing holistic support— covering policy, finance, research and development, and community engagement — the whole timeline for achieving decarbonization goals can fall apart. But there’s a fix: proactively identifying, analyzing, and engaging with key stakeholders, and RMI’s stakeholder analysis and mapping tool, S.A.M., makes that possible. 

S.A.M. is a digital tool that creates clear visuals of how different stakeholders interact, and can help developers understand the realities and local power dynamics on the ground. This way, they can learn about concerns and objections before starting a long, expensive permitting process. Identifying and understanding the connections enables better outreach and engagement strategies to derisk project deployment. 

Beyond mapping project stakeholders, S.A.M. is incredibly versatile, with use applications for in grid planning and electricity markets, supply chain visualization, partnership management and research in fields like finance, energy, public health, sustainability, urban planning, and more. This makes it a valuable tool not only for clean industrial hubs, but for holistic ecosystem support across industries. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about the S.A.M. tool and effective stakeholder management to derisk projects.

Access to clean, safe, affordable energy is life changing yet remains a challenge around the world. To mark Black History Month, RMI explores how helping communities shift to lower-cost, clean energy can save money, build business, and improve quality of life for Black communities in the United States. 

In 27 cities stretching from Honolulu to Boston, RMI organized cohorts to make rooftop solar affordable and accessible for lower- and moderate-income (LMI) communities. In total, these Solarize campaigns helped nearly 1,200 households install more than 10 megawatts of residential solar, thanks to close collaboration between governments, community-based organizations, and others. 

The solar panels are already delivering real saving. And over their 25-year lifespan, the panels will avoid $8 million in electricity bills and reduce CO2 emissions by 106,000 tons, equivalent to planting over 1.76 million trees. Overall, 40 percent of the households that purchased solar systems through RMI’s Solarize cohort programs were in low- to middle-income communities. 

RMI also developed an Electrify cohort to help 12 teams — representing 20 local governments and their 30 partner organizations — develop and launch programs to help their community members electrify their homes, focusing on heat pumps. Across the country, these Electrify programs are projected to result in over 3,000 heat pump installations, which will help lower heating and cooling costs and improve air quality for households. A major part of these programs’ success was ensuring that community-based organizations were involved to ensure that resident voices were part of program design. 

Visit RMI.org to see how RMI’s Solarize and Electrify cohorts have made cleantech available to more Americans, saving them money on their energy bills while also creating business opportunities for local contractors.

Clean Industrial Hubs can play a critical role in decarbonizing the heavy industry and transport sectors and can spur economic growth, create employment opportunities, and unlock new technologies. But what exactly are they?

Clean Industrial Hubs bring together project developers, policymakers, financial institutions, and community-based organizations to support regional clusters of clean energy and industrial decarbonization projects. These stakeholders work together to minimize their environmental impact while benefiting the local economy by sharing infrastructure, creating demand for low-emissions fuels and materials, and implementing innovative technology.

For a hub to be successful there are three main components that must be considered: 

🌱 A supportive ecosystem 

🏗️ A variety of decarbonization projects 

🌐 Reliable connective infrastructure 

This #HubsWeek, we dive into the components of a successful hub, where these hubs are taking shape, and how RMI is working to support them. Click the link in our bio to learn more about our work supporting clean industrial hubs.

To build a successful sports team, you need more than just individuals who can score. Defense, tactics, roster depth, and teamwork all play a role as well. 

Similarly, a reliable electricity grid requires different components — including dispatchable and intermittent energy resources — which work together to maintain the availability of electricity for day-to-day and unusual situations, such as during extreme weather events. 

🏭 "Dispatchable" energy resources can be ramped up or down to increase or decrease power output on-demand during normal operating conditions, although sometimes they fail and cause outages. 

☀️ "Intermittent" energy resources like standalone solar and wind generation bolster the grid, reduce costs during their operation, and have weather-dependent, forecastable outputs. They can be “firmed” or made increasingly dispatchable with the addition of battery energy storage systems. 

Dispatchability is often misrepresented as the primary driver of electricity grid reliability, but system reliability as a whole is more important than dispatchability alone. 

To create affordable, whole-system reliability for customers, grid planners should assess and value how various system components such as intermittent resources, battery storage, virtual power plants, and electric transmission all complement each other and contribute to the grid. The waste sector accounts for nearly 20% of global methane emissions, but action on methane mitigation in this sector is lacking. One issue? Nailing down exactly where the methane is coming from. 

What if we could identify sites where waste methane is escaping into the atmosphere, and give municipalities resources to implement evidence-based reduction measures? RMI’s Waste Methane Assessment Platform, WasteMAP, does just that. 

By consolidating global emissions data from municipal solid waste, and offering advanced analysis tools and case studies, WasteMAP empowers decision makers to enact science-based strategies. WasteMAP can serve as a central source for addressing the escalating issue of waste sector methane emissions. 

Are you close to a secret super-emitter? Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Leaky oil and gas pipelines, wells, tanks, and other assets are major sources of methane, a super-potent global warming gas. The good news is that methane mitigation is quickly gaining momentum. The past five years have ushered in a host of methane pollution gains. 

From low-leakage gas to reduced flaring and better maintenance, steps that keep methane in the system improve efficiency, market competitiveness, and can help drive down costs for the end consumer while cutting energy waste. 

That's why it's in everyone's best interest to protect programs, such as the waste emissions charge, that accelerate methane reduction.

Ammonia and ammonia-based fertilizers are essential to giving crops the nitrogen and other nutrients that they need. But the way we currently produce ammonia depends heavily on fossil fuels, making it difficult for the industry to cut down on emissions. The good news? There are promising new technologies that show potential for sustainable methods of ammonia production and can be an alternative to the widely-used Haber-Bosch method: 

🔵 Blue Ammonia – Conventional Haber-Bosch with carbon capture and storage (CCS) 

🟢 Green Ammonia – Produced using electrolysis-based hydrogen 

🔌 Electrochemical Ammonia – Uses electricity to drive nitrogen conversion 

⚡ Plasma-Catalysis Ammonia – Uses plasma technology for a more efficient reaction 

With advances in renewable energy, carbon capture, and innovative reactor designs, the next decade could bring ammonia production technologies that not only meet the world's fertilizer needs, but also build a more low-carbon, sustainable future. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

As food scraps, yard clippings, and other organic waste decompose in landfills, they create methane, a powerful greenhouse gas. New initiatives at @ocwaste (OCWR) landfills tackle methane leaks by turning organic waste into compost and making technology upgrades to boost methane capture. 

Processing organic waste separately to create compost gives it a second life, transforming it into a resource that sequesters carbon, enriches soil, boosts crop yields, and cuts reliance on chemical fertilizers. To date, OCWR’s composting program has diverted over 64,000 tons of organic waste to make free compost and mulch for residential and community use. It’s a simple process with profound impacts, linking waste reduction to stronger agricultural systems and healthier communities 

OCWR’s Smart Landfill Program (SLP) uses real-time data assessment, robotic dogs, drone technology, and infrared imaging to monitor and manage the landfill system, improving efficiency and cutting methane and co-pollutants. The SLP can proactively identify leaks and fine-tune methane capture from its landfills, reducing greenhouse gas emissions by up to 15 percent. 

By diverting organic waste and implementing smart landfilling technologies, OCWR is demonstrating how to comprehensively cut methane emissions while delivering local benefits.

Over half of all trips in 2021 were three miles or less and 60 percent of all vehicle trips were less than six miles. If people used electric bikes to take these trips instead of gas-powered cars, air quality, health outcomes, and transportation access would significantly improve. 

While policymakers, advocates, and other stakeholders recognize these benefits, many struggle to quantify them. That's why RMI created the E-Bike Environment and Economic Impact Assessment Calculator, which measures specific environmental, health, and economic impacts that e-bikes could have if they replaced a significant percentage of short motor vehicle trips under five miles. With this information stakeholders can illustrate the value of initiatives that accelerate e-bike adoption.

Clean steel is beginning to gain steam 💨, but to decarbonize the sector we must first address the role of iron production in the process. 

Producing iron — a key component of steel production — is a highly carbon-intensive process, accounting for over 80% of the steel supply chain emissions. Therefore, cleaning up iron production is key to decarbonizing the steel sector. While there is rising public and private interest in producing clean iron today, there is currently no common definition for clean iron production. 

RMI’s new article zeroes in on key use cases for a near-zero iron threshold to stimulate the technology investments needed to enable the global steel transition. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Retail brands have been leading the charge in championing sustainability in recent years, but progress has hit a snag. There is a lack of uniformity in defining what a low emissions product is. 

Most of these retail products start from a common group of petrochemical materials such as naphtha (derived from crude oil and natural gas), ethylene, paraxylene, and PET which are higher-polluting plastic materials. This means that, along with retailers, suppliers must work to accelerate the market for climate-differentiated chemicals. 

The good news? Retailers are now taking matters into their own hands. Goldwin, an outdoor apparel company based in Japan, is pioneering a sustainable polyester supply chain. Others, like GAP and Bestseller, are joining initiatives focused on electrifying supply chains and transitioning to renewable energy. 

No matter the approach, it’s clear that businesses want to work with suppliers to reduce their climate pollution, especially since supply chains contribute upwards of 70% of retailers’ total emissions. 

RMI’s latest analysis can help retailers and suppliers figure out how much and where in the supply chain emissions need to be reduced and accelerate the market for more sustainable chemical-derived products. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Across the United States, coal plants are operating at a financial loss. These uneconomic facilities are costing ratepayers money — and harming their health. 

The pollutants emitted by uneconomically operating coal plants lead to serious health consequences, such as worsening asthma symptoms and heart conditions, especially for densely populated communities nearby. Costs for medical visits, sick time, medications, and other healthcare needs go up, too. 

If coal plants were used only when they were the cheapest available resource to meet electricity demand, communities could see improvements in public health and their quality of life, along with reduced healthcare costs. 

By shifting to economic dispatch practices, utilities can significantly cut emissions, improve air quality, and lessen the strain on healthcare systems. Click the link in bio to learn more

Fertilizers play a huge role in feeding the world—helping to grow the food that sustains over half of the global population. Despite all the good they do, synthetic nitrogen fertilizers are also responsible for 1.31 gigatons of CO2 emissions each year, which is more than the aviation ✈️ and shipping 🚢 sectors combined. 

The issue lies in how fertilizers are made. Current production process, especially the Haber-Bosch process, is highly energy-intensive and relies on natural gas, which makes it a major source of carbon emissions. 

The good news? Sustainable fertilizers can change the game. They can improve soil health, promote biodiversity, and reduce dependence on fossil fuels—all while ensuring more stable agricultural outputs. If we can decarbonize fertilizer production, we not only stand to reduce emissions from this sector but also enhance the resilience of our global food system. 
 
Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Today, we reflect on the vision of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., who dreamed of a world of justice and equality for all. King’s legacy inspires changemakers like Catherine Coleman Flowers, a modern champion for environmental and social justice and a member of RMI’s Board of Trustees. 

As a MacArthur “genius” grant recipient, Catherine dedicates her work to addressing the intersection of poverty, climate change, and public health. She advocates for marginalized communities to have access to basic human rights, including clean air and safe water. 🌍

For more than 40 years, the Rocky Mountain Institute and its co-founder Amory Lovins have practiced Applied Hope: that acting out of hope can cultivate a different kind of world worth being hopeful about. On the eve of a major US political transition and ongoing global shifts, Lovins shares a powerful new essay exploring why we need to double down on this mindset. 

“It's time to shift language and action from “somebody should” to “I will,” to do real work on real projects, and to go to scale... We persuade people by delivering the diverse outcomes of a clean, prosperous, just, and secure energy future, without imposing specific motives. In short, we focus on outcomes, not motives: on what we can do together, not why each of us might want to.” 

Dive into Lovins’ reflections on why Applied Hope will continue to guide RMI’s approach to the energy transition.

Newly installed solar panels on the Elmer A Henderson: A Johns Hopkins Partnership School (Henderson-Hopkins) in Baltimore, Maryland, are helping students’ families, staff, and the local community tackle their electricity bills. 

The Solar4Us @ Henderson-Hopkins project, developed and financed by the Climate Access Fund (CAF) with technical assistance from RMI, will save approximately 150 low-to-moderate income households an estimated $1.1 million over the lifetime of the panels, or more than $200 per year per household. The project will also reduce climate pollution, provide clean energy education opportunities for students, and has already provided opportunities for job training and local hiring. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how CAF and RMI are working to make community solar a vehicle for economic development and other local benefits with support from the Inflation Reduction Act.

As the cement industry continues to work through ways to decarbonize, limestone calcined clay cement (LC3) is much cleaner than ordinary Portland cement and could unlock economic benefits for the sector. 

So, what is LC3? It’s a low-carbon cement blend that combines calcined clay and limestone to greatly reduce the need for traditional clinker, the most carbon-intensive component of cement. Traditional clinker is responsible for 85%–90% of cement's carbon emissions, but adopting LC3 can cut the use of clinker in half. 

RMI’s new report dives into why LC3 could be the next big thing for the industry, offering both climate and financial benefits. By acting now, stakeholders can seize the opportunity to lead in a rapidly evolving market while contributing to global climate goals. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Like every other major industry, the construction industry is also looking to decarbonize. But adoption of low-carbon cement has been slowed down by a significant hurdle: the lack of performance standards and specifications (PSS). 

PSS can help transition the concrete industry to net-zero, lower costs, and ensure long-term performance and durability of construction projects. However, to make PSS a reality, we need reliable performance-predicting testing and clearly defined responsibilities for their implementation.

RMI’s new guidance on developing PSS includes testing strength💪, durability ✅, and workability👷. These tests ensure that low-carbon cement mixtures meet the practical needs of the construction industry. We have a unique opportunity to trial these tests on low-carbon concrete projects happening today, which could accelerate the adoption of low-emissions materials.

Learn more about the development of performance standards and specifications, including what role you could play at the link in our bio.

From his early aspirations of working in oil and gas in Lagos, Nigeria, to leading the charge for renewable energy solutions across sub-Saharan Africa, Suleiman Babamanu’s journey is a testament to the power of clean energy transforming lives. 

Suleiman joined RMI in 2022 as the Program Director for the Africa Energy Program, where he now leads a passionate team dedicated to delivering affordable, reliable, and sustainable energy across sub-Saharan Africa. From electrifying rural villages to empowering urban centers, they believe energy is more than just a resource — it's a catalyst for opportunity, growth, and resilience.

A new roadmap, launched by The Bezos Earth Fund charts the course to remove 10 gigatons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere annually by 2050. The report, prepared in collaboration between RMI, and the Earth Fund, will help researchers, governments, and communities align on how to safely and efficiently scale technological approaches to remove greenhouse gases (GHGs) from the atmosphere. 

Communities around the world are working hard to cut GHG emissions, by increasing energy efficiency, switching to renewable energy sources, managing landfills, and other strategies. These efforts need to be supported and ramped up. 

We also need to support complementary efforts to remove GHGs already in the atmosphere. As the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) states, carbon dioxide removal is now necessary to reach our climate goals. If deployed sustainably and responsibly, these technologies can also deliver other benefits to the environment, economy, and local communities. 

The roadmap contains critical insights for achieving ambitious GHG removal goals, identifying priorities across technologies and sectors.  We hope it will serve as a valuable coordination tool for the GHG removal ecosystem in 2025 and beyond, as it works to establish new science, new tools, and a new industry. 

Click the link in bio to learn more.

Europe, the United States, and China are leading the way on cleantech manufacturing and deployment.

However, there is significant regional variation within these three economies when it comes to renewable energy deployment, electric vehicle adoption, electrification, and cleantech manufacturing. The territories that have become local leaders in these areas are achieving the benefits more quickly, including economic growth and competitiveness, lower energy costs, energy security, and improved public health. 

In a new report, RMI looks at the energy system transformation at the bloc, region, and territory levels. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

US clean energy projects surged in 2024. Local governments, businesses, and other organizations used federal support to transition to clean energy and bring related benefits to their communities, including lower electricity bills, employment opportunities, and reduced pollution. 

In Montana, Big Sky Sustainability Network Organization, in partnership with Riverside LLLP, installed solar panels on affordable housing and took advantage of federal incentives with support from RMI’s Clean Energy Academy, reducing residents’ electricity costs. 

The City of San Antonio, Texas developed one of the largest municipal on-site solar projects in the country, thanks to RMI’s technical assistance. The project will offset 13% of City operations’ electricity consumption and save $7-11 million over its lifetime. 

The Port of Los Angeles, California – the country’s busiest trade gateway – is planning to upgrade to zero-emissions equipment, infrastructure, and vehicles, as well as support workforce development and community engagement, thanks to the EPA’s Clean Ports Program. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how RMI helped organizations across the country get the most out of the Inflation Reduction Act in 2024.

As we continue working toward a clean energy future, it’s important to recognize areas where we’ve seen progress. From falling battery prices to methane-detecting satellites, this past year saw historic advances in renewables, storage, pollution reduction, and more -- around the world. 

To celebrate the new year, we’re counting down with ten of the top climate developments of 2024. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Petrochemicals, including plastics, account for 3-10% of global climate pollution. While people tend to notice visible plastic pollution — such as plastic bags — they’re often unaware of the climate risks that come from producing these products. 

Over the past week, RMI has highlighted the climate risks related to petrochemicals and plastics, along with the opportunities to create a cleaner chemicals and plastics industry. 

Learn more about RMI’s work to reduce emissions from plastics and petrochemicals at our new hub page at the link in our bio!

Despite the rise in “fast-fashion”— low-cost, low-quality clothing that doesn’t last long — consumers and retailers alike have said they want greener alternatives. However, in order to develop sustainable alternatives to polyester and other textiles, there are key challenges that need to be addressed: From the costs of cleaner production to consistent emissions reporting, we learned from key stakeholders in the polyester value chain about the challenges and opportunities to develop a lower-emissions chemicals market: 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

As we continue through plastics and petrochemicals week here at RMI, we can't forget to talk about one of the most common plastic materials, polyester. Made of polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyester is one of the higher-polluting plastic materials and now compromises over half of global textile fiber production with an annual production volume of approximately 73 million tons. 

The problem with polyester is that most polyester fibers aren’t biodegradable. And the little that can “degrade down” still ends up as microplastics, creating big challenges to manage polyester waste.  The good news is many consumers are willing to pay a premium for sustainable goods. By evaluating where emissions, and value, are created in the polyester value chain we can help create a market for clothing and textiles that are better for our planet. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

We may often think that all plastics come from the same place, but they simply have different uses. The reality is that different plastics have different manufacturing processes leading to sometimes drastic differences in their climate impact. 

Research into the lifecycle of plastics has shown the full production power sources, method of oil or gas extraction, type of oil or gas being extracted, and the methane management practices of upstream operators can drastically affect the carbon intensity of plastics. 

The plastics landscape is complex and diverse, but with improved transparency and traceability we can create a more sustainable industry. 

Click the linking our bio to learn more.

Did you know that every year, petrochemical plants produce over 350 million tons of plastics? 

Making plastics consumes about 12 percent of the world’s oil supply every year, accounting for 3.4 percent of global carbon pollution. For a safer future, decarbonizing the plastics industry is essential — and luckily there are options available to help. 

Use the link in out bio to see how we can reduce pollution from plastics.

From children’s toys, to hi-tech cold weather wear, to seemingly endless packaging, plastic is nearly inescapable this holiday season. And plastic’s dominance isn’t seasonal. Projections of plastics growing use show how far we’re behind on efforts to cut the environmental impact of this material. 

Making the impact of plastic products more visible can help the entire plastics supply chain make better-informed decisions. 

RMI’s new plastics emissions reporting guidance makes that transparency possible, by improving how we measure the impact of every step in the plastics value chain, from fossil fuel extraction to finished products and disposal.

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

‘Petrochemicals.’ It’s a word you may have heard, but do you know what petrochemicals really are?

Well, petrochemicals are made primarily from crude oil and methane gas, and lead to pollution that can harm both our health and the environment. Yet petrochemicals are the building blocks for many products we use in our everyday lives, such as plastics. 

This week we’ll explore the value chain and lifecycle effects of making and using plastics and petrochemicals. And we’ll dive deeper into how we can create more sustainable products for a greener planet. 🌍💚 

Stay tuned!

The Robinyan community in Ogun State is now powered by one of Nigeria’s most forward-looking energy solutions — a 500 kW interconnected minigrid that blends grid electricity with solar PV and battery storage. 

Commissioned by Sahara Power Group, Ikeja Electric, and Darway Coast, and supported by GEAPP and RMI, this project signals a broader shift: 

🔅 From grid-constrained service to distributed resilience 
🔅 From top-down utility models to collaborative innovation 
🔅 From energy access gaps to inclusive, sustainable development 

This project shows what’s possible when technical innovation meets community need — and why scaling interconnected minigrids is critical to Nigeria’s energy future.

In Ijoko, Ogun State, residents once faced near-constant power outages. Today, thanks to a pioneering interconnected minigrid model, power is no longer a luxury — it's reliable, sustainable, and here to stay. 

The Robinyan interconnected minigrid, developed by Darway Coast and Ikeja Electric with GEAPP and RMI providing technical and financial support, deploys 500 kW of solar and 630 kWh of battery storage to meet residential, commercial, and institutional energy needs. 

The project proves the viability of distributed energy resources and was built with deep community engagement, ensuring local trust and sustainability. 

Learn more about this project and the importance of interconnected minigrids through RMI’s new report through the link in our bio.

When power returned to Toto, it wasn’t just a win for one community — it was proof that integrated, public-private energy solutions can scale for impact. 

Toto Interconnected Minigrid, named AFSIA 2023 Minigrid Project of the Year, with 352 kW solar + 972 kWh battery storage (enough to power ~1000 homes and businesses), is a model for how smart infrastructure and community-centered planning can deliver real impact. 

🎬 See how a strong collaboration restored reliable electricity to a town that had none for over 10 years. 

Want to learn more? Check out RMI's recent report to discover the importance of interconnected minigrids in bringing reliable power to communities around the world through the link in our bio.

Marginal wells—low-producing oil and gas wells that extract less than eight bathtubs worth of oil or gas per day—are spread across the United States and are usually overlooked by elected officials and regulators. Nearly one in ten Americans lives in a county with over 1,000 marginal wells. These wells can be poorly maintained, leading to negative health effects on surrounding communities that outweigh the economic benefits of extracting such small amounts of oil or gas. 

And, like all leaky oil and gas systems, marginal wells can be a significant source of methane emissions that can cause fires and explosions, contribute to smog, and heat the planet over 80 times more powerfully than carbon dioxide 

Click the link in our bio to explore RMI’s Marginal Wells Map and see how many marginal wells there are in your state.
