🌺 This May, we honor the leadership and legacy of Asian American and Pacific Islander communities — and the bold climate action underway across Asia and the Pacific. 

Across the Pacific Islands, many nations are shifting away from costly diesel imports to generate electricity and moving towards clean, resilient energy systems such as solar-powered minigrids. RMI is proud to support this transformation — helping governments and communities build systems that work better, for both people and the planet. 

Many countries are aiming for 100% renewable electricity — a powerful signal to the world of what’s possible when vision meets action.

California is an economic powerhouse, responsible for more than 14 percent of US GDP. The state has the industrial sectors to match, with refineries, trucks, airports, ports, and other industries that drive the economy, but also contribute 80 million metric tons of CO2 emissions per year. 

More than 30 percent of California’s emissions come from heavy industry and heavy transport, including aviation, shipping, and heavy-duty trucks. The good news is that California is uniquely positioned to decarbonize these sectors. The state has some of the most ambitious environmental regulations in the nation, and project developers are taking advantage of this policy environment, with over 60 industrial decarbonization projects announced across the state. 

RMI and Mission Possible Partnership, in partnership with the @BezosEarthFund, have been working on a clean industrial hub in California, providing technical assistance, analysis, and facilitation to directly support 10 first-of-a-kind industrial decarbonization projects. On Earth Day, they gathered to celebrate their successes and discuss the journey ahead. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

As organic waste such as food scraps and yard clippings decomposes in landfills, it generates methane — a greenhouse gas with over 80 times the heat-trapping power of carbon dioxide in the short term. In California, landfills are the second-largest source of human-caused methane emissions, representing 22 percent of the state’s 2022 methane inventory.

One way to stop these methane emissions is to keep organic waste from reaching landfills in the first place. In January 2022, California implemented state law SB 1383 with the goal of cutting landfill-bound organic waste 75 percent by 2025. The landmark law has already diverted 295 million pounds of food waste from landfills, created over 440 jobs in food recovery, and provided millions of meals to those in need. 

At OC Waste & Recycling (OCWR), composting operations have diverted over 64,000 tons of organic waste, turning it into free compost for the public. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

More than 10 million US families rely on fuels delivered regularly by truck — such as oil and propane — to provide heat in the winter and hot water year-round. Electric heat pumps can deliver the same heating services using about one-fourth the energy. Overall, the higher efficiency of air-source heat pumps and heat-pump water heaters can help save households an average of $970 per year on their energy bills. 

With the right mix of policies and programs, millions of households could benefit by upgrading to heat pumps. Such a shift would unlock thousands in cost savings and other benefits for Americans. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Cities in states that prioritize the buildout of abundant, affordable transportation options have less traffic — saving time, cutting costs and pollution, and creating vibrant, connected communities that attract economic development. 

For example, since 2013, the City of Minneapolis has enacted policy to build 199 miles of bikeways and expand bus and light rail service, leading to a three percent decrease in car traffic between 2019 and 2024, even as population increased by over 118,000 in the same time period. 

Minnesota lawmakers have adopted a series of laws to scale Minneapolis’ framework statewide. RMI analysis quantified the benefits of transportation choice abundance and found Minnesotan households stand to save $1,720 annually from avoided gas, maintenance, and depreciation costs due to the availability of more affordable ways to get around. 

Following in Minnesota’s tracks, many states are joining the race to unlock the cost savings of multimodal transportation planning. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

The SAFc Registry, Chooose, and Alaska Airlines are partnering to streamline sustainable aviation certificate transactions by launching a first-of-a-kind software integration! 🛫 

The SAFc Registry was launched in 2023 to help create consistency, transparency, and auditability of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) certificates. By partnering with the Registry, Chooose will give its aviation and logistics partners access to robust environmental attribute tracking, while still having the ability to receive, transfer, and retire SAFc directly from the Chooose platform. 

Alaska Airlines, an industry leader in SAF adoption, is the first to utilize this integration for their SAF program, expanding access to alternative fuels and giving their customers a simple way to take action on travel and transport emissions. 

The collaboration between Chooose, SAFc Registry, and Alaska Airlines is setting a new benchmark for the SAF industry, and helping pave the way for a more transparent, scalable, and efficient future for aviation sustainability. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about the SAFc registry.

In uncertain times, it helps to take a breath and look at the data. One great reference point is the @internationalenergyagency (IEA), and this year’s Global Energy Review provided some striking insights on clean energy in 2024. In sum? The trends are positive, but there is still a lot more to do. 

Click the link in our bio to read about five big things that stood out from this year’s report.

Investment in clean energy technology from both private and public sources has climbed steadily across the United States over the past six years, according to RMI analysis of data from Clean Investment Monitor. 

From 2018 to 2024, the flow of investment, new jobs, and related growth has tilted toward red states: Republican-voting states took in $650 billion in clean energy investment; Democratic-voting states received $440 billion. As of March 2024, total investment had created nearly 349,000 related jobs. 

However, many of those benefits are now at risk as the federal government rolls back funding and support for clean energy. In the first two months of 2025, more than $5 billion in investment was withdrawn, Clean Economy Tracker estimates. 

From domestic manufacturing growth to job opportunities, Americans across the country stand to benefit from investment in clean energy. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Testing shows that super-efficient ACs use 60% less energy than typical AC units while enhancing occupant comfort, according to a new real-world study conducted by Global Cooling Efficiency Accelerator (GCEA) in Palava City, India, in partnership with @lodhagroup_india and @CEPTuniversity1. The study also uncovered critical gaps in current testing standards, which currently underestimate the extra energy used by typical ACs when attempting to control humidity. 

As global temperatures continue to rise, so too will the demand for air conditioning. GCEA — a coalition of scientists and cooling experts — is working to build a pathway to support the commercialization of super-efficient air conditioners that are optimized for managing humidity and use less energy than conventional ACs. 

Click the link in our bio to read this latest study, whcih brings us one step closer to commercializing affordable, accessible, super-efficient ACs that can help provide cooling to all, without putting undue stress on the electricity grid.

Methane waste causes a lot of issues for communities, consumers, and companies alike, but how can we manage this excess waste? 

Measuring methane offers the best way to manage methane waste and can help educate companies and policy makers on where to tackle leaks and other super-emitter spots. However, to really get a complete picture of methane emissions, we have to take a look at a combination of technologies from the ground, air, and space. 🛰️ 

RMI’s newest macrograph shows exactly what technologies are being deployed to address methane waste, and deep dives into the way each technology can help measure and continually improve methane detection around the globe. Click the link in our bio to check it out!

Clean energy is growing for many reasons. It delivers new jobs, spurs growth, and strengthens economic resilience. And surveys show the shift to clean energy is supported by most people in most countries. 

For over 40 years, RMI has been working with governments, companies, and communities to expand these benefits. This Earth Day, we are celebrating a few of our many projects that have helped bring cleaner, more affordable, and resilient energy to people everywhere. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Colorado just introduced one of the most effective landfill methane rules in the country, bringing long-overdue relief to nearby communities and setting a new standard for other states to follow. 

It’s an economic win, too: every dollar spent is expected to return $6 in avoided health and climate costs, and the rule will support hundreds of new jobs across the state. 

Methane is a potent greenhouse gas that is formed as buried food scraps and organic waste break down beneath a landfill’s surface. Methane leaks from landfills raise concerns not only about the warming impacts, but also about nearby residents' exposure to hazardous air pollutants and smog. 

Following the lead of California, Oregon, Maryland, Michigan, and Washington, Colorado is the latest state to propose comprehensive landfill methane controls. Its rule could serve as inspiration as more states look to improve air quality, protect public health, and rein in methane pollution. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Methane has a warming potential 80x that of carbon dioxide over 20 years. Now climate scientists, political leaders, and communities have realized that reducing methane emissions offers the single biggest opportunity to address near-term warming. 

Luckily, there are many opportunities to reduce methane waste and emissions. From plugging oil and gas wells, to rethinking waste disposal, and activating markets, we have many ways to lower methane emissions. But first we have to identify where the excess is coming from. How do we fix something that we can’t see? 

Learn more about making the invisible, visible here at the link in our bio, and stay tuned for our new series called #MethaneMonday where we’ll talk about all things methane.

A circular electric vehicle battery economy, where end-of-life EV batteries are reused, repurposed, or recycled, can help ensure a responsible, resilient supply chain as demand for EVs grows. 

To fully leverage this opportunity, we need to shift how we think about the battery supply chain: 

🔋 Improve vehicle and battery design, efficiency, and recyclability to get the most out of mined minerals. 

🔋 Implement policies and market-based solutions to communicate the demand for and value of recycled materials. 

🔋 Invest in domestic EV battery recycling and midstream manufacturing to strengthen the supply chain’s resilience, increase energy security, and boost the economy. 

RMI’s Battery Circular Economy Initiative Dashboards provide stakeholders with the data and information they need to support the creation of a circular battery economy. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Last year was a year of virtual power plant (VPP) action in the United States! 

As the power system faces demand growth and extreme weather events, VPP programs aggregate distributed energy resources — electric vehicles, batteries, smart thermostats, and more — to provide critical grid services and maintain and enhance grid reliability, resilience, safety, and affordability. 

Over the course of 2024: 

🏛️ Utility commissions in 10 states and DC took action to expand deployment of VPPs 

📃 Ten state legislatures introduced VPP bills and four states passed VPP legislation 

⚡ Utilities in 34 states and Puerto Rico took meaningful action to initiate or expand VPPs 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how RMI’s VP3 program is catalyzing industry and change to scale VPPs in ways that lower electricity costs and strengthen the power grid.

There are more than 1 billion tons of underutilized biomass — grain straw, wood from wildfire thinnings, recycled cardboard, and other natural materials— generated by US farms, forests, and landfills every year. This low-value biomass can be transformed into cost-competitive, sustainable building products for wall panels, flooring, and insulation that are market-ready and help make affordable healthy housing, durably store millions of tons of carbon, and create thousands of new jobs and $79 billion in economic growth opportunities. 

A manufacturing transformation in the building products market that takes advantage of available biomass feedstocks would also increase incomes for farmers and foresters, improve air quality, and reduce the environmental hazards that pose a risk to American homes and buildings. 

RMI’s analysis delves into the multiple benefits of converting underutilized biomass into valuable building materials and presents case studies of affordable new homes built with the types of biomaterials featured in our modeling, alongside examples of recently established manufacturing facilities for such products across the United States.

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Traditional energy efficiency programs often focus on material and appliance upgrades. These measures are important, but energy efficiency solutions extend beyond specific products. 

Considering the entire system instead of focusing on singular technology substitutions within the existing system can help energy efficiency upgrades reduce energy waste, lower costs, and lead to broader benefits across sectors. For example, addressing how cities are developed, organized, and managed is a powerful leverage point that influences the buildings, mobility, and freight transportation sectors, plus many others, while also improving human health and safety. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how energy efficiency opportunities are interconnected.

RMI is excited to welcome @Google as the newest member of the Sustainable Steel Buyers Platform! 

SSBP is a coalition of leading corporations that are accelerating steel decarbonization by collaborating on sustainable iron and steel procurement. Google uses steel to build datacenters, buildings, and related infrastructure. Sustainable steel offers Google a great choice to reduce the tech company’s overall environmental impact. 

As the latest company to be welcomed into SSBP, Google joins seven other members that together are signaling strong demand for sustainable steel. First-mover companies are leading the charge to help establish a strong market in the United States, where demand for green steel is forecast to increase to ~7 megatons a year by the end of this decade. 

To learn more about SSBP and the work that RMI is doing to drive sustainable steel production and purchasing, visit the link in our bio.

Women’s voices are critical to helping create innovative solutions to accelerate the energy transition, and the work they do gives us hope for the future. 

Take a minute to listen to women speak about their work at RMI, the importance of mentors, and how women can inspire and educate future generations. 

Featuring 🎥 Alex Wilkins, Catherine Coleman Flowers, Leia Guccione, Marty Pickett, Siana Teelucksingh, and Sally P.

Clean energy passed a major milestone in 2024: For the first time wind and solar produced more electricity than coal in the United States. The costs of renewable energy resources have fallen by 70–90 percent over the past decade, often making them cheaper to deploy than coal, gas, or oil. 

And cheaper generation usually means cheaper electricity costs for consumers, although many factors go into the final price. In Texas, renewables supply 35 percent of all power and help consumers and businesses save $20 million per day, as compared to fossil-fuel based generation. 

In addition to cost savings, renewable energy also creates jobs and business opportunities, enhances electricity reliability, and improves air quality. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Navigating a career? It doesn't have to be a solo mission — just ask Melissa Le Blanc.

From her early academic days to her role at FortisTCI in Turks and Caicos, Le Blanc's journey into renewable energy shows just how powerful mentorship and strong networks can be. 🌍⚡

Now, she’s giving back — mentoring the next generation of women in renewable energy through the Women in Renewable Energy (WIRE) Network. 💪

💬 “Every step of the way, it just seemed to keep working out. But it wasn't just luck — it was my network. It was people that had my back every step of the way.” - Melissa Le Blanc

🔗 Tap the link in bio to read how mentorship is shaping the future of Caribbean energy.

🇮🇳 India’s trucking demand is set to quadruple by 2050, with over 320,000 new trucks hitting the roads each year. To improve air quality & lower operation costs, the path forward is clear: zero-emission trucks (ZETs). 

ZETs can cut fuel costs by 46%, reduce diesel use by 838 billion liters, and avoid 3.8 gigatons of CO₂ (that’s as much as the EU’s annual emissions!). The adoption of ZETs could also strengthen India as a global battery hub and leader in clean freight. 

🔗 Read our latest ZET article to learn more. (Link in bio)

Carbon credits can help support climate solutions—but only if we have the right data. 

The voluntary carbon market funds solutions like protecting forests and removing carbon from the atmosphere. But with confusing and inconsistent data, it’s hard to tell which credits are actually making a difference. 

At RMI, we’re working to change that. We’ve created an open framework that makes carbon credit data clearer, more accessible, and easier to verify. By improving transparency, we can help businesses, project developers, and communities make smarter, more effective climate investments. 

A stronger, more trusted carbon market means more real impact. Read more about how at link in bio.

In coming years, the US electric grid is going to require significant investments to meet increasing electricity demand from the proliferation of data centers, renewed manufacturing growth, and beneficial electrification. At the same time, upgrades and repairs will be necessary for the grid to remain resilient in the face of extreme weather events, such as heat waves and storms.

RMI’s latest report creates a comprehensive framework of cost control strategies that regulators and utility stakeholders across the United States can use to make sure that we are making only the smartest financial investments to deliver reliable power. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Methane leaks are a common occurrence that wastes energy, and costs consumers—and oil and gas companies—a lot of money 💰. But why does methane leak, when are companies not aware, how can we spot it, and how can we fix the problem? 

Methane leaks from many different places, including faulty equipment, pressure changes underground, and from old, barely-producing marginal and abandoned wells. While some of these leaks are small, others can be huge causing a whopping 66 percent of the gas produced to be thrown away. The good news is that solutions, like equipment replacement, routine monitoring, open certification, and buyers' prioritizing the procurement of low-leakage gas, can stop leakage and cut methane waste. 

At #CERAWeek, energy solutions across the board were discussed, including how oil and gas can aid in the energy transition. RMI’s latest article takes a dive deep into why methane leaks, and what companies can do to fix leaks and save money. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Banks and investors are under growing pressure to understand how the shift to cleaner energy will affect the companies they finance. But the corporate transition assessments they currently use to evaluate how prepared a company is for the energy transition are too high-level. They need deeper, more actionable evaluations so they can make smarter decisions about risk, capital allocation, and client engagement. 

We recommend two key improvements: 
1. Strengthen analytical depth beyond big-picture items to dig into crucial details like specific assets, technologies, or business activities. 

2. Develop more actionable insights that go beyond basic scoring systems to provide information that directly informs financial decisions like risk analysis, capital allocation, and client engagement. 

In a recent whitepaper, we outline nine key components that contribute to more robust assessments and three approaches in particular that can help. 

Read it at the link in our bio.

From the Arctic Circle to the Caribbean, energy leaders are driving innovative solutions in some of the most remote and challenging environments in the world. 

The Energy Leadership Accelerator (ELA), a partnership between the Alaska Center for Energy and Power and RMI, brought together fellows from across Alaska, Hawaii, Puerto Rico, the US Virgin Islands, and mainland tribal communities to exchange ideas, tackle shared energy challenges, and strengthen local capacity. 

Watch to see the experience unfold and learn more about the ELA through the link in our bio.

In the summer, millions of Americans see their energy bills soar due to the need to cool their homes. Concerns about utility disconnections loom large during these periods of extreme heat: Utilities disconnect power far more frequently in the summer than other times of year, and most heat-related deaths occur indoors. 

As extreme heat becomes more frequent and severe, state policymakers can strengthen safeguards to protect Americans from the health, financial, and safety threats that can occur when their electricity and/or gas is shut off due to their inability to pay their monthly bill. 

States can adopt weather protections to prohibit shutoffs once temperatures reach certain thresholds. Making energy more affordable can also help tackle disconnections at the root. 

Click the link in our bio to read RMI’s latest report on policy options to reduce the risks and impacts of electricity shutoffs.

As kids head back to the classroom, one Baltimore school has been benefiting the community year-round in a way you might not expect: lowering families’ electricity bills. 

Instead of powering the building below, solar panels on the roof of Baltimore’s Elmer A Henderson: A Johns Hopkins Partnership School (Henderson-Hopkins) are part of a community solar project, where students’ families, staff, and the wider community have the chance to subscribe to the project and take advantage of both clean energy generation and discounted electricity for their homes. 

From the educational opportunities for students to learn more about rooftop solar, to the subscribers who could save an estimated $200 per year, this project shows that clean energy can have far-reaching benefits for all.

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Our vision of the future inspires us to THINK bigger, DO boldly, and SCALE globally. 

At RMI, we are committed to reducing energy waste, improving access to affordable clean energy, and slashing harmful climate pollution. We are inspired by our co-founder, Amory Lovins, who reminded us recently that “the most solid foundation for feeling better about the future is to improve it tangibly, durably, reproducibly, scalably.” 

In the past few months, we have: 

☀️ Offered updated guidance on designing hurricane-resilient solar energy systems for regions prone to extreme weather 

🦺️ Released a series of studies that outline how sectors like construction, mining, and water treatment can integrate carbon removal into daily operations 

🌍 Revealed how emerging climate solutions — from advanced cooling and carbon-storing materials to decentralized renewables — can drive economic growth, cut air pollution, and lower energy costs 

➕ And more! 

Read about our latest work in our quarterly impact report through the link in our bio.

Electric vehicle (EV) ownership is accelerating worldwide. More than 17 million new electric cars were sold across the globe in 2024, 20 percent of all new cars purchased. 

🚘 When federal tax credits are applied, RMI analysis determined that EVs can save US drivers an estimated $1,000 or more annually. However, even without federal tax credits in the United States (which will sunset September 30, 2025), owning a light-duty EV can still be cheaper over a vehicle’s lifespan than owning a gas-powered car thanks to ongoing savings from fuel, less maintenance, and other recurring benefits. 

🚚 The number of electric medium- and heavy-duty trucks also continues to grow globally as their purchase prices trend toward parity with diesel. This cost parity is the determining factor for the switch to electric, given how price sensitive trucking fleets are. 

🔋 In addition, battery recycling technology is improving. We estimate that enough battery minerals will be in circulation by 2040 to reduce or possibly eliminate the need for additional mining — a sharp contrast to the ongoing oil extraction needs of gas-powered vehicles. 

Learn more about the positive trends in EV adoption, both globally and in the United States through the link in our bio.

Air conditioners are becoming a necessity as dangerous heat and humidity events become more common. But to manage humidity, they often have to *over* cool the air — an extremely inefficient process. 

To spur innovation in AC design, RMI co-launched the Global Cooling Prize in 2018. Prize winners Gree and Daikin have since partnered with RMI and The Global Cooling Efficiency Accelerator to test their units in India over nine months of varied weather conditions. The results showed these machines cut energy use by a whopping 60%, while effectively tackling the humidity problem. 

Another challenge is that "international standards for residential air conditioners don’t yet measure the energy used to remove humidity,” says RMI cooling expert @Ankit Kalanki. RMI is working with the international standards organization to bring dehumidification into the conversation. 

Carrying this momentum forward, startups like Blue Frontier and Transaera — supported by RMI’s climate tech accelerator, Third Derivative — are pioneering advanced technologies that could drive AC efficiency gains even further. 

Check out the Fast Company article at the link in our bio to learn more.

Resilient clean energy solutions can meet rising electricity demand from data centers. But there is a high-profile effort to keep retiring coal-fired power plants running in order to meet this demand. However, coal plants tend to be old, unreliable, and inflexible. 

Not only are they expensive to maintain and modernize, but coal plants also fail to deliver under stress — a vulnerability that is worsened by extreme weather — and they struggle with sudden load shifts, a poor match for the dynamic and often unpredictable nature of data center demand. 

A range of cleaner, more resilient solutions is already available and scalable to support the needs of a digital economy.

RMI’s Clean Energy Incentive Hub is now updated with the latest information about recently changed federal incentives to help households, businesses, local governments, nonprofits, and other organizations save money on energy efficiency, electrification, and renewable energy projects. See what incentives are available to generate cost and energy savings before they expire. 

States can play a key role in helping their constituents reduce costs before federal support ends. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Because it’s the main component of natural gas, identifying and repairing methane leaks in the oil and gas supply chain improves efficiency and avoids product loss. That’s a win for business, climate, and public health. 

Methane gas leaks are found throughout oil and gas operations, including from well heads, pumps, tanks, pipelines, compressors, and flares. An array of sensing technologies on the ground, in the air, and in space can work in concert to find where methane escapes. From there, many of these leaks can be repaired with existing cost-effective improvements. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how to reduce methane emissions from oil and gas. 

#MethaneMonday

Around 96 percent of manufactured goods are produced using chemicals. This is why global chemical manufacturing is a $4.7 trillion industry — and one of the most polluting industries. 

RMI and Third Derivative launched the Industrial Innovation Cohorts to reduce emissions in heavy industry by supporting startups pursuing solutions in the cement, steel, and chemicals sectors. 

With the support of investors and companies, the chemicals sector can reduce its emissions by: 
➡️ Making Less 
➡️ Making Better 
➡️ Making New 

Read “Catalyzing Change: The New Wave of Innovation in the Chemicals Sector” at the link in our bio to learn how.

Extreme heat and humidity are pushing records — and our air conditioners are pushing the grid to its limit. 

Most ACs weren’t built for this climate. They waste energy, struggle with humidity, and use polluting refrigerants. But a new era of cooling solutions is rising to meet the challenge: 

🆒 Super-efficient, next-gen ACs tested in real conditions 
🔌 Virtual power plants that ease grid strain 
🧑‍🔬 Cooling startups reimagining the materials and technologies behind AC 

Together, these grid-friendly solutions can help us meet cooling demand without increasing energy-use and emissions. Head to the link in our bio to learn more.

Recent heat waves across the United States have underscored the urgent need to keep people safe from extreme temperatures. As RMI’s Laila Atalla puts it, “Cooling is going from being a luxury or a ‘nice to have’ in many places to being essential for health and safety.” But many households can’t afford air-conditioning and need help. 

In a recent Bloomberg News article, Atalla points out how pairing free air conditioner programs with heat pumps could make a big difference in energy savings for households. At the same time, state and utility energy efficiency incentive programs should be streamlined to ensure easier access for people who don’t have the time and resources to navigate them. 

Read the article at the link in our bio.

Affordable electricity is a concern around the world, with good cause: US natural gas prices are expected to double between 2024 and 2026, according to yesterday’s short-term energy outlook from the US Energy Information Administration. This trend is flatter overseas, but it is still an example of gas power’s volatility. 

On the other hand, clean energy is becoming even more affordable, as shown by recent United Nations and International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) reports. More than 90 percent of last year’s global projects were cheaper than their fossil fuel alternatives on a levelized cost basis. 

With electricity demand on the rise, it is more important than ever to make choices that cut costs, use energy efficiently, and promote affordability over volatility. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

The seemingly simple question — “What types of carbon credits are available?” — is surprisingly hard to answer. That’s why RMI’s Carbon Markets Initiative just launched a new interactive tool that maps the full landscape of credit types. It makes comparisons between credit types easy, explores market and geographic trends, and identifies the key risks and quality criteria that matter most for each credit type. 
 
What you’ll find: 

• Sector-level overview: A high-level snapshot of credit activity organized by the climate sectors where emissions occur. 

• Sub-sector credit types: A breakdown of credit types within each sector, helpful for those targeting specific emissions but still exploring credit type options. 

• Technical explainers: Deep dives into each credit type’s market context, quality risks, and mitigation strategies. Ideal for buyers doing due diligence on a specific project. 

Explore the carbon credit landscape at the link in our bio.

States that take the lead on supporting industrial decarbonization — the electrification and efficiency upgrades of production processes — can help build a cleaner, more competitive industrial future while attracting new investment, creating high-quality jobs, and positioning themselves as hubs for clean manufacturing. 

Industrial sectors, from cement to food processing, have different needs, technologies, and constraints, which means that decarbonizing industry is not one-size-fits-all. But there are some common themes: the transition will require significant investment, updated regulations, and a reliable supply of clean electricity. 

As worldwide demand for clean technologies and low-emission products increases, states can play a pivotal role in whether American industries stay competitive. A recent RMI analysis of industrial decarbonization pathways in the state of Washington offers valuable lessons for states across the country.

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Worldwide demand for low-emissions technologies and products is growing. States that support clean industry will be better positioned to take advantage of this trend and export goods to global markets, as well as improve air quality for state residents. 

With comprehensive planning and evaluations, states can better understand where their industrial emissions are coming from, which technologies can best promote decarbonization, and what policies unlock investment decisions. States can then strategically leverage their existing infrastructure and workforces to make sure that their industries gain or maintain a competitive advantage in shifting international markets with tightening carbon emission requirements. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how states can modernize industry, improve health, and reduce climate pollution.

States across America can take action to measure and manage methane emissions from often-overlooked low-producing, “marginal” oil and gas wells. 

Over half a million marginal wells are spread out across the United States. These wells produce small amounts of oil and gas and, like all oil and gas systems, they are a significant source of methane, which causes fires and explosions, contributes to smog, and heats the planet over 80 times more powerfully than carbon dioxide. 

Many low-producing wells run the risk of being abandoned without being properly shut down, ultimately passing those costs on to taxpayers and becoming a liability for communities, governments, and companies. Greater transparency and stricter oversight of marginal wells would help prioritize proper closures, avoid abandonment, and explore potential opportunities for repurposing these sites in ways that benefit nearby communities. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Landfills produce methane, a climate super-polluter, along with other harmful chemicals. For the more than 36 million Americans living within three miles of a landfill, it's a major public health concern. That’s why reducing landfill pollution is one of the most impactful and cost-effective ways to slow near-term warming while protecting our lungs, our water, and boosting the local economy. And local governments can implement solutions today. 

City waste sites can use proven best practices and cost-effective technologies to protect public health from landfill methane emissions by upgrading local landfill design or advancing solutions through private sector contracts. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about the top five opportunities to cut landfill methane pollution today.

State regulators can enact energy poverty and cost control policies that make energy more affordable for their customers. 

One in three US households reports going without food or medicine to pay household energy bills. As energy costs continue to rise — averaging 4.8 percent per year from 2019 to 2023 and outpacing inflation — state public utility commissions (PUCs) have the power to implement safeguard policies that help customers afford their bills, including percentage-of-income payment plans (PIPPs), low-income discount rates, arrearage management plans (AMPs), and low-income energy efficiency support. 

Beyond these more targeted measures, PUCs can reduce systemwide costs and support affordability with utility return on equity (ROE) reforms, economic dispatch, and clean repowering. 

Link in bio for more.

State legislators, regulators, and governors’ offices can take action to improve the electricity system and promote reliable, affordable, and clean electricity tailored to their state’s needs. A framework for taking effective action includes three main components: 

⚡ On-the-ground solutions: implementing technologies, infrastructure, and operations such as clean generation and improved grid management. 

🤝 Policy and market enabling conditions: supporting regulatory, economic, and institutional environments that strengthen the feasibility, effectiveness, and impact of on-the-ground solutions. 

🏛️ State levers: advancing solutions directly or creating the appropriate conditions with state mechanisms. 

RMI’s Electricity Action Framework and Menu for States provides guidance on how these elements can be combined to support specific state priorities, plus practical examples that states can use to get started. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Clean technologies are an economic growth opportunity. Following recent US federal policy shifts, states can still develop and deploy cleantech to save money, improve health, and position their businesses and residents to thrive. 

RMI highlights six recommendations for states to prioritize: 

🚗 Convert specific fleets to electric vehicles immediately 

🏗️ Speed up permitting and build clean energy faster 

🤝 Fill financial gaps with public-private partnerships and aggregated purchasing 

💰 Prioritize energy affordability for consumers 

🏡 Launch home energy rebates 

💡 Attract manufacturing and lead cleantech innovation 

States must move fast to bring in the billions of dollars available in federal support for these technologies, all of which have a closing window of availability. Click the link in our bio to learn more.

In the voluntary carbon market (VCM), finding trustworthy information isn’t easy. Project details are often buried in PDFs, scattered across sources, or shared in different formats, making it hard to know which carbon credits are truly delivering on their climate promises. 

That changes now ✋ 

RMI’s Carbon Crediting Data Framework (CCDF) brings order to this chaos by offering an open-source, standardized structure to organize, assess, and compare data on carbon credits. Built from over 160 market and scientific sources, the CCDF helps VCM stakeholders align around a common data language. 

📊 In Chapter 5, explore how Centigrade, a data utility, is already using the framework to visualize the performance of over 6 million credits. 

💡Then head to Chapter 6 to learn about potential applications and opportunities to build on the CCDF. 

Click the link in our bio to see how the CCDF could support your work.

🗓️ July 24, 2025, is this year’s Earth Overshoot Day — the point at which humanity has consumed all the resources that Earth can regenerate in one year. Everything we use from July 25 through December is “on ecological credit,” drawing down forests, soils, and biodiversity. 

Over the past decade, the date has hovered around late July, offering a striking reminder: we’re consistently overshooting by nearly five months every year. The biggest driver? Carbon pollution. The good news: we know what it takes to change course. Decarbonizing energy systems, cutting food waste, investing in regenerative land use and biodiversity, and building efficient, low-carbon infrastructure are all methods we can take to reduce our burden on the planet. 

At RMI, we’re advancing practical solutions that address overshoot at its core — by driving deep decarbonization, energy equity, and systems-level change. 

🌱 Every action matters — and collective momentum can move this date in the right direction.

As organic waste such as food scraps and yard clippings decomposes in landfills, it generates methane — a greenhouse gas with over 80 times the heat-trapping power of carbon dioxide in the short term. Addressing methane emissions from landfills helps prevent superheating the planet while also protecting public health and keeping air and water clean. 

In California, landfills are the second-largest source of human-caused methane emissions, representing 22 percent of the state’s 2022 methane inventory. The state has a plan to reduce methane leaks from the waste sector, and @ocwaste (OCWR) is leading the way by turning organic waste into compost while making technology upgrades to boost methane capture at the landfill. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Electricity demand growth is projected to surge due to AI data centers, but the actual extent of that growth remains to be seen. Data centers account for just 2 percent of global electricity demand today and are forecasted to account for approximately 10 percent of electricity demand growth over the next six years. 

Data centers can affect local electricity demand more immediately when they are concentrated in specific regions. Fortunately, local jurisdictions and utilities have a range of options available to responsibly meet and manage load growth with fast, low-cost solutions, including energy efficiency upgrades, enhanced flexibility, and alternative transmission technologies. 

When data centers are willing to invest in fast, flexible, and affordable electricity solutions, utilities, regulators, and policymakers can balance economic opportunities with electricity reliability and affordability for all. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Cool drinks without compressors? Comfort without refrigerants? 

🧊 Two startup founders are turning that vision into reality — with help from RMI’s climate tech accelerator, Third Derivative. 

Meet: 
 🌡️ Berardo Matalucci of MIMiC Systems, rethinking HVAC with thermoelectric cooling. 
 🧲 Timur Sirman of Magnotherm, commercializing magnetic refrigeration for the real world. 

In this two-part Q&A, they share: 
 ✔️ The inspiration behind their tech 
 ✔️ Challenges to adoption 
 ✔️ Why now is the moment for change 
 ✔️ How Third Derivative helped turn science into impact 

🌟 Read their stories and explore how solid-state cooling is shaping the future at the link in our bio.

Desalination is the process of removing salt from seawater to make it drinkable — a growing solution to water scarcity worldwide. But what if the same infrastructure could also help clean up our atmosphere? 

Our latest report explores how coastal desalination plants could play a transformative role in carbon dioxide removal. By integrating emerging electrochemical approaches, these facilities can provide much needed freshwater and use brine waste to remove and store CO₂ from the atmosphere or ocean, offering environmental, operational, and waste management benefits. 

Desalination is growing 6%–12% annually, with hundreds of new plants coming online each year — offering a massive deployment opportunity. As freshwater scarcity and climate risks increase globally, aligning desalination with carbon management presents a compelling opportunity to address multiple challenges at once.

Let’s take a look at a large but mainly undercover source of methane emissions: landfills. 

Every year, a staggering 145 million tons of waste is taken to landfills across the United States. Over half of that is comprised of organics like food waste. When that organic waste decomposes, it generates methane, a super-pollutant with over 80 times more heat-trapping potency than carbon dioxide. 

The intensity of the methane problem in landfills depends on their design and operational practices. But the good news is that solutions can quickly be implemented by: 

💻Improving monitoring and controls to mitigate methane waste 
🥔Reducing organic waste disposal to prevent future methane 

Acting now on landfill methane waste will lead to benefits across the spectrum, ranging from protecting nearby communities to saving households money, and even creating revenue and jobs in composting and recycling.

Unlike your dog at home, you actually want these robotic, methane-sensing hounds going through your garbage — once it gets to the landfill, at least. 

As part of its Smart Landfill Program, OC Waste & Recycling (OCWR) uses real-time data assessment and cutting-edge technology, like robotic dogs and drones, to patrol landfill sites and map methane emissions. Compared to traditional monitoring, which requires a technician to walk the site for miles, these methods are safer, more efficient, and more economical. 

OCWR uses this data to proactively identify methane leaks and implement targeted solutions, from expanding gas collection systems to improving cover materials. Finding and managing methane from landfills is important to slow global warming over the near term, securing clean air and water, and protecting public health. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

This June, we honor the strength, leadership, and innovation of Caribbean communities — especially those driving the region’s bold energy transition. 

Island nations across the Caribbean are replacing imported fossil fuels with clean, resilient energy systems. RMI is proud to support this transformation by working alongside governments, utilities, and communities. 

Across the region, RMI has supported: 

🌴 29 completed and 22 renewable energy projects under development, representing a total of over 430 MW of solar generation capacity across 20 island nations 
🌴 Microgrid installations, including a 100% solar-powered system on Ragged Island in the Bahamas and Anegada 
🌴 National energy transition planning to unlock sustainable investments 
🌴 Regional workforce development in partnership with RMI's Energy Transition Academy 
🌴 The development of hurricane-prone solar minigrids for resilient energy infrastructure when needed most 

These efforts reflect the power of local leadership, trusted partnerships, and the shared vision of a more secure, sustainable future. 

Learn more about our work in the region through the link in our bio.

As average energy costs continue to rise, millions of Americans are struggling to afford the energy necessary for basic needs such as heating, cooling, and cooking. In 2024, one in three US households reported cutting back on food, delaying medical care, or keeping their homes at unsafe temperatures in order to pay their household energy bills. 

Despite the magnitude of this problem, there are proven policy solutions. State public utility commissions (PUCs) have the power to implement safeguard policies that help energy-insecure customers afford their bills. 

RMI created the Energy Poverty Policy Simulator (EPPS) to evaluate different policies, their effects on average bills and energy burdens, and their estimated total cost of implementation with clear, data-driven evidence. The EPPS is the first tool that allows decision makers and stakeholders to leverage a suite of energy poverty policies in every state to chart a path to ending energy poverty. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

Drivers in the US are losing an all-time high of 63 hours a year sitting in gridlock traffic — more than a typical work week — despite significant investments in widening freeways. 

Fortunately, we have the technology to slash congestion and save money in the long run by leveraging 21st-century public transit and active transportation solutions. 

New research from the Institute for Transportation and Development Policy demonstrates that investing in bus, rail, and biking infrastructure would help states save an average 10% of their annual transportation expenditures while also improving air quality and reducing traffic. 

Learning from successes in Illinois and Virginia, state-level decision makers have an opportunity to develop target-driven policy frameworks that unlock the benefits of expanded transportation choices. Link in bio.

tag_name: [1] 18. Support Climate Tech Startups In Valleys Of Death
text:
Concrete is the most widely used human-made material on Earth. It is also among the biggest sources of industrial emissions. Producing it accounts for nearly 8 percent of global CO₂ emissions, yet it remains essential for growth, resilience, and infrastructure worldwide. As global construction accelerates, decarbonizing these materials is essential to meeting net-zero goals. 

The latest article from Third Derivative explores how innovation and investment can reshape this critical sector. Through collaboration with major producers, startups are pioneering technologies that reduce emissions across the cement and concrete value chain — advancing solutions that can be scaled globally and tailored locally. 

Read how these innovators are laying the groundwork for a more sustainable future: https://ow.ly/v32550Xw45c
tag1: [1] 18. Support Climate Tech Startups In Valleys Of Death

tag_name: [1] 14. Drive Market For Climate Products And Services
text:
Representatives from around the world are gathering today in Belém, Brazil for #COP30 Agriculture Day. 

As one of the world’s leading agricultural producers, #Brazil relies heavily on nitrogen fertilizer. But with such a high demand, the country has to import most of its supply — mainly from China, Russia, Nigeria, and Oman — leaving it vulnerable to global market swings and geopolitical uncertainty. 

Despite its strong agricultural sector, Brazil has struggled to sustain or expand domestic fertilizer production, largely due to high natural gas prices, a key input for producing ammonia and its fertilizer derivatives. 

As demand continues to rise, these challenges could open the door to a new opportunity: by leveraging its abundant renewable energy resources, Brazil could produce low-emissions fertilizers at home, improving energy security, reducing reliance on imports and ensuring price stability. 

In a new joint publication, RMI and the E+ Institute explore how Brazil can take steps to produce low-carbon fertilizers at home more affordably while advancing its climate goals. 

Learn more 👇  https://ow.ly/c6Tx50XtQJM
tag1: [1] 14. Drive Market For Climate Products And Services

tag_name: [1] 13. Empower Leaders To Fast-Track Clean Energy Transition
text:
Decision makers across light industry (think consumer goods like food, beverage, and clothing) recognize that implementing modern processes like electrification, automation, and smart manufacturing creates value. So why are so many companies still slow to modernize? 

Old-school investment frameworks tend to miss the bigger picture and undercount the benefits of these modern technologies, leading promising projects to be overlooked or undervalued. 

To close this gap, RMI and @schneiderelectric developed a new framework to help light industry investors, policymakers, and business leaders better evaluate electrification and automation projects, beyond just the usual cost/payback. Some of these sources are simply unseen, but if nothing changes, value will remain trapped. 

📹 Watch the video above to learn more about the Integrated Net Present Value Framework (iNPV).

📖 And read our report for a deep dive on how iNPV can unlock value in the food and beverage industry: https://ow.ly/lXRV50XtOyp
tag1: [1] 13. Empower Leaders To Fast-Track Clean Energy Transition

text:
Gas utilities in the United States are spending billions each year to replace aging service lines. But there may be a better way. 

RMI’s latest analysis introduces a framework for service line non-pipeline alternatives (NPAs) — programs that could give customers the option to electrify their homes instead of automatically receiving a new gas line. With utilities spending an estimated $7 billion annually on service line replacements, this approach could help reduce costs for ratepayers, cut emissions, and support state goals for affordability and climate progress. 

Our new brief explores how regulators, utilities, and policymakers can design scalable programs that benefit everyone, and speed the shift to a cleaner, more electrified energy system. 

Learn more 👉 https://ow.ly/64lr50XoMr0

tag_name: [1] 12. Reduce Methane Leakage In India, China, Us
text:
From cutting methane emissions to electrifying transport and building clean industrial hubs, RMI has accomplished so much in the past year. 

But as the world faces record-breaking weather and rising energy demand, one thing is clear: we need a power shift. RMI is proving that clean, affordable, and resilient solutions are within reach. 

Hear from our CEO, Jon Creyts, in a message to our partners in Applied Hope — and get a glimpse inside our FY25 Annual Report 👇 
https://ow.ly/JC8A50XmSw3

text:
Every year, #TrellisImpact unites corporate leaders, startups, investors, and NGOs to figure out how to deploy climate tech at scale to create a profitable, net-zero economy. 

We hope you’ll join RMI and Third Derivative at Trellis Impact this year in San Jose, CA, from October 28–30 to exchange insights on carbon accounting, clean energy, industrial decarbonization, next-gen transport, and the innovation pipelines making it all possible. 

Join us and 3,500+ other professionals, 500+ speakers, and 80+ sessions for 3 days of climate tech momentum. 

🌟 Register at the link in our bio and use code TI25RMI for 20% off!

Net-zero aligned targets are now embedded across the financial system, but progress is hard to see. As climate goals have gained board-level oversight and attention from shareholders and clients, their challenges are mounting — technical, economic, legal, and reputational. Meanwhile, emissions keep rising. 

If the point of commitments and targets was always to see finance flow to the transition, then shouldn’t we ask: What is stopping the transition-enabling transactions from happening? 

In our latest report we offer a hopeful and pragmatic path forward: one where banks are empowered to do what they do best — make deals that move capital toward the transition. 

We believe the following four shifts are essential: 

1️⃣ Recognize banks as commercial actors: Banks have a business model to adhere to, but that doesn’t mean they don’t have a role to play. Understanding that climate-smart investing is just smart investing is only the start. 

2️⃣ Let them lead through what they know: Structuring, advising, deploying capital. That’s how banks can accelerate the transition, not just measure it. 

3️⃣ Build smarter transition intelligence: Metrics that rely on high-level or aggregated data on companies and sectors have not always led to helpful insights, incentives, or market signals. Focusing on the insights that drive financial decisions are key. 

4️⃣ Design systems that work: Climate action is collaborative. Policy, finance, and real-economy actors must work together to remove roadblocks and reward progress. 

By working with, not against, the realities of the financial system, we can unlock the next wave of climate progress. 

📘 Explore the report at the link in our bio to learn more.

Latin America is at the center of a global shift to clean energy and climate action. From Uruguay’s switch to nearly 100% renewable electricity to Brazil’s hosting of #COP30 this November, as well as region-wide progress in energy efficiency and sustainable finance, Latin America is a powerful player in clean energy and climate initiatives. 

Across a region home to nearly 700 million people, RMI is committed to supporting regionally driven and equitable action on clean energy and climate. Our work includes: 

🔅 Advancing Brazil’s green iron opportunity, showing how the country can lead the world in low-carbon industrial innovation. 
🔅 Partnering with investors and regulators in the region to align financial portfolios with climate goals and unlock new opportunities. 
🔅 Publishing climate and finance research in Spanish and Portuguese, to expand access and ensure that regional voices are at the center of the transition. 

This Hispanic/Latine Heritage Month, RMI celebrates the strength of collaboration across cultures and languages. We reaffirm our commitment to collaborating closely with Latin American leaders on innovative solutions. 

🔗 Explore our Latin America research and insights through the link in our bio.

Each step of the oil and gas production process — from extraction, processing, and transport — needs energy. This means that emissions intensity – the level of emissions per barrel of oil or gas — can vary wildly depending on those processes. We also can’t forget super-heating methane, which can leak at any one (or all) of these steps. 

This difference in intensity can have profound impacts on energy security and public safety, but has largely been hidden from markets, regulators, and citizens for decades. 

RMI’s free interactive Oil Climate Index plus Gas (OCI+) tool reveals life-cycle emissions, highlights areas to prevent energy waste, and shares strategies so that industry, policymakers, investors, and researchers can take action to tackle methane leaks. The latest update of OCI+ also includes satellite observation data from @carbonmapper’s Tanager-1 and other point source satellites to combine detected methane measurements with modeled estimates. 

OCI+ underscores the critical role of targeted, resource-specific strategies to reduce energy waste, cut oil and gas emissions, and make the benefits of reducing emissions more transparent for decision-makers. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how we can take methane waste from invisible to securable. 

#MethaneMonday

Do you know what type of refrigerant your HVAC system uses? Do you know how much is in it? 

Managing refrigerant charge is an important aspect of keeping ACs and heat pumps running efficiently. Even small leaks or undercharging can lower performance, driving up bills, straining the grid, and worsening climate pollution. 

The good news is that it's fixable. Read our new article at the link in our bio to learn  how!

The western electricity grid is powerful because no state stands alone — utilities continuously adjust supply and demand to route power to where it can serve customers most reliably and affordably. 

As power needs grow due to increased manufacturing, new data centers, and more, regional transmission can help balance dynamic demand on a daily basis. During severe weather, unexpected plant outages, or sudden demand surges, regional transmission can be the difference between the lights staying on and a widespread blackout. 

Investing in regional transmission planning and expansion can help every Western state secure lasting economic growth, affordable electricity, and long-term reliability.

Click the link in our bio to learn more.

This year’s Climate Week NYC drew record participation with nearly 1,000 events and 100,000+ attendees, despite a rollback in US clean energy support. This year, there was less discussion on policy-led decarbonization and a greater focus on the energy transition by the private sector. 

At the top of the corporate docket: effectively meeting rising costs for power, dealing with the rise in power demand from data centers, and how to lift US competitiveness in clean industry. 

RMIers led conversations on removing market barriers, such as establishing better carbon accounting standards, as well as technical steps to lower emissions from shipping, aviation, and other industries. 

💡 Visit the link in our bio to read what they took away from the whirlwind week!

“Renewables get all the headlines because they’re visible,” says RMI cofounder Amory Lovins. “But energy is invisible, and the energy you don’t use is almost unimaginable.” 

By rethinking how our buildings, vehicles, factories, and data centers are put together, Amory explains, whole-system “integrative design” can boost energy efficiency fivefold while cutting costs and equipment needs. 

From passive homes to lightweight electric cars and retrofitted skyscrapers, proven solutions already show how small design shifts can yield massive savings. The challenge now is making integrative design literacy mainstream. 

Celebrate #EEDay2025 by reading the key takeaways from Amory's recent Stanford talk on integrative design at the link in our bio ⚡

✈️ Air travel is one of the fastest-growing sources of climate pollution — and making it cleaner is one of the toughest challenges we face. 

New fuels and propulsion technologies like sustainable aviation fuel, hydrogen, and electric aircraft are promising — but without serious planning and infrastructure investment at airports, they won’t scale fast enough. 

RMI, in partnership with POWDR and the #PlayForever Foundation, hosted workshops in Utah and Colorado to help unlock this path forward — identifying what airports need to do now to prepare for this transition, and where coordinated action across the aviation ecosystem can accelerate momentum. 

If you work in aviation, energy, or infrastructure: this is the time to be the early-mover! Cleaner air travel is coming, and the planning starts now. 

🔗 More info at the link in our bio!

Robert Fortunato's “Green Idea House” — a remodeled 1950s home in California that generates more energy than it uses — exemplifies how thoughtful design and energy-efficient technology can cut costs and pollution. RMI’s Chris Magwood weighs in on what it takes to build more efficient and sustainable homes and why anyone tackling a renovation should have a firm plan with clear goals to accomplish. 

Read the full story by NPR at the link in our bio.

Clean energy can deliver economic growth, make companies more cost-competitive, and cut consumers’ risks of surprise spikes in energy prices. Motivated by these and other benefits of advanced energy systems, communities and states across America, as well as  most countries globally, are speeding the shift. 

US renewable power output has risen sharply since 2000, driven by a combination of falling costs and supportive policies. While related federal incentives have fluctuated, state-level policies have remained steadier. Early embrace of renewables by community leaders, businesses, and elected leaders, combined with states’ economic development efforts, have laid a durable foundation. 

And as homeowners, cities, and countries reckon with rising costs due to damage amplified by climate change — such as homes lost to wildfire, flash floods from unprecedented deluges, and crops lost to drought — cutting emissions remains a top priority, along with improving energy accessibility, reliability, and affordability. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about the market dynamics, politics, and policy driving the shift to clean energy.

Businesses trying to reduce their scope 3 emissions need to buy cleaner fuels and products such as steel, cement, aluminum, plastics, and fertilizers. But production of these clean inputs is only just getting started, meaning supply is limited and buying options are scarce. 

Corporate buyers and procurement teams hold the key to changing that. By showing a willingness to pay for cleaner materials and signing longer-term contracts, companies can give suppliers the confidence and financing they need to scale up production. 

Our new report, launched today at #GreenMarketsDay25 at #ClimateWeekNYC, breaks down the tools companies can use to accelerate this transition and illustrates how voluntary buyer action has helped scale other solutions such as CDR technology and sustainable aviation fuel. 

Here’s what RMI’s Josh Henretig had to say: 

"Acting early isn’t just about cutting emissions, it’s a strategic investment. First movers strengthen their brands, secure scarce supply, and help shape the future markets." 

For more, read our report, Catalytic Procurement: How Corporations Can Lead the Green Commodity Transition, at the link in our bio.

Improving efficiency reduces waste, costs, and pollution while still allowing us to meet our needs. 

Delivering fully on the promise of energy efficiency means creating not just better products, buildings, and factories, but upgrading broader systems such as cities, industrial hubs, and global supply chains. Part of that shift will include upgrading from fossil fuel technologies to advanced energy and electrification solutions, which are often two to four times more efficient. 

Coupled with a rapid transition to advanced energy, this holistic approach — evaluating not just a single system but how multiple systems can work better in harmony — can make the transition faster, more affordable, and more widely beneficial. Link in bio for more.

Construction on the Empire State Building, one of the world’s first supertall buildings, began in 1930. Now, almost a century later, the Art Deco icon is setting the standard for large building upgrades. 

Working with RMI, @ESRTsocial was able to reduce the @empirestatebldg’s energy consumption by more than half, and save $17 million, thanks to deep retrofits that optimized the building as a system. As part of the investment in the late 2000s, over 6,500 windows were remanufactured. Optimized to insulate better, and to let in light but reflect heat, the new windows helped to cut the building’s heating needs in the winter and cooling load in the summer. 

Click the link in our bio to learn more about how cities  and states can use efficiency upgrades to reduce energy waste and pollution from buildings. #ClimateWeekNYC

Cement and concrete are responsible for ~8 percent of global carbon emissions, yet low-carbon alternatives remain scarce and not available at scale. Why? It’s a classic chicken-and-egg problem: buyers can’t access clean cement, and producers won’t invest in it without clear demand signals. 

Today, RMI and the Center for Green Market Activation (GMA) are launching the Sustainable Concrete Buyers Alliance (or SCoBA) to help break this cycle. 

In the words of our CEO Jon Creyts, “By aggregating demand from major buyers and establishing rigorous standards, we're creating the market conditions for breakthrough low-carbon cement innovations to scale.” 

This approach builds on the success of RMI and GMA’s buyer alliances in aviation, steel, and trucking — proving that collective demand can unlock clean solutions in even the hardest-to-abate sectors. 

✨ Read the press release at the link in our bio to learn more.

RMI is joining other organizations, business leaders, and decision makers at #ClimateWeekNYC to highlight the market-based solutions and strategies that drive the world’s shift to more efficient, prosperous, and secure clean energy options, both locally and globally. 

To support the ongoing transformation of the energy system, we’re focusing our conversations around: 

💵 Efficiency investments that can help companies cut costs, win market share, boost profits, and reduce vulnerability to volatile energy and material prices.

💪 How corporations that stick with their clean energy commitments will reap the benefits of cost savings, reduced waste, and improved reliability and resilience. 

📈 The opportunities for countries and companies to use fast-growing energy technology markets to maintain a competitive edge, enhance energy security, and grow both profits and prosperity.

🔋 Batteries are the workhorse of an affordable, reliable electricity grid. They can pump power into the grid when it needs it most, balance it when there are huge swings in demand, and help jumpstart the system if it falters. 

Texas has helped monetize the services batteries provide to systems, which has grown the market and made it competitive, lowering costs for everyone. These huge battery additions have saved customers millions and helped avoid serious reliability issues in extreme weather. 

Batteries can also store energy when costs are low and make it available when demand is high. This is happening in Texas and in California, where CAISO has shifted to hourly assessments to determine if there is enough generation and resources to meet the electricity demand. This has increased competitiveness and incentivized a surge of flexible and longer-duration battery storage deployment, and it has increased competitiveness and incentivized a surge of flexible and longer-duration battery storage deployment. 

Click the link in our bio to learn how US grid planners can take action to capitalize on the reliability and affordability benefits of batteries.
